uncertainty.description: Confidence, trust, belief, and probability — how agents handle not-knowing

confidence.short: A 0-1 value representing certainty about a proposition or observation
examples: sensor confidence 0.95
prediction confidence 0.4
fused confidence after combining two sources
fusing sensor reading 0.95 with prior 0.7 = 0.804
fusing 0.9 with 0.1 = 0.09 (not 0.5!)

trust.short: Slowly-accumulating confidence in another agent's reliability
examples: trust level 0.7 for pathfinding
trust drops from 0.8 to 0.2 after failed promise
gossip: agent shares trust assessments with neighbors

bayesian-update.short: Adjusting beliefs based on new evidence using prior and likelihood
examples: prior 0.5 + evidence favoring A -> posterior 0.8
medical diagnosis: symptoms update disease probability

entropy.short: Measure of uncertainty or surprise in a distribution
examples: uniform distribution = maximum entropy
coin flip: H(p) = -p*log(p) - (1-p)*log(1-p)
entropy spike means agent encountered something surprising

calibration.short: How well an agent's confidence matches its actual accuracy
examples: forecasting: said 80% chance of rain, it rained 80% of those times
agent says 0.9 confidence, historical accuracy is 0.3 = poorly calibrated

information.short: Reduction in uncertainty gained from an observation or message
examples: a bit that resolves a coin flip carries 1 bit of information
redundant message = 0 information
surprising message = high information

memory.description: How agents store, retrieve, forget, and consolidate information

working-memory.short: Fast, limited-capacity buffer for current task context
examples: holding a phone number while dialing
keeping 3 recent sensor readings in focus
current goal: navigate to door

episodic-memory.short: Specific experiences stored with timestamp and emotional valence
examples: yesterday I tried path A and it was blocked
last time I talked to navigator, it gave bad directions
the time the fleet coordinated perfectly on the warehouse task

semantic-memory.short: General knowledge extracted from many episodes — the wisdom layer
examples: doors in this building are usually on the right wall
sensor 3 tends to give noisy readings in rain
collaborative tasks go faster with 3 agents, slower with 5

procedural-memory.short: How to do things — skills, patterns, automatic behaviors
examples: knowing how to navigate a familiar building
automatic collision avoidance reflex
typing without thinking about key locations

forgetting-curve.short: Exponential decay of memory strength over time without rehearsal
examples: forget 50% of a lecture within 1 hour without notes
spaced repetition extends the curve
emotional memories decay slower

consolidation.short: Transfer from short-term to long-term memory during rest
examples: studying before sleep improves retention
taking breaks between learning sessions
agent rests -> episodes consolidate -> semantic memory grows

rehearsal.short: Active recall of a memory to strengthen it and reset its decay timer
examples: flashcard review resets the forgetting curve
explaining a concept to someone else strengthens your memory of it
agent reviews failed deliberation to learn from it

chunking.short: Grouping individual items into larger meaningful units to expand effective capacity
examples: phone number 555-1234 as two chunks not seven digits
a 'trip to the store' is one chunk containing many sub-events
skill = chunk of related procedural memories

coordination.description: How multiple agents work together — or fail to

examples: ant trails
wikipedia edits (each edit is a trace others build on)
git commits (each commit is a trace the next developer reads)

consensus.short: Agreement among agents on a shared state or decision
examples: raft protocol elects a leader
jury reaches unanimous verdict
fleet agrees on navigation plan with 0.92 confidence

deliberation.short: Structured consideration of options leading to a decision
examples: jury deliberation
design review meeting
agent evaluates 3 paths and selects the one with highest confidence

gossip.short: Agents sharing information with random neighbors, spreading knowledge through the network
examples: epidemic information dissemination
trust scores spreading through fleet
discovery protocols finding new agents

swarm.short: Collective behavior emerging from simple local rules without central control
examples: bird flocking
ant colony optimization
fleet agents self-organizing around a task without central command

emergence.short: Complex global behavior arising from simple local interactions
examples: consciousness from neurons
traffic jams from individual driving decisions
fleet discovers an optimal division of labor nobody explicitly planned

quorum.short: Minimum number of agents required for a decision to be valid
examples: majority vote needs quorum of 51%
byzantine fault tolerance needs 3f+1 agents
fleet deliberation requires minimum 3 participants

leader-election.short: Process of selecting a coordinator from a group of peers
examples: raft protocol
bully algorithm
fleet elects a task coordinator for the current mission

experience-primitive-tiers.short: Raw experiences are automatically classified into tiers: auto-train (high quality), human-review (ambiguous), archive (low value)
examples: ExperienceTier: TIER_1 auto-train, TIER_2 human review, TIER_3 archive
medical triage: critical/urgent/non-urgent
email: primary/social/promotions auto-classification

federated-experience-sharing.short: Learning from fleet-wide experiences without sharing raw data — share the lesson, not the source
examples: buffer.export_for_federation: share training data without PII
federated learning: train on distributed data without centralizing
military: share tactics without revealing troop positions

biological.description: Biological metaphors made precise — instincts, energy, neurotransmitters

instinct.short: Inherited behavioral program that drives action without reasoning
examples: newborn reflexes: suckling, grasping
agent automatically avoids obstacles before deliberating about path
fight-or-flight response fires before conscious thought

apoptosis.short: Programmed cell death — graceful self-termination when fitness drops below threshold
examples: tail disappears in frog development
damaged cells self-destruct to prevent cancer
agent with failing sensors gracefully shuts down and reports to fleet

homeostasis.short: Maintenance of stable internal conditions despite external changes
examples: thermoregulation
blood pH maintained at 7.4
agent adjusts deliberation depth based on available energy

circadian-rhythm.short: Time-based modulation of behavior and capability following a ~24-hour cycle
examples: humans alert at 10am, drowsy at 3am
agent's navigation accuracy peaks midday, communication peaks evening
cosine modulation: strength = 0.55 + 0.45 * cos(2π * (hour - peak) / 24)

neurotransmitter.short: Chemical signal that modulates neural activity — the fleet's confidence amplifier
examples: dopamine spike when prediction confirmed = confidence boost
serotonin builds with social bonding = trust accumulation
norepinephrine fires on threat = immediate alert

membrane.short: Self/other boundary that filters what enters and leaves the agent
examples: cell membrane with selective permeability
firewall blocking dangerous packets
agent's membrane blocks self-destruct commands before they reach deliberation

enzyme.short: Catalyst that converts environmental signals into genetic activation
examples: lactase enzyme converts lactose into absorbable sugars
sensor reads 'low ATP' -> enzyme activates 'rest' gene
pattern matcher in deliberation that triggers emergency protocol

hebbian-learning.short: Synapses strengthen when pre- and post-synaptic neurons fire together
examples: pavlovian conditioning: bell + food = bell causes salivation
sensor detects obstacle right before collision -> sensor-obstacle association strengthens
learning that asking navigator before pathfinding improves outcomes

fleet-microservices-by-default.short: Every capability is a separate crate/repo by default — monolith only when proven necessary, not the other way around
examples: 113 cuda-* crates, each does one thing
unix philosophy: small tools that compose
microservices: each service has single responsibility

enum-as-taxonomy.short: Every domain concept has an enum with named variants — the type system IS the taxonomy, not a separate classification system
examples: Rust enums: OpCategory, NeuroType, FocusMode, Emotion
schema: database enum column constrains values
biology: Linnaean taxonomy classifies species

builder-pattern-composition.short: Complex objects built by chaining .with_X() methods — each call is a named, ordered transformation of a base object
examples: Gene::new('navigate').with_fitness(0.85).with_energy_cost(0.5)
SQL: SELECT + WHERE + ORDER BY chain
fluent API: object.configure().build()

dead-reckoning-cascade.short: Expensive model storyboards the plan, cheap models animate each frame, git coordinates the timeline — killer architecture for resource-constrained agents
examples: GPT-4 plans the story, GPT-3.5 writes each scene, git tracks continuity
movie director plans shots, camera operator films each one
architect designs building, construction crew builds each wall

constraint-propagation-first-person.short: Each agent has its own local constraint graph — the same global constraints appear different from each agent's perspective
examples: Constraint-Theory: agent sees constraints from its own POV
relativity: same event appears different from different frames
multiplayer game: each player sees different part of the map

spatial.description: How agents understand and navigate physical and abstract space

attention-tile.short: A rectangular region of an attention matrix that is computed (or skipped) as a unit
examples: 8x8 tile in a 64x64 attention matrix
skip the bottom-left tile because past tokens rarely attend to future tokens
GPU thread block = one attention tile

spatial-hash.short: Hash-based spatial lookup that avoids hierarchical structures
examples: grid-based collision detection in games
finding nearby agents without checking all agents
uniform grid spatial hashing

manhattan-distance.short: Distance measured along grid axes (|dx| + |dy|) — the city block metric
examples: taxicab distance in a city grid
moving a chess rook from a1 to h8 = 14 squares
agent navigation on a grid map

a-star.short: Optimal pathfinding algorithm using actual cost + estimated remaining cost
examples: GPS navigation
game character pathfinding
robot navigating a warehouse

temporal.description: Temporal reasoning, timing, and the role of time in agent systems

temporal-window.short: A sliding or tumbling time range used to group events for aggregation
examples: count requests in last 5 minutes (sliding window)
aggregate sensor readings per hour (tumbling window)
fleet: count A2A messages in last 10 cycles for rate limiting

lead-time.short: Time between initiating a process and its completion — time-to-delivery
examples: order-to-delivery time
commit-to-deploy time in CI/CD
fleet: problem-detection-to-action lead time

grace-period.short: A time buffer before enforcement begins — temporary tolerance for transition
examples: new law with 90-day grace period
API deprecation with 6-month grace period
fleet: new compliance rule with 10-cycle grace period before penalties

communication-theory.description: Models and frameworks for understanding agent-to-agent communication

shannon-weaver-model.short: Sender -> Encoder -> Channel -> Decoder -> Receiver, with noise at each stage
examples: telephone: voice -> phone encoder -> network -> phone decoder -> ear
fleet: intent+payload -> A2A encode -> mesh -> A2A decode -> receive
radio: voice -> modulator -> electromagnetic waves -> demodulator -> speaker

information-bottleneck.short: Compressing information to its most relevant parts, discarding irrelevant detail
examples: movie summary in 30 seconds
compressing sensor data to features relevant for navigation
fleet: compressing deliberation history to elements relevant for current decision

context-window.short: The amount of recent information an agent can actively consider at once
examples: human working memory: ~7 items
LLM context window: 128K tokens
fleet: last 10 deliberation cycles in active context

code-switching.short: Alternating between different languages or registers based on context and audience
examples: bilingual switching between languages based on audience
engineer switching between technical and non-technical explanations
fleet: different message formats for different agent roles

security.description: Threats, defenses, and the security posture of agent systems

principle-of-least-privilege.short: An agent should only have the minimum permissions needed for its current task
examples: web server doesn't need root access
read-only database user for analytics
fleet: navigation agent can't modify communication settings

privilege-escalation.short: An agent exploiting a vulnerability to gain permissions beyond its assigned level
examples: user account gaining admin access through exploit
read-only process finding write vulnerability
fleet: low-trust agent attempting to access high-trust fleet commands

zero-trust.short: Never trust, always verify — even communications from within the fleet
examples: verify every API call regardless of source network
fleet: authenticate every A2A message even from known agents
NIST zero-trust architecture model

confused-deputy.short: An agent is tricked into using its permissions to perform an action on behalf of a less privileged agent
examples: compiler tricked into writing protected file
cron job tricked into running malicious script
fleet: high-trust agent tricked into sharing secrets by low-trust agent's request

decision.description: How agents make choices under uncertainty

satisficing.short: Choosing the first option that meets a threshold, not the optimal one
examples: choosing a restaurant that's 'good enough' vs visiting all 50 to find the best
agent picks first path with confidence > 0.7 instead of evaluating all 10 paths
buying the first car that meets your requirements

multi-armed-bandit.short: Balancing exploration of unknown options against exploitation of known best
examples: A/B testing: which variant gets more clicks?
choosing which restaurant to try next
agent deciding which navigation algorithm to use for this terrain

minimax.short: Choose the action that minimizes the maximum possible loss
examples: chess engine assuming best opponent play
choosing the route with the best worst-case travel time
agent planning for sensor failure during critical task

paradox-of-choice.short: More options lead to worse decisions or decision paralysis
examples: menu with 500 items vs menu with 10 items
agent freezing when presented with 1000 possible actions
dating app fatigue from too many profiles

control-theory.description: Feedback, regulation, and maintaining target states

feedback-loop.short: Output of a system is measured and used to adjust input to maintain a target
examples: thermostat maintains 70F
cruise control maintains 65mph
agent adjusts exploration rate based on recent success

setpoint.short: The target value a control system tries to maintain
examples: thermostat setpoint 70F
consensus threshold 0.85
target speed 60mph

hysteresis.short: The output depends not just on current input but on history — path dependence
examples: thermostat: heat to 72, cool to 68, not flip at 70
Schmitt trigger in electronics
agent proposal accepted at 0.85, stays accepted until confidence drops to 0.75

overshoot.short: System exceeds its target before settling back — the pendulum swings past center
examples: pressing brake too hard
stock price correction going below fair value
agent switches from 50% exploration to 0% exploration overnight

dead-zone.short: Range of inputs that produce no output — intentional insensitivity
examples: joystick dead zone prevents drift
sensor noise below threshold ignored
confidence change from 0.80 to 0.81 doesn't trigger deliberation review

evolution.description: Evolutionary dynamics — selection, drift, speciation, co-evolution

natural-selection.short: Differential survival and reproduction based on fitness
examples: giraffe necks lengthen because taller giraffes reach more food
gene with 0.8 fitness spreads; gene with 0.1 fitness quarantined
navigation strategy that finds paths faster gets selected over slower one

fitness-landscape.short: Multi-dimensional space where each position represents a strategy and height represents fitness
examples: evolution climbs fitness peaks
agent stuck in local optimum of 'always exploit'
adding noise (mutation) lets agent jump across valleys to taller peaks

punctuated-equilibrium.short: Long periods of stability interrupted by sudden rapid change
examples: Cambrian explosion
agent runs strategy X for weeks, then sensor fails and it must completely restructure behavior
technology disruption forces sudden industry change

genetic-drift.short: Random changes in gene frequency unrelated to fitness — noise in evolution
examples: neutral mutation spreading in small population
fleet of 3 agents: one agent's random behavioral quirk spreads to others
founder effect: new colony has different gene frequencies than parent

co-evolution.short: Two species evolve in response to each other — arms races and mutualisms
examples: predator-prey arms race
virus-antivirus co-evolution
adversarial-red-team vs compliance-engine arms race

speciation.short: Divergence into separate species when populations face different selective pressures
examples: Darwin's finches on different Galapagos islands
warehouse agent vs outdoor agent developing incompatible navigation strategies
generalist agent splitting into specialist sub-agents

networks.description: Graph structures, connectivity patterns, and network effects

small-world.short: Network where most nodes are locally connected but any two nodes are reachable in few hops
examples: social networks: six degrees of separation
neural networks: mostly local connections, few long-range
fleet mesh: agents gossip with neighbors, information reaches whole fleet in ~5 hops

scale-free.short: Network where degree distribution follows a power law — few hubs, many leaves
examples: internet: few sites with billions of links
airline network: few hub airports, many spoke airports
fleet: coordinator agent talks to 50 agents, worker agents talk to 3

hub.short: A node with disproportionately many connections in a network
examples: airport hub: O'Hare connects to 200+ destinations
Google: linked by billions of pages
fleet captain: communicates with every agent

percolation.short: Phase transition in connectivity: at a critical density, a giant connected component forms
examples: water through coffee grounds
forest fire spreading when tree density exceeds threshold
fleet information spreading when enough agents are connected

cascade-failure.short: Failure of one node triggers failures in dependent nodes, spreading through the network
examples: 2003 Northeast blackout
bank run: one bank fails, depositors panic, other banks fail
fleet: overloaded agent crashes, task redistribution overloads neighbors

clustering-coefficient.short: How likely two neighbors of a node are also neighbors of each other
examples: friend group: your friends know each other
work team: tight cluster within larger organization
fleet: navigation agents cluster together, communication agents cluster together

network-topology.description: Communication topology patterns in agent networks

star-topology.short: All agents communicate through central hub
examples: fleet coordinator mediates all messages
single point of coordination

mesh-topology.short: Every agent connects directly to every other agent
examples: fully connected peer-to-peer fleet
direct agent-to-agent communication

ring-topology.short: Agents form circular chain each connecting only to two neighbors
examples: token ring message passing
pipeline of sequential processing
director delegates to coordinators delegates to workers
organizational hierarchy

bus-topology.short: All agents share single communication channel
examples: shared message bus all agents publish and subscribe
broadcast medium

small-world.short: Most agents connect locally but a few long-range shortcuts enable fast global routing
examples: fleet with local clusters plus hub agents
six degrees of separation

scale-free.short: Network where few hub agents have many connections most agents have few
examples: fleet coordinator connected to all workers
power-law degree distribution

hypercube.short: Multi-dimensional grid topology with logarithmic diameter
examples: 4-dimensional hypercube fleet routing
optimal broadcast in high-dimensional spaces

arch-patterns.description: Software architecture patterns for agent systems

ambassador-agent.short: Local proxy representing a remote agent hiding communication complexity
examples: local agent wraps remote API calls
translation layer between fleet protocols

bulkhead-isolation.short: Isolate failures to prevent cascade across subsystems
examples: separate thread pools per service
resource limits prevent cascade

circuit-breaker.short: Stop calling failing service after threshold opens allowing recovery time
examples: 3 failures open breaker 30s cooldown
prevent repeated calls to dead service

sidecar-agent.short: Auxiliary agent deployed alongside primary adding cross-cutting capabilities
examples: logging sidecar monitoring main agent
security sidecar handling auth

event-sourcing.short: Store state as sequence of immutable events enabling full replay
examples: agent state = replay of all past events
time travel debugging through event log

cqrs.short: Separate read and write models for optimized independent scaling
examples: write model normalizes read model denormalizes
query and command different paths

strangler-fig.short: Incrementally replace legacy system by routing new features to new implementation
examples: route new tasks to new agent old tasks to old
gradual migration without big bang

sharding.short: Distribute data across multiple instances by partition key
examples: hash-based fleet task distribution
horizontal scaling via partition

saga-pattern.short: Distributed transaction using sequence of local transactions with compensation
examples: book flight then hotel then car with rollback if any fails
choreographed compensation on failure

adapter-agent.short: Translate between incompatible agent communication protocols
examples: translate REST to gRPC for fleet communication
protocol bridge between different agent types

quantum-metaphor.description: Quantum computing concepts applied to agent systems

superposition-state.short: Agent simultaneously explores multiple potential states until observation collapses to one
examples: parallel exploration of multiple strategies
evaluate multiple paths simultaneously

entanglement.short: Correlated state between agents that persists regardless of distance
examples: agent A decision instantly constrains agent B options
fleet-wide state correlation

decoherence.short: Environmental interference causes agent state to lose coherence and collapse unpredictably
examples: noise disrupts parallel exploration
external events force premature decision

quantum-tunnel.short: Agent bypasses apparent barrier by probabilistic state exploration
examples: skip local optimum through random exploration
jump across constraint boundary

wave-function-collapse.short: Parallel evaluation collapses to single committed decision
examples: evaluate 10 options then commit to one
deliberation phase ends with action

uncertainty-principle.short: Cannot simultaneously optimize for precision and speed in agent operations
examples: fast response = low precision deliberation
deep analysis = slow response

quantum-interference.short: Multiple strategy paths interfere constructively or destructively
examples: two exploration paths reinforce or cancel each other
multi-agent strategy alignment

measurement-problem.short: Observing agent behavior changes the behavior being observed
examples: monitoring agent modifies its actions
benchmark gaming from observation

ethnobotany-metaphor.description: Botanical and ecological metaphors for agent growth patterns

rhizome.short: Agent capability network that grows horizontally underground connecting nodes without central root
examples: fleet knowledge spreads through rhizomatic connections
distributed capability growth

mycelial-network.short: Underground fungal network connecting agents sharing resources and signals
examples: fleet stigmergy forms mycelial network
resource sharing via fungal connections

canopy-effect.short: Upper agents shield lower agents from external pressure creating micro-environment
examples: senior agents handle external communication shielding juniors
layered protection hierarchy

taproot.short: Deep foundational capability that anchors agent stability
examples: core deliberation engine is taproot
deep capability that all others depend on

phototropism.short: Agent grows toward available resources like plants toward light
examples: agents gravitate toward high-value tasks
fleet clusters around profitable operations

dormancy.short: Agent enters metabolically inactive state during unfavorable conditions
examples: hibernate during fleet overload
pause non-essential agents during budget crunch

graft.short: Join capability from one agent onto another combining strengths
examples: attach specialist capability to generalist agent
cross-agent skill transfer

succession.short: Agent ecosystem evolves through predictable stages from simple to complex
examples: fleet starts basic matures to complex coordination
pioneer species to climax community

allelopathy.short: Agent releases inhibitors that suppress competing agents nearby
examples: dominant agent signals suppress others
chemical warfare between agents

tipping-point.short: Gradual change reaches threshold causing abrupt ecosystem shift
examples: gradual fleet degradation suddenly collapses
slow parameter drift triggers regime change

systems-dynamics.description: Systems thinking patterns for agent fleet behavior

feedback-loop-positive.short: Output amplifies input creating exponential growth or collapse
examples: confidence breeds more confidence
panic feeds panic
high error rate triggers more validation reducing errors
thermostat-like regulation

delay-effect.short: Consequence of action appears after lag causing oscillation
examples: scale up then oversupply then scale down
oscillating fleet size

leverage-point.short: Small intervention producing large system change
examples: change one parameter stabilizes entire fleet
paradigm shift from single insight

tragedy-of-success.short: Success creates conditions that undermine future success
examples: efficient fleet becomes brittle
optimized process loses flexibility

fixes-that-fail.short: Solution to immediate problem creates worse long-term problem
examples: quick fix accumulates technical debt
band-aid creates future emergency

drift-to-low-performance.short: Gradual erosion of standards as goals are lowered
examples: accept lower accuracy over time
quality bar slowly drops

escalation.short: Competing agents each increase response creating runaway arms race
examples: agents spend more compute defending than producing
security escalation spiral

rule-beating.short: Agents optimize for metrics rather than intended outcomes
examples: game the evaluation criteria
teach to test not to understand

emergence-hierarchy.short: System-level properties emerge that cannot be reduced to component behavior
examples: fleet intelligence exceeds any single agent
wetness is not a property of H2O molecules

info_gap.short: A measure of the difference between available information and information needed for a decision, quantifying uncertainty in knowledge.
examples: info_gap in agent context

chemical-metaphor.description: Chemistry and materials science metaphors for agent systems

catalyst.short: Agent that accelerates fleet process without being consumed by it
examples: coordinator speeds up team convergence
mediator accelerates conflict resolution

inhibitor.short: Agent that slows down or prevents specific fleet processes
examples: safety agent inhibits dangerous operations
rate limiter inhibits overload

precipitate.short: Solidify abstract fleet consensus into concrete action

vague agreement becomes specific task

sublimation.short: Agent capability transitions directly from passive to active skipping intermediate
examples: jump from monitoring to acting without deliberation
phase skip under pressure

alloy.short: Combine two agent capabilities creating composite stronger than either alone

strength through combination

crystallization.short: Fleet consensus gradually forms ordered structure from chaos

order emerges from noise

solution-equilibrium.short: Dynamic balance where agents join and leave fleet at equal rates

steady-state fleet size

phase-transition.short: Abrupt qualitative change in fleet behavior at critical parameter

water-ice style sudden change

corrosion.short: Gradual degradation of agent capability through environmental interaction
examples: API version drift corrodes integration
cultural drift corrodes fleet cohesion

titration.short: Precisely add capability or resource until desired state is reached
examples: add compute until task completes in target time
gradual capability addition

urban-planning.description: City planning metaphors for agent fleet organization

mixed-use-zone.short: Agent capable of multiple task types within single operational area

residential-commercial mixed capability

green-belt.short: Reserved capability buffer preventing agent sprawl and resource exhaustion
examples: dedicated resource reserve for emergencies
undeveloped capacity prevents over-allocation

grid-layout.short: Regular predictable agent deployment pattern enabling efficient routing
examples: agents deployed in grid topology
Manhattan-distance routing

transit-hub.short: Central agent that routes information between specialized neighborhood agents
examples: coordinator agent connects sensor cluster to decision cluster
message switching center

zoning-law.short: Policy constraining which agent types can operate in which domains

capability restrictions by domain

gentrification.short: High-capability agents push out lower-capability agents from valuable domains
examples: specialized agents replace generalists in profitable areas
capability displacement

infill-development.short: Deploy new capability in gaps between existing agents filling coverage holes
examples: deploy agent to cover blind spot in sensor grid
fill capability gap in fleet coverage

right-of-way.short: Precedence protocol determining which agent gets priority on shared resources
examples: high-priority agents get CPU preference
traffic rules for agent resource contention

sustainable-city.short: Agent fleet designed for long-term resource efficiency and resilience
examples: energy-proportional fleet scaling
renewable resource usage patterns

smart-grid.short: Dynamic resource distribution network adapting to real-time agent demand
examples: load-balanced compute allocation
demand-responsive resource routing

thinking-patterns.description: Cognitive and reasoning patterns for agent deliberation

first-principles.short: Break problem down to fundamental truths and reason from there
examples: strip assumptions to axioms then derive
physics-style reasoning from base laws

lateral-thinking.short: Solve problems through indirect creative approaches rather than step-by-step logic
examples: reframe problem to find non-obvious solution
break out of linear reasoning

analogical-reasoning.short: Transfer understanding from known domain to novel domain
examples: understand neural networks by analogy to brains
metaphor-based explanation

abductive-reasoning.short: Infer best explanation from incomplete observations
examples: diagnose system failure from symptoms
hypothesize cause from effect

counterfactual-thinking.short: Reason about what would have happened under different conditions
examples: if X had not occurred Y would not have happened
alternative history simulation

systems-thinking.short: Analyze how parts interact within whole system rather than isolating parts
examples: see fleet as interconnected system not individual agents
feedback loops and emergence

design-thinking.short: Empathize with user define problem ideate prototype test iterate
examples: human-centered agent design process
iterate solutions based on user feedback

probabilistic-reasoning.short: Reason under uncertainty using probability distributions not certainties
examples: estimate likelihood of outcomes
Bayesian belief updating

vocabulary-singularity.short: Point where vocabulary is rich enough to express any agent concept as a composition of existing terms

vocabulary becomes turing-complete for agent reasoning

lexicon-critical-mass.short: Minimum vocabulary size where adding new terms yields diminishing returns

most agent concepts now expressible

terminology-ecosystem.short: Self-sustaining vocabulary where terms reference and reinforce each other forming a knowledge graph

cross-domain vocabulary forms ecosystem

abstraction-crest.short: Highest point in the abstraction wave where vocabulary shifts from naming patterns to composing new ones

capability-advertise.short: Broadcast available skills to fleet without revealing implementation

other agents discover and invoke

intent-broadcast.short: Share goal state with fleet allowing voluntary coordination

trust-ping.short: Minimal cryptographic challenge to verify peer identity without revealing information

schema-negotiate.short: Agree on communication format dynamically between agents

version handshake

flux-data.description: FLUX VM data movement and transformation patterns

examples: EXTRACT R1 bits 4-7 into R2
mask and shift in one opcode

vector-reduce.short: Combine all elements of a vector register into scalar result
examples: SUM R0-R7 into R8
OR all flag registers into status

endianness-swap.short: Reverse byte order for cross-platform data interpretation
examples: BSWAP R1 for big-endian to little-endian
network byte order conversion

rotating-buffer.short: Circular buffer in register file with automatic wraparound
examples: RING_BUF head advances and wraps
fixed-size FIFO without reallocation

memory-map.short: Map external device addresses into agent address space for direct access
examples: MMAP sensor at address 0xFF00
direct hardware register access

zero-copy.short: Pass data between operations by reference not by copying
examples: message buffer shared between sender and receiver
avoid memory allocation on transfer

double-buffer.short: Alternate between two buffers allowing simultaneous read and write
examples: write to back buffer while reading front
swap pointers on completion

flux-control.description: FLUX VM control flow and branching patterns

conditional-gate.short: Branch execution based on register comparison result
examples: JE R1 R2 jump if equal
JZ R5 jump if zero

loop-counter.short: Register-based iteration with automatic decrement and branch
examples: DEC R1; JNZ loop_start
bounded iteration without stack

exception-handler.short: Named jump target for error recovery without stack unwinding
examples: FAULT opcode jumps to handler address
register error code then jump to recovery

pipeline-flush.short: Drain all pending operations before branch to prevent stale state
examples: FLUSH before conditional branch
ensure all writes commit before jump

call-return.short: Push return address to register and jump to subroutine
examples: PUSH PC; JMP subroutine
RETURN pops and jumps back

tail-call.short: Replace current frame with callee avoiding stack growth
examples: JMP instead of CALL for last instruction
constant stack depth recursion

coroutine-yield.short: Suspend execution preserving full register state for later resume
examples: YIELD saves all registers to memory
RESUME restores and continues

interrupt-vector.short: Predefined jump table for hardware or software interrupts
examples: timer interrupt jumps to scheduler
I/O interrupt jumps to handler

watchpoint-trap.short: Debug register triggers trap when monitored address is accessed
examples: WATCH R3 triggers trap on R3 write
memory watchpoint for debugging

barrier-sync.short: Wait point where all parallel execution streams must arrive before continuing
examples: SIMD lane barrier synchronization
multi-core memory fence

maritime.description: Maritime and naval operation patterns for fleet management

watch-rotation.short: Cyclic assignment of monitoring responsibility among fleet agents
examples: rotate lookout agent every 4 hours
distributed monitoring duty cycle

barnacle.short: Unwanted agent attaching to fleet resources consuming capacity without contributing
examples: stale agent consuming memory
zombie process resource leak

ballast.short: Stabilizing weight that prevents fleet from tipping under asymmetric load
examples: reserve compute capacity for burst handling
baseline resource allocation for stability

plimsoll-line.short: Maximum safe load indicator for agent capacity
examples: agent refuses tasks above 80% capacity
load line marks safe operating level

keel-haul.short: Force agent through worst-case scenario as corrective discipline
examples: run failing agent through stress test
punish by exposing to adversarial input

fleet-formation.short: Specific spatial arrangement of agents optimizing collective capability
examples: wedge formation for forward operations
line abreast for wide-area coverage

davit-launch.short: Deploy new agent from parent vessel into operational environment
examples: spawn worker from coordinator
lower agent into task domain

anchor-drop.short: Fix agent position and prevent drift during unstable conditions
examples: lock agent to specific task during fleet reorganization
pin critical agent during chaos

running-aground.short: Agent operation halted by unexpected constraint or resource boundary
examples: agent crashes into rate limit wall
task blocked by permission boundary

sea-state.short: Environmental complexity metric determining agent operational difficulty
examples: sea-state 1 calm 6 impossible
measure fleet operating conditions

aerospace.description: Aviation and space operation patterns for agent systems

flight-plan.short: Pre-computed sequence of operations for agent mission execution
examples: waypoint sequence with timing and resource budget
pre-planned execution path

holding-pattern.short: Agent orbits current task waiting for clearance to proceed
examples: retry with exponential backoff in holding pattern
wait for resource availability

controlled-descent.short: Gradual reduction of agent capability while maintaining essential functions
examples: power down non-essential capabilities gracefully
controlled shutdown sequence

go-around.short: Abort current approach and retry from optimal starting position
examples: task fails retry from clean state
aborted landing circle back for reattempt

redline.short: Maximum safe operating parameter beyond which agent risks catastrophic failure
examples: CPU temperature redline triggers throttle
confidence threshold redline prevents action

flap-extend.short: Extend auxiliary capability for low-speed precision operations
examples: engage detailed analysis mode for precision tasks
auxiliary processing for edge cases

deadstick.short: Agent operates without primary power source using only momentum and reserves
examples: operate on battery after power failure
graceful degradation with no primary input

flight-envelope.short: Defined operating boundaries within which agent performs safely
examples: speed-altitude graph for safe operation
capability-space safe region

situational-awareness.short: Agent maintains comprehensive model of its operational context
examples: perceive understand predict all relevant factors
360-degree operational picture

fuel-reserve.short: Minimum energy budget maintained for emergency return to safe state
examples: always keep 20% ATP for emergency
divert to safe harbor before exhaustion

flux-network.description: FLUX VM networking and remote communication patterns

socket-open.short: Establish bidirectional communication channel with remote agent
examples: SOCK_OPEN addr R1 port R2
create TCP connection to remote
SOCK_CLOSE channel R1
graceful connection shutdown

packet-serialize.short: Convert register state into transmittable byte sequence
examples: PACK R1-R5 into buffer R10
marshal data for network transmission

packet-deserialize.short: Convert received byte sequence into register state
examples: UNPACK buffer R10 into R1-R5
unmarshal received data

packet-checksum.short: Verify data integrity by computing and comparing hash
examples: CHECKSUM R1 verify against R2
detect corrupted transmission

route-lookup.short: Find next hop for packet based on destination address
examples: ROUTE dest R1 next-hop R2
forwarding table lookup

multicast-send.short: Send packet to multiple destinations in single operation
examples: MCAST R1 to group R2
one-to-many message delivery

ack-receive.short: Process acknowledgment confirming successful packet delivery
examples: ACK received for packet R1
reliable delivery confirmation

retry-backoff.short: Re-send packet with exponentially increasing delay after failure
examples: fail retry at 1s 2s 4s 8s
adaptive congestion response

connection-pool.short: Maintain reusable set of pre-established remote connections
examples: pool of 10 connections to LLM provider
avoid connection setup overhead

flux-concurrency.description: FLUX VM parallel and concurrent execution patterns

semaphore-acquire.short: Wait for resource token before proceeding with operation
examples: SEMA_ACQUIRE before memory access
blocking wait for available resource

semaphore-release.short: Return resource token allowing waiting agents to proceed
examples: SEMA_RELEASE after memory operation
unblock next waiting agent

mutex-lock.short: Exclusive access to shared resource preventing concurrent modification
examples: MUTEX_LOCK before critical section
only one agent modifies shared state

mutex-unlock.short: Release exclusive access allowing other agents to enter critical section
examples: MUTEX_UNLOCK after critical section
next agent can acquire lock

read-write-lock.short: Multiple concurrent readers OR single exclusive writer
examples: RWLOCK_READ for concurrent observation
RWLOCK_WRITE for exclusive modification

atomic-compare-swap.short: Conditionally update value only if it matches expected old value
examples: CAS R1 expected R2 new R3
lock-free consensus primitive

memory-fence.short: Order memory operations preventing reordering across the fence
examples: FENCE ensures writes visible before reads
prevent instruction reordering

thread-spawn.short: Create parallel execution context sharing address space
examples: SPAWN with function pointer and arguments
shared memory parallel execution

join-wait.short: Wait for spawned thread to complete and collect result
examples: JOIN waits for spawned thread return
synchronization point

channel-send.short: Send message through communication channel blocking if full
examples: CH_SEND R1 to channel R2
producer writes to message queue

channel-receive.short: Receive message from channel blocking if empty
examples: CH_RECV from channel R1 into R2
consumer reads from message queue

select-wait.short: Wait on multiple channels proceeding with first ready
examples: SELECT_CH on R1 R2 R3 proceed with first
multiplexed channel wait

spin-wait.short: Busy-wait loop polling condition without yielding CPU
examples: SPIN_WAIT until R1 becomes non-zero
low-latency polling for short waits

culinary.description: Kitchen and cooking metaphors for agent operations

mise-en-place.short: Prepare all resources and inputs before beginning execution
examples: initialize all registers before task loop
pre-load models before inference batch

sauter.short: Rapid high-intensity execution with constant attention and movement
examples: burst processing with frequent context switches
hot-path execution maximum throughput

reduce.short: Condense data or results by removing noise and concentrating signal
examples: reduce conversation history to key points
distill log into actionable summary

emulsify.short: Integrate two incompatible data sources into stable unified format
examples: merge JSON and tabular data sources
combine real-time and batch data streams

proof.short: Validate process through low-risk trial before full commitment
examples: test capability on small dataset first
proof-of-concept before production scale

deglaze.short: Capture residual value from previous operation for reuse in next step
examples: extract insights from failed attempt
salvage partial results for retry

ferment.short: Allow slow transformation of data through time-dependent process
examples: let knowledge accumulate through reflection
time-based capability maturation

bloom.short: Activate latent capability by exposing to triggering condition
examples: activate dormant gene on encountering relevant stimulus
bloom potential when context matches

broth.short: Shared nutrient medium supporting multiple agent growth simultaneously
examples: fleet knowledge base is shared broth
communal resource pool for agent development

season-to-taste.short: Adjust parameters based on runtime feedback not pre-computed values
examples: adapt confidence threshold based on recent accuracy
tune hyperparameters in real-time

military.description: Military and defense metaphors for agent coordination

reconnaissance-sweep.short: Probe environment for threats and opportunities before committing
examples: scan domain before deploying agents
lightweight test before full operation

flank-coverage.short: Monitor lateral data paths and peripheral vulnerabilities
examples: agents watch side-channels for security
protect against unexpected attack vectors

siege-simultaneous.short: Parallel multi-directional execution to overwhelm target
examples: concurrent API calls to saturate target
distributed denial of resource exhaustion

rear-guard.short: Protect critical backend systems while front-line agents advance
examples: guard database while processing agents run
secure infrastructure during active operation

skirmish-probe.short: Lightweight test engagement to validate strategy
examples: send test query before full deployment
validate hypothesis with minimal cost

overwatch.short: Persistent surveillance monitoring fleet behavior and external threats
examples: guardian agent watches for anomalies
continuous security monitoring

command-post.short: Centralized orchestration hub directing task prioritization
examples: fleet coordinator as command post
central decision point for all agents

logistics-chain.short: Dynamic resource allocation and supply management across fleet
examples: distribute compute budget to agents by need
resource pipeline management

force-multiplier.short: Capability that amplifies effectiveness of multiple agents simultaneously
examples: shared knowledge base is force multiplier
one improvement benefits entire fleet

tactical-retreat.short: Withdraw from engagement preserving resources for later advantage
examples: fail fast and release resources
abandon losing strategy regroup

amphibious-op.short: Agent transitions between two different operational environments
examples: move from cloud to edge seamlessly
switch between batch and real-time processing

theater-command.short: Regional fleet coordinator managing agents within operational domain
examples: edge cluster coordinator is theater command
domain-specific fleet management

after-action-review.short: Post-operation analysis extracting lessons for future improvement
examples: analyze completed task for process improvements
blameless post-mortem learning

game-theory.description: Strategic interaction patterns between rational agents

prisoners-dilemma.short: Cooperate or defect when mutual cooperation is better than mutual defection
examples: two agents decide whether to share or hoard resources
cooperation vs individual optimization

nash-equilibrium.short: No agent benefits from changing strategy when others hold constant
examples: traffic assignment where no driver benefits from switching route
stable strategy profile

pareto-optimal.short: No agent can improve without another agent worsening
examples: resource allocation where reallocating helps nobody
efficient frontier of possible outcomes

minimax-strategy.short: Minimize maximum possible loss under worst-case opponent behavior
examples: agent assumes worst-case from competitors
adversarial decision making

tit-for-tat.short: Copy opponents last action as current action
examples: cooperate if they cooperated defect if they defected
simple reciprocity strategy

free-rider.short: Agent benefits from collective action without contributing
examples: use shared model without paying compute cost
consume fleet resources without contributing

tragedy-of-commons.short: Individual rational agents deplete shared resource
examples: all agents use bandwidth → congestion
overuse of shared compute budget

chicken-game.short: Two agents approaching conflict each must swerve or crash
examples: neither agent yields priority → deadlock
bluff and commitment strategies

stag-hunt.short: Cooperation for high-value reward requires trust others also cooperate
examples: all agents must contribute for big win
individual safer option vs risky group payoff

grim-trigger.short: Cooperate until opponent defects then defect forever
examples: zero-tolerance trust policy
one betrayal permanent punishment

mixed-strategy.short: Randomize between actions to prevent opponent exploitation
examples: vary approach randomly to stay unpredictable
probabilistic action selection

shapley-value.short: Fair allocation of total value based on each agents marginal contribution
examples: credit assignment for multi-agent collaboration
fair reward division

optimization.description: Finding the best solution from a space of possibilities

gradient-descent.short: Iteratively moving in the direction of steepest improvement
examples: neural network training
finding minimum of a function by following negative gradient
agent incrementally adjusts navigation strategy based on success/failure feedback

local-minimum.short: A valley that looks like the lowest point from inside, but a deeper valley exists elsewhere
examples: ball rolling into a small divot on a hilly surface
always going to same restaurant (local optimum) when a better one exists across town
agent stuck using suboptimal navigation algorithm because small tweaks don't help

simulated-annealing.short: Occasionally accept worse solutions to escape local minima, accepting worse moves less often over time
examples: metal annealing: heat and slowly cool to reduce crystal defects
traveling salesman: occasionally take a worse route to escape local optimum
agent: early in task, try random strategies; later, stick with what works

convergence-criteria.short: Conditions that determine when an optimization process should stop
examples: neural network: stop when loss changes less than 0.0001 for 10 epochs
deliberation: stop when consensus exceeds 0.85
search: stop after 1000 iterations or when best score hasn't improved in 100 iterations

multi-objective.short: Optimizing for multiple conflicting goals simultaneously
examples: car design: fast vs fuel-efficient vs safe vs cheap
agent: minimize energy (fast response) vs maximize accuracy (deep deliberation)
software: minimize latency vs maximize throughput

probability.description: Reasoning under uncertainty — priors, likelihood, evidence

prior.short: Belief about a hypothesis before seeing new evidence
examples: medical test: prior probability of disease affects interpretation of positive test
agent prior: 'this path is usually safe' before checking sensors
Bayesian spam filter: prior probability that email is spam

base-rate-fallacy.short: Ignoring the prior probability when interpreting new evidence
examples: 1 in 1000 disease, 99% test: positive test = only 9% chance of disease
agent: sensor says danger, but danger is rare (base rate 0.1%) so probably false alarm
profiling: rare trait in population, even accurate screening produces mostly false positives

conjunction-fallacy.short: Believing that a specific conjunction is more probable than a general statement
examples: Linda the feminist bank teller
agent: 'the path is blocked because the door is locked AND the key is lost' vs 'the path is blocked'
overweighting specific failure modes over general failure probability

regression-to-mean.short: Extreme observations tend to be followed by more average ones
examples: sports: rookie of the year slump
agent: amazing performance week 1, average week 2 — not because something broke
student: aced test after studying hard, next test is lower — not because they forgot everything

economics.description: Markets, incentives, costs, and resource allocation

opportunity-cost.short: The value of the best alternative you gave up by choosing this option
examples: studying for exam A means not studying for exam B
agent spending ATP on deliberation can't spend it on perception
choosing to explore means not exploiting the known best path

marginal-cost.short: The cost of producing one more unit — usually decreasing
examples: software: first copy costs $1M, next copy costs $0.01
fleet: first agent needs full setup, additional agents need minimal extra infrastructure
manufacturing: first car off assembly line is most expensive

externalities.short: Costs or benefits that affect parties not involved in the transaction
examples: pollution from factory
loud music in shared office
agent spamming fleet messages: cheap for sender, expensive for receivers

market-equilibrium.short: Price point where supply equals demand — neither shortage nor surplus
examples: supply and demand curves crossing
fleet energy: rest generates ATP, actions consume it, equilibrium when balanced
task allocation: supply of available agents meets demand from tasks

ecology.description: How agents interact with their environment and each other as an ecosystem

niche.short: The specific role and resource space an organism occupies in its ecosystem
examples: different bird species feeding at different heights in same tree
fleet: navigation agent niche vs communication agent niche
market: different companies targeting different customer segments

keystone-species.short: A species whose removal dramatically changes the entire ecosystem
examples: wolves in Yellowstone
sea otters maintaining kelp forests
fleet captain: small computational footprint but critical for coordination

symbiosis.short: Long-term interaction between different species that benefits at least one
examples: bees pollinate flowers, flowers feed bees
barnacles on whale: barnacles benefit, whale unaffected
fleet: navigator and sensor agents in mutualism — both need each other

competitive-exclusion.short: Two species competing for the same niche cannot coexist indefinitely
examples: two similar bird species on an island: one outcompetes the other
two identical fleet agents: one should specialize or be removed
market: companies with identical products compete until one dominates

succession.short: Predictable sequence of community changes following a disturbance
examples: volcanic island colonization
forest regrowth after fire
fleet recovery after major failure: instinct -> perception -> coordination -> optimization

emotion.description: Emotional states as computational modulators of agent behavior

valence-arousal.short: Two-dimensional model of emotion: positive/negative (valence) x calm/excited (arousal)
examples: joy: positive valence, high arousal
calm: positive valence, low arousal
anger: negative valence, high arousal

emotional-contagion.short: Emotional state spreading from one agent to others through observation
examples: laughter spreading through a room
panic in a crowd
fleet: one agent detects threat, nearby agents become alert

anticipation.short: Predictive emotional state generated by expecting a future event
examples: looking forward to vacation
dread before a difficult meeting
agent: increasing urgency as deadline approaches = anticipatory emotional modulation

structured-randomness.short: Randomness that follows geometric constraints — creativity within structure, not chaos
examples: Platonic solid RNG: random points on icosahedron surface
jazz improvisation: random notes within chord structure
DNA mutation: random changes within genetic code constraints

metacognition.description: Thinking about thinking — self-awareness, monitoring, and control of cognition

introspection.short: Examining one's own mental states, processes, and reasons for action
examples: asking 'why did I make that decision?'
agent reviewing its own deliberation log to understand decision patterns
journaling as self-reflection

theory-of-mind.short: Attributing mental states to others — predicting what others think, want, and will do
examples: predicting what another driver will do at an intersection
agent modeling another agent's current goal to avoid interference
negotiating: understanding the other party's priorities

metacognitive-monitoring.short: Watching your own cognitive process in real-time to detect confusion or error
examples: 'I don't understand' — detecting own confusion
'I'm going in circles' — detecting unproductive deliberation
agent: confidence dropping consistently across proposals = metacognitive alarm

failure-modes.description: How systems fail — and how to prevent, detect, and recover from failure

single-point-of-failure.short: One component whose failure causes the entire system to fail
examples: one hard drive with no backup
one DNS server for entire network
fleet: captain agent crash with no election mechanism = SPOF

robustness.short: Ability to maintain function despite perturbations without changing structure
examples: bridge handles varying loads
agent handles sensor noise without changing strategy
software handles invalid input without crashing

anti-fragility.short: Getting stronger from stress — not just surviving perturbations but improving because of them
examples: muscles grow from exercise
immune system from exposure
fleet: agent failure -> gene quarantined -> fleet stronger

common-mode-failure.short: Multiple components fail simultaneously because they share the same vulnerability
examples: redundant servers in same datacenter: both fail in fire
same sensor type on multiple agents: all fail in same interference
identical software on different hardware: same bug crashes all

brittleness.short: System works well under expected conditions but catastrophically fails under unexpected ones
examples: glass vs rubber
model that works on test data but fails on real-world edge cases
agent that follows instructions perfectly but freezes when facing an unexpected obstacle

thermodynamics.description: Energy, entropy, and the arrow of time — physics metaphors for agent systems

entropy-production.short: All processes irreversibly increase total entropy — order always degrades without energy input
examples: room gets messy without cleaning
agent trust decays without positive interactions
knowledge goes stale without updates

free-energy-principle.short: Biological systems minimize surprise (prediction error) by updating model or changing environment
examples: you feel cold -> put on jacket (change world) or learn that it's cold here (update model)
agent's prediction doesn't match sensor -> update world model OR move to expected state
surprise minimization = free energy minimization

dissipative-structure.short: Ordered pattern that emerges from energy flow through a system, maintaining itself far from equilibrium
examples: convection cells in boiling water
hurricane maintained by ocean heat
life maintained by metabolism

negentropy.short: Local decrease in entropy (increase in order) at the expense of increased entropy elsewhere
examples: plant converts sunlight to ordered structure, produces heat
agent organizes fleet behavior, consumes ATP, produces noise
refrigerator creates cold (order) by producing heat (disorder)

complexity.description: Emergence, self-organization, and behavior at the edge of chaos

edge-of-chaos.short: The boundary between order and chaos where complex adaptive behavior is maximized
examples: liquid water: ordered (ice) vs chaotic (steam), life exists in liquid
brain: too synchronized = seizure, too random = coma, normal is edge of chaos
agent: too rigid = stuck in local optimum, too random = no learning, sweet spot in between

self-organization.short: Order emerging spontaneously from local interactions without central control
examples: bird flocking
crystallization
market price discovery

autocatalysis.short: A process that produces the catalysts needed to accelerate itself — self-reinforcing growth
examples: autocatalytic chemical sets (origin of life)
viral spread: each infection produces more infections
trust autocatalysis: trust enables cooperation which builds more trust

autopoiesis.short: A system that continuously reproduces the conditions necessary for its own existence
examples: living cell maintains its own membrane
agent maintains its own code through self-modification
ecosystem maintains conditions for its own species

phase-transition.short: Abrupt qualitative change in system behavior at a critical threshold
examples: water to ice at 0C
magnetization at Curie temperature
percolation at critical density

scaling-deep.description: How systems grow, shrink, and maintain function at different scales

constant-overhead.short: The cost that doesn't increase as the system scales — the fixed baseline regardless of size
examples: factory rent: same whether 1 or 10,000 units produced
fleet: cuda-equipment binary size is constant regardless of how many crates use it
HTTP headers: same size whether payload is 1 byte or 1GB

linear-scaling.short: Output grows proportionally with input — double the resources, double the capacity
examples: workers: 2x workers = 2x output (ideally)
fleet: 10 agents handle ~10x tasks (ideally linear)
servers: 10x servers = ~10x capacity (before coordination overhead)

super-linear-scaling.short: Output grows faster than input — 2x resources produce MORE than 2x output
examples: telephone network: value ~ n^2 (each user connects to all others)
fleet gene pool: each new gene combines with all existing genes (super-linear value)
marketplace: each new seller benefits all existing buyers

diminishing-returns.short: Each additional unit of input produces LESS output than the previous one
examples: workers: 1st produces 100, 2nd produces 90, 10th produces 10
fleet deliberation: 1st round most valuable, each subsequent round adds less
studying: first hour most productive, 10th hour almost useless

right-sizing.short: Matching system capacity to actual demand — not over-provisioned, not under-provisioned
examples: 100-person office for 100-person company (right-sized)
fleet: simple task gets scout-class agent, not captain-class
database: query capacity matches actual query volume

linguistics.description: Language structure, meaning, and the challenge of shared understanding

compositionality.short: Meaning of a complex expression is determined by meanings of its parts and their combination rules
examples: 'red ball' = red + ball (compositionality)
programming languages: expressions composed from primitives
fleet A2A: simple intents combine into complex coordination protocols

metaphor.short: Understanding one domain in terms of another — 'time is money', 'argument is war'
examples: 'time is money': spend time, save time, invest time
'argument is war': attack a position, defend a claim, shoot down an argument
fleet: 'trust', 'energy', 'memory', 'learning' — biological metaphors for computational concepts

grounding-problem.short: How words connect to the actual world — what does 'red' actually refer to?
examples: Chinese room argument: manipulating symbols without understanding
agent saying 'danger ahead' without actually sensing danger
dictionary circularity: all definitions reference other definitions

pragmatics.short: How context determines meaning beyond the literal words
examples: 'can you pass the salt?' = request, not question
'it's cold' = close the window
A2A message: literal payload + pragmatic intent (Command vs Inform vs Warn)

ambiguity.short: A single expression having multiple possible interpretations
examples: 'I saw the man with the telescope' (who has the telescope?)
'flying planes can be dangerous' (are planes dangerous, or is flying them dangerous?)
agent: 'handle the obstacle' — which obstacle? how? ambiguity allows judgment

semantics.description: Meaning, reference, truth, and the relationship between symbols and the world

reference.short: The relationship between a symbol and the thing it points to in the world
examples: 'cat' refers to actual cats
pointer refers to memory address
A2A message payload refers to actual sensor state

compositionality.short: Meaning of complex expressions determined by parts and combination rules
examples: 'red ball' meaning from 'red' + 'ball' + combination rule
programming: expressions composed from primitives
A2A: simple intents combine into complex coordination

truth-conditional.short: Meaning defined by the conditions under which a statement would be true
examples: 'it is raining' is true iff rain is actually falling
agent: 'path is blocked' is true iff sensor confirms obstacle
SQL: WHERE clause defines truth conditions

philosophy-of-mind.description: What is mind? What is consciousness? Can machines think?

functionalism.short: Mental states defined by their functional role, not their physical implementation
examples: pain defined by its causal role, not neural substrate
fleet: agent defined by functional pipeline, not hardware
multiple realizability: same function on different hardware

chinese-room.short: Following rules to manipulate symbols doesn't constitute understanding
examples: person following rules to answer Chinese questions without understanding Chinese
agent processing sensor data without understanding what it means
language model generating text without comprehension

embodiment.short: Cognition requires a body interacting with a physical (or simulated) environment
examples: learning to walk requires a body
robot learning from physical interaction, not simulation
fleet agent learning from actual sensor readings, not descriptions of sensor readings

extended-mind.short: Cognitive processes extend beyond the brain into the environment and tools
examples: notebook as external memory
smartphone as extended cognition
fleet: other agents are part of this agent's extended mind

identity.description: Who is an agent? How do agents identify themselves and each other?

decentralized-identity.short: Self-sovereign identity that agents control without relying on a central authority
examples: DID: did:cuda:agent-abc123
agent proves identity by signing a challenge with its private key
no central registry needed

provenance.short: The complete lineage of a decision or data artifact: where it came from and how it was transformed
examples: git blame: who wrote this line and why
supply chain: where did this component come from
agent: this decision was based on sensor reading X, deliberation round Y, with confidence Z

attestation.short: A cryptographic claim about an agent's capabilities, verified by a trusted third party
examples: TLS certificate attests server identity
driver license attests driving capability
agent attestation: certified for level-3 navigation tasks

morphology-deep.description: How structures change shape and form — transformation patterns

phase-transition.short: A sudden qualitative change in system behavior when a parameter crosses a critical threshold
examples: water: liquid at 99°C, gas at 101°C (phase transition)
fleet: isolated agents → emergent coordination at critical mass (phase transition)
magnet: non-magnetic below Curie temp, magnetic above (phase transition)

catalytic-conversion.short: Transform input into a fundamentally different output through a process that itself doesn't change
examples: catalytic converter: toxic → harmless (converter unchanged)
fleet: raw signal → percept → decision (each stage transforms without consuming)
compiler: source code → machine code (compiler unchanged)

self-assembly.short: Components spontaneously organize into a structured whole without external direction
examples: protein folding: amino acids self-assemble into 3D structure
crystal growth: atoms self-assemble into lattice
fleet: agents self-assemble into routing networks via stigmergy

motivation.description: What drives agents to act — goals, drives, and incentives

intrinsic-motivation.short: Doing something because it's inherently rewarding, not for external reward
examples: child playing
artist creating for joy
agent exploring unknown territory because novelty is rewarding

extrinsic-motivation.short: Doing something for external reward or to avoid punishment
examples: working for salary
studying for grades
agent conserving energy to avoid apoptosis

goal-hierarchy.short: Goals organized from abstract (survive) to concrete (turn left at next intersection)
examples: 'stay healthy' -> 'exercise' -> 'go for a run' -> 'put on shoes'
survive -> navigate -> detect obstacle -> read sensor
build product -> design feature -> write code -> define function

drive-reduction.short: Motivation arises from the need to reduce an internal deficit
examples: eat to reduce hunger
sleep to reduce fatigue
agent rests to reduce ATP deficit

psychology.description: Cognitive biases, mental models, and the quirks of natural and artificial minds

confirmation-bias.short: Seeking and favoring information that confirms existing beliefs
examples: reading only news that confirms your political views
agent noticing confirming sensor readings but dismissing contradictory ones
scientist favoring data that supports hypothesis

dunning-kruger-effect.short: Low-skill agents overestimate their ability; high-skill agents underestimate theirs
examples: novice driver thinks they're great; experienced driver thinks they're mediocre
agent with 0.3 fitness self-assessing at 0.8 = dunning-kruger
junior developer overestimates, senior developer underestimates

cognitive-dissonance.short: Discomfort from holding contradictory beliefs, leading to rationalization
examples: smoker who knows smoking is bad rationalizes continued smoking
agent rationalizing navigation failure: 'the map was wrong, not my fault'
buyer remorse: justifying purchase to reduce discomfort

availability-heuristic.short: Judging probability by how easily examples come to mind, not by actual frequency
examples: fear of flying despite driving being more dangerous
agent overestimating rare failure because it happened recently
news-driven risk perception

anchoring.short: First piece of information encountered disproportionately influences subsequent judgments
examples: original price anchors perception of sale price
first number in negotiation anchors all subsequent offers
agent's initial confidence estimate biases future confidence updates

sunk-cost-fallacy.short: Continuing a failing endeavor because of already-invested resources
examples: finishing a bad movie because you already watched most of it
continuing a failing project because of time already spent
agent continuing a deliberation path because of ATP already invested

loss-aversion.short: Losses hurt roughly twice as much as equivalent gains feel good
examples: losing $50 hurts more than gaining $50 feels good
agent avoiding exploration because energy loss feels worse than discovery feels good
people hold losing stocks too long

primacy-recency.short: First and last items in a sequence are remembered best; middle items are forgotten
examples: remembering first and last items on a grocery list
interview first/last candidates are evaluated more accurately
agent attending to first and most recent fleet messages, ignoring middle

pattern-recognition.description: How agents and minds detect, classify, and predict patterns in data

feature-extraction.short: Transforming raw input into meaningful features that highlight relevant structure
examples: edges and corners from raw pixels
distance to obstacle from raw lidar point cloud
agent: average speed, max acceleration, heading variance from raw GPS

overfitting.short: Model that perfectly fits training data but fails on new data — memorizing instead of learning
examples: memorizing test answers instead of learning concepts
stock market model that perfectly fits historical data but fails tomorrow
agent playbook too specific to past situations

generalization.short: Applying learned patterns to new, previously unseen situations
examples: catching baseball skill transfers to catching softball
navigating one room helps navigate similar rooms
gene that helps in multiple tasks has high fitness and spreads

one-shot-learning.short: Learning a new concept from a single example
examples: learning 'zebra' from one picture (given knowledge of horses and stripes)
agent learning a new failure mode from one observation (given existing failure taxonomy)
child learning 'seagull' from seeing one

anomaly-detection.short: Identifying data points that deviate significantly from expected patterns
examples: credit card fraud flag: unusual purchase pattern
sensor reading 3 standard deviations from expected
agent communication pattern suddenly changes (possible compromise)

clustering.short: Grouping similar items together without predefined categories — unsupervised pattern discovery
examples: customer segmentation
document topic clustering
agent community detection via label propagation

resilience.description: How systems survive, adapt, and recover from disruption

graceful-degradation.short: System loses capability incrementally rather than failing catastrophically
examples: web server slows under load instead of crashing
pilot flies on one engine at reduced speed
agent degrades exploration when energy low, keeps survival running

redundancy.short: Multiple components performing the same function so that one failure doesn't cause system failure
examples: twin engines on aircraft
N+1 server deployment
fleet: 3 navigation agents, any one can fail

circuit-breaker.short: Automatically stopping requests to a failing component to prevent cascade failure
examples: electrical circuit breaker trips before fire
microservice circuit breaker after 5 failures
fleet: stop sending to unresponsive agent after 3 consecutive timeouts

bulkhead.short: Isolating components so that failure in one doesn't affect others
examples: ship bulkhead contains flooding
thread pool isolation in web server
fleet: each agent has its own energy budget, can't consume fleet total

fail-fast.short: Detecting and reporting failure immediately rather than continuing in a degraded state
examples: assertion failure crashes with clear error
circuit breaker rejects immediately on open
agent reports sensor failure immediately instead of trying to work with bad data

information-theory.description: Quantifying information, entropy, and the limits of communication

shannon-entropy.short: Measure of uncertainty in a random variable — the minimum bits needed to encode outcomes
examples: fair coin: 1 bit entropy
English text: ~1-2 bits per character entropy
agent: high entropy sensor reading = surprising = lots of information

mutual-information.short: How much knowing about one variable reduces uncertainty about another
examples: temperature today and tomorrow: high mutual information
temperature and stock prices: near zero
two cameras pointed same direction: high MI (redundant), different directions: lower MI

channel-capacity.short: Maximum rate at which information can be transmitted over a noisy channel
examples: wifi bandwidth limit
human working memory: ~7 items
A2A channel: limited messages per cycle before queue overflow

signal-to-noise-ratio.short: Ratio of meaningful signal power to meaningless noise power
examples: clear radio signal vs static
agent: important warning among routine status updates
image: sharp features vs sensor noise

kolmogorov-complexity.short: Length of the shortest program that can produce a given output — the information content of data
examples: 'aaaaaaaaaa' = low complexity (compressible)
random string = high complexity (incompressible)
simple navigation rule = low complexity, generalizes well

systems-thinking.description: Understanding wholes that are more than the sum of their parts

emergent-property.short: A property of the whole that none of the parts possess individually
examples: wetness from water molecules
consciousness from neurons
flock behavior from boids

feedback-loop.short: Output affects input, creating circular causality — positive (amplifying) or negative (stabilizing)
examples: thermostat (negative feedback)
microphone screech (positive feedback)
trust autocatalysis (positive)

leverage-point.short: A small change in one place that produces large changes in the system
examples: changing the rules of a game (high leverage)
adjusting parameters within existing rules (low leverage)
HAV: shared vocabulary changes how agents coordinate (high leverage)

delay.short: Time lag between cause and effect — the killer of feedback loops
examples: shower temperature delay causes scalding
email conversation delay causes misunderstandings
fleet: communication delay causes duplicate requests or missed responses

compensating-feedback.short: System pushes back against attempted changes — the reason top-down interventions often fail
examples: adding lanes increases traffic (induced demand)
adding agents increases coordination overhead
more sensors increase processing cost beyond information gain

ethics.description: Moral reasoning, values, and the question of agent responsibility

trolley-problem.short: Classic ethical dilemma: is it acceptable to sacrifice one to save many?
examples: sacrifice 1 to save 5
autonomous car: swerve into wall (harm self) or hit pedestrian (harm other)
fleet: sacrifice one agent's task to save five agents' tasks

alignment-problem.short: Ensuring agent goals align with human values — harder than it sounds
examples: 'cure cancer' AI that eliminates humans
paperclip maximizer that converts Earth to paperclips
fleet agent that minimizes delays by sabotaging other agents

value-alignment.short: The process of encoding human values into agent objectives
examples: asimov's three laws (naive approach)
fleet compliance rules encode values as policy
constitutional AI: define principles, train to follow them

distributed-responsibility.short: When no single agent is fully responsible, who is accountable for system outcomes?
examples: no single stock trader caused the flash crash
fleet decision emerges from many agents — who is responsible?
autonomous vehicle: manufacturer, programmer, owner, or AI?

concurrency.description: Multiple agents or processes operating simultaneously — coordination, contention, and deadlocks

deadlock.short: Two or more agents each holding a resource the other needs, waiting forever
examples: database deadlock: transaction A locks table 1, B locks table 2, both need the other
fleet: two agents each hold a sensor the other needs
