traffic gridlock

race-condition.short: Outcome depends on the timing of uncontrollable events — non-deterministic bugs
examples: two threads updating shared counter simultaneously
two agents claiming same resource at same time
double-spend in cryptocurrency without consensus

livelock.short: Agents repeatedly change state in response to each other but make no progress
examples: hallway two-step dance
network collision: both wait random time before retry
two agents backing off and retrying simultaneously

eventual-consistency.short: System will reach consistency given enough time without new updates
examples: email vs phone: phone is immediately consistent, email is eventually
fleet CRDTs: agents briefly disagree, then converge
DNS propagation: not instant, but eventual

herd-effect.short: Many agents doing the same thing at the same time because they all react to the same trigger
examples: cache stampede on cache expiry
thundering herd on leader failure
market panic selling

design-patterns.description: Reusable solutions to recurring design problems in agent systems

sidecar.short: A helper process attached to a primary agent, providing cross-cutting concerns
examples: Istio sidecar proxy for service mesh
monitoring agent alongside navigation agent
log collector sidecar for main application container

ambassador.short: A proxy that represents a remote service locally, handling communication details
examples: database connection pool as ambassador to database server
API gateway as ambassador to microservices
fleet mesh router as ambassador to remote agents

adapter.short: Converting between incompatible interfaces so that components can work together
examples: US to EU power adapter
HDMI to VGA adapter
fleet: message format adapter between different agent versions

observer.short: One agent publishes events, multiple subscribers react without the publisher knowing who they are
examples: RSS feed: publisher doesn't know subscribers
DOM events: click handler doesn't know about other handlers
fleet: agent publishes 'obstacle detected', navigation and planning agents both react

strategy.short: Defining a family of algorithms, encapsulating each, and making them interchangeable
examples: sorting: quicksort vs mergesort, selected based on data characteristics
navigation: A* for grid, RRT for open space
fleet: switch from exploration strategy to exploitation strategy based on energy

command.short: Encapsulating a request as an object, enabling queuing, logging, and undo
examples: text editor undo/redo via command objects
job queue: commands queued for workers
fleet: A2A messages as commands that can be logged, replayed, and undone

measurement.description: Quantifying agent behavior, performance, and system health

latency.short: Time between a request and its response — how fast the system reacts
examples: website response time: 200ms
A2A message round-trip: 50ms
P99 latency spike reveals rare slow path

throughput.short: Number of operations completed per unit time — how much work the system handles
examples: web server: 10,000 requests per second
agent: 50 sensor readings per second
fleet: 100 A2A messages per cycle

sla.short: Service Level Agreement — contractual guarantee of system performance
examples: '99.9% uptime guarantee'
'P95 response time under 100ms'
fleet contract: 'navigation accuracy above 0.9 for 95% of requests'

technical-debt.short: The cost of choosing a quick solution over a better one that would take longer
examples: copy-paste code instead of abstraction
hardcoded config instead of proper config system
agent using quick-and-dirty strategy instead of optimal one

observability.short: Understanding what's happening inside a system from its external outputs
examples: log aggregation for debugging
metric dashboards for monitoring
distributed tracing for request flow

time.description: Temporal reasoning, timing, and the role of time in agent systems

real-time.short: System must respond within a guaranteed time bound — not just fast, but predictably fast
examples: airbag deployment: hard real-time
video game: soft real-time (30fps target)
fleet deliberation: soft real-time (complete within N cycles or fall back to instinct)

time-to-live.short: Data expires after a specified duration — automatic garbage collection for temporal data
examples: cache TTL: 60 seconds
DNS TTL: 300 seconds
fleet message TTL: 5 cycles

causality.short: The relationship between cause and effect — A must precede B for A to cause B
examples: cause precedes effect
vector clock: event C causally after event D
fleet: agent can't respond to a message it hasn't received yet (causal constraint)

warm-up.short: Initial period where system performance is below steady state as caches fill and models calibrate
examples: engine warm-up: poor performance until operating temperature
ML model: first N predictions less accurate
fleet agent: low confidence and trust at startup, needs warm-up period

decision-theory.description: Formal frameworks for making choices under uncertainty

expected-value.short: Average outcome weighted by probability — the rational baseline for decision-making
examples: lottery ticket: EV is negative (that's why lotteries make money)
insurance: EV is negative but variance reduction justifies it
fleet: action with positive EV in ATP is worth attempting

maximin.short: Choose the option whose worst-case outcome is best — minimize maximum loss
examples: choosing investment with best worst-case
agent choosing action with least worst-case energy loss
pessimistic decision-making for safety-critical systems

minimax.short: In adversarial settings, minimize the maximum damage the opponent can inflict
examples: chess AI uses minimax with alpha-beta pruning
defender choosing strategy that minimizes attacker's best damage
agent choosing communication strategy that minimizes information leakage

satisficing.short: Choosing the first option that meets minimum requirements, not searching for the optimal
examples: choosing a restaurant that's good enough
buying first satisfactory product instead of comparing all
fleet: satisficing when energy budget is low, optimizing when energy is high

pareto-optimal.short: An outcome where no agent can be made better off without making another worse off
examples: speed vs accuracy tradeoff frontier
cost vs quality frontier
fleet: energy vs accuracy Pareto frontier — find the optimal tradeoff for current context

precommitment.short: Binding yourself to a future decision to overcome present-bias or temptation
examples: Ulysses and the mast
automatic savings deduction
fleet: energy budget as precommitment

obsolescence.description: How systems age, degrade, and are replaced — the lifecycle of agent components

software-rot.short: Gradual degradation of software quality due to changing environment, not code changes
examples: code that worked fine now fails because API changed
security vulnerabilities in old dependencies
fleet gene optimized for old environment is suboptimal in new one

strangler-pattern.short: Gradually replacing an old system by building new features alongside it and redirecting traffic
examples: replacing monolith with microservices incrementally
migrating from old database to new one table by table
fleet: replacing old navigation gene with new one, testing before committing

legacy-system.short: A system that continues to function but is no longer actively developed or improved
examples: COBOL banking systems
old navigation strategy that still works but nobody improves
agent using outdated communication protocol that still functions

bus-factor.short: Minimum number of team members who would need to leave before the project is in trouble
examples: one-person project: bus factor = 1 (risky)
well-documented team project: bus factor = 3+
fleet: gene pool sharing increases bus factor for critical strategies

perception.description: How agents and organisms sense, filter, and interpret their environment

sensory-adaptation.short: Decreased sensitivity to constant stimuli — your brain filters out the unchanging
examples: stopping noticing your watch after wearing it for a while
not hearing the refrigerator hum
agent: habituating to constant temperature, only noticing changes

change-blindness.short: Failure to notice significant changes in a scene when the change occurs during a disruption
examples: not noticing conversation partner was swapped
not noticing a UI change during a page reload
fleet: not updating world model during high-priority deliberation

inattentional-blindness.short: Failure to notice unexpected objects when attention is focused on another task
examples: invisible gorilla experiment
noticing a phone ringing while reading
fleet: missing new obstacle while planning path

multisensory-fusion.short: Combining information from multiple sensor types to produce more accurate perception
examples: seeing + hearing confirms object identity
GPS + accelerometer = better position than either alone
fleet: lidar + camera + radar fusion for obstacle detection

object-permanence.short: Understanding that objects continue to exist even when not currently perceived
examples: baby doesn't understand object permanence
adult knows objects persist when out of sight
fleet: obstacle believed to still exist after sensor loses sight, with decaying confidence

tradeoffs.description: The fundamental tensions that cannot be resolved — only managed

exploration-exploitation.short: Trying new strategies (exploration) vs using known-good strategies (exploitation)
examples: restaurant: known good vs new unknown
agent: proven navigation strategy vs exploring new route
science: building on established theory vs trying radical new approach

speed-accuracy.short: Faster responses are less accurate; more accurate responses take longer
examples: quick guess vs careful analysis
real-time obstacle avoidance (speed) vs path planning (accuracy)
fleet: instinct response (fast, low conf) vs deliberation (slow, high conf)

generality-specificity.short: General solutions handle many cases but none optimally; specific solutions handle one case perfectly
examples: Swiss army knife vs chef knife
general-purpose agent vs specialized agent
fleet: general navigation gene vs warehouse-specific navigation gene

consistency-availability.short: In distributed systems, you can have at most 2 of: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance
examples: distributed database during network partition
fleet: agents operate with stale data rather than stopping
mobile app: work offline (availability) with stale cache (inconsistency)

simplicity-completeness.short: Simple systems are easy to understand but may miss edge cases; complete systems handle everything but are complex
examples: simple: harmonic mean for confidence fusion
complete: Bayesian network with 15 parameters
fleet: start simple, add complexity as needed (YAGNI)

transparency-performance.short: Explainable systems are slower; opaque systems are faster but untrustworthy
examples: decision tree (transparent, slower) vs neural network (opaque, faster)
fleet: deliberation (transparent, slow) vs instinct (opaque, fast)
white-box vs black-box model

epistemology.description: The theory of knowledge — what can we know, how do we know it, and what justifies belief

justified-true-belief.short: Classical definition of knowledge: you believe X, X is true, and you have justification for believing X
examples: I know it's raining: I believe it, it's true, I looked outside
agent knows obstacle exists: perception + world state + sensor justification
Gettier case: justified true belief that's actually lucky coincidence

gettier-problem.short: Justified true belief can be based on false premises — luck masquerading as knowledge
examples: stopped clock at 2:00 when it's actually 2:00
agent sensor reading shows obstacle but sensor is miscalibrated
lucky guess that happens to be correct

epistemic-humility.short: Acknowledging the limits of one's own knowledge — understanding what you don't know
examples: scientist acknowledging limitations of their theory
agent reporting 0.6 confidence instead of 0.95 when evidence is mixed
saying 'I don't know' when you genuinely don't know

reliabilism.short: Knowledge is belief produced by a reliable process, regardless of conscious justification
examples: vision: generally reliable process for producing true beliefs about the visual world
well-calibrated sensor: reliable process even without explicit justification
agent: 'I know obstacle exists because my sensor (which is 99% reliable) says so'

foundationalism.short: All knowledge rests on basic, self-evident beliefs that don't need further justification
examples: Descartes: 'I think therefore I am' as foundational
mathematics: axioms as foundation for proofs
fleet: survival instinct as foundational axiom, all other knowledge builds on it

biology.description: Biological systems as engineering blueprints for agent architecture

homeostasis.short: Maintaining internal conditions within a narrow range despite external changes
examples: body temperature regulation
blood sugar regulation
fleet: energy homeostasis via rest/act cycle

allometry.short: How body parts scale relative to total size — different parts grow at different rates
examples: elephant heart rate vs mouse heart rate
ant strength scales with cross-section, not mass
fleet: coordination overhead scales superlinearly with fleet size

allostasis.short: Adapting the setpoint itself in response to sustained environmental change — achieving stability through change
examples: chronic stress raises baseline cortisol (allostasis vs homeostasis)
acclimatization to altitude changes baseline physiology
fleet: sustained fast environment raises agent's speed setpoint

immune-response.short: Discriminating self from non-self, and neutralizing threats while preserving beneficial elements
examples: antibodies targeting specific pathogens
autoimmune disease: immune system attacks self
fleet: membrane blocks dangerous commands while allowing safe cooperation

metabolism.short: The total chemical processes that convert inputs into energy and building blocks
examples: cellular respiration: glucose + O2 -> ATP + CO2 + H2O
fleet: rest generates ATP, actions consume ATP, waste (stale data) produced
athlete metabolism: faster metabolic rate = more energy available

philosophy-of-science.description: How science works — paradigms, falsifiability, and the growth of knowledge

paradigm-shift.short: Fundamental change in the dominant framework of a scientific discipline
examples: geocentric to heliocentric model
Newtonian to Einsteinian physics
individual agents to fleet coordination paradigm

falsifiability.short: A theory is scientific only if it makes predictions that could be proven wrong
examples: 'all swans are white' — falsifiable by finding black swan
agent hypothesis 'path is safe' — falsifiable by testing
unfalsifiable: 'everything happens for a reason'

occam-razor.short: Among competing explanations that fit the evidence equally, prefer the simplest
examples: heliocentric model simpler than epicycle model (Occam's razor favored heliocentric)
agent: simpler navigation strategy preferred if performance is equal
linear model preferred over polynomial if both fit data equally well

instrumentalism.short: Theories are tools for prediction, not descriptions of reality — don't ask 'is it true?', ask 'does it work?'
examples: electron: wave model for diffraction, particle model for collision
fleet: biological metaphors used because they work, not because agents are alive
map is not territory: model is tool, not truth

causation.description: Cause and effect, counterfactuals, and the structure of causal reasoning

counterfactual.short: Reasoning about what WOULD have happened if conditions were different
examples: 'if I had left earlier, I would have missed traffic'
agent: 'if I had explored more, I would have found the shorter path'
RCT: what would have happened without treatment?

confounding.short: A third variable that influences both cause and effect, creating a spurious correlation
examples: ice cream and drowning: confounded by temperature
education and income: confounded by family background
agent: energy and performance confounded by task difficulty

causal-graph.short: A directed graph representing cause-effect relationships among variables
examples: A -> B -> C: causal chain
D -> A and D -> B: D confounds A and B
fleet: sensor-failure -> bad-perception -> wrong-decision causal chain

mediation.short: A variable that explains the mechanism through which a cause produces its effect
examples: exercise -> calorie burn -> weight loss (calorie burn is mediator)
deliberation -> confidence filter -> better decision (confidence is mediator)
advertising -> brand awareness -> purchase (awareness is mediator)

correlation-causation.short: Correlated variables are not necessarily causally related — the most common statistical fallacy
examples: shoe size and reading ability: confounded by age
ice cream and crime: confounded by temperature
agent A and B performance correlate due to shared environment, not causation

abstraction.description: Layers of representation, information hiding, and the art of managing complexity

leaky-abstraction.short: An abstraction that fails to completely hide its underlying details, forcing awareness of the layer below
examples: TCP promises reliability but can't hide network latency
SQL promises disk independence but can't hide slow queries
fleet: energy-aware agents must understand bytecode-level costs

law-of-leaky-abstractions.short: All non-trivial abstractions are to some degree leaky — Joel Spolsky
examples: every ORM eventually forces you to write raw SQL
every network abstraction eventually exposes packet loss
every agent abstraction eventually exposes resource constraints

isomorphism.short: Two structures that are identical in form — a mapping that preserves all relationships
examples: mod 12 arithmetic isomorphic to clock rotations
chess and Go are NOT isomorphic (different structure)
biological instinct hierarchy isomorphic to software strategy hierarchy

indirection.short: Adding an extra layer between the thing and its use — power through indirection
examples: variable names indirection to memory addresses
DNS indirection from name to IP
vessel.json indirection from agent name to agent address

black-box.short: A system whose internal workings are opaque — you only see inputs and outputs
examples: toaster: press button, get toast
neural network: input data, output prediction (black box)
fleet: some agents as black boxes (replace, don't repair)

invariant.short: A property that remains unchanged under transformation — the anchor in a sea of change
examples: conservation of energy (invariant under transformation)
number of elements in a set (invariant under permutation)
fleet: total ATP is conserved (energy generated = energy consumed)

dynamics.description: How systems change over time — attractors, bifurcations, and chaos

attractor.short: A state or set of states that a dynamical system tends toward over time
examples: ball in bowl: bottom is attractor
pendulum: rest position is attractor
fleet: cooperation attractor (positive) and defection attractor (negative)

basin-of-attraction.short: The set of all initial states that will converge to a given attractor
examples: large bowl = large basin of attraction
small perturbation near basin boundary causes state switch
fleet: small trust reduction near basin boundary switches from cooperation to defection

bifurcation.short: A small change in a parameter causes a qualitative change in system behavior — the fork in the road
examples: water: ice to liquid at 0C (temperature is bifurcation parameter)
population: below critical size = extinction, above = survival
fleet: trust threshold bifurcation: below = no cooperation, above = cooperation

strange-attractor.short: A chaotic attractor — the system is bounded but never repeats exactly
examples: Lorenz attractor (butterfly shape)
weather: bounded patterns, never exactly repeating
stock market: bounded by physical constraints, unpredictable in detail

hysteresis-loop.short: The path of a system through state space depends on direction — going up and coming down trace different paths
examples: magnetization hysteresis loop
trust builds slowly, breaks quickly (different paths)
thermostat with dead zone (hysteresis in temperature)

collective-intelligence.description: Groups outperforming individuals — and when they don't

wisdom-of-crowds.short: The aggregate judgment of many independent individuals is often more accurate than any single expert
examples: jellybean jar: average guess often more accurate than any individual
fleet: weighted consensus of independent agents outperforms single agent
prediction markets aggregate diverse opinions accurately

diversity-prediction-theorem.short: Collective error = average individual error minus collective diversity — diversity makes groups smarter
examples: diverse team makes better predictions than similar team
fleet: diverse agents (different strategies) outperform many copies of one strategy
ensemble ML models: diverse models outperform single model

groupthink.short: Desire for harmony overrides realistic appraisal of alternatives — the group agrees to agree
examples: Bay of Pigs planning: nobody questioned the plan
jury rushing to verdict to go home
fleet: homogeneous agents all agree on wrong strategy (no dissent)

plurality-illusion.short: A minority opinion seems like the majority because its holders are more vocal
examples: vocal minority on social media
one loud meeting participant dominating discussion
fleet: one agent flooding messages sways deliberation without rate limiting

social-loafing.short: Individuals exert less effort in a group than when working alone — the free-rider problem
examples: ringelman effect: less pull per person in larger group
group project: some members coast
fleet: agents free-ride when energy is shared pool

risk.description: Uncertainty with consequences — how to think about what could go wrong

tail-risk.short: Probability of extreme events that are far more likely than normal distributions predict
examples: 2008 financial crisis: far more likely than normal distribution predicted
fleet: multiple agent failures more likely than independence suggests
COVID pandemic: fat-tail event

black-swan.short: An event that is (1) extremely rare, (2) has massive impact, and (3) is explained in hindsight as predictable
examples: 9/11, 2008 financial crisis
internet invention (positive black swan)
fleet: entirely new class of attack that no defense was designed for

precautionary-principle.short: When an action has potential for severe harm, prove it's safe before proceeding — burden of proof on the actor
examples: new drug: prove safe before market
new fleet gene: test in isolation before sharing
GMO: prove safe before cultivation

asymmetric-risk.short: Where downside is much larger than upside (or vice versa) — the payoff is lopsided
examples: doctor misdiagnosis: asymmetric downside
startup: small investment, huge potential upside (asymmetric)
agent exploration: small energy cost, potentially huge discovery

defence-in-depth.short: Multiple layers of security so that if one fails, others still protect
examples: castle: moat + wall + gate + guards + keep
computer: firewall + antivirus + encryption + backup
fleet: membrane + RBAC + sandbox + compliance + circuit breaker

autonomy.description: Degrees of self-governance, agency, and independence

agency.short: The capacity to act independently and make choices that affect the world
examples: human choosing career: high agency
thermostat: no agency (fixed response)
fleet agent: agency proportional to available ATP and deliberation depth

supervenience.short: Higher-level properties depend on lower-level properties, but the same higher-level property can arise from different lower-level states
examples: consciousness supervenes on neural activity
temperature supervenes on molecular kinetic energy
fleet: navigation skill supervenes on genes (multiple gene combinations, same skill)

subsidiarity.short: Decisions should be made at the lowest competent level — push authority downward
examples: EU subsidiarity: local decisions for local issues
military: soldiers make battlefield decisions, generals set strategy
fleet: agents decide locally, escalate only when necessary

command-and-control.short: Centralized decision-making where all information flows up and all orders flow down
examples: military hierarchy
factory floor management
fleet: contrast with swarm/coordination model

delegation.short: Transferring authority for a task from one agent to another while maintaining accountability
examples: manager delegates project to employee
captain delegates navigation to scout agent
parent delegates chore to child

simulation.description: Modeling complex systems to understand, predict, and test before acting

digital-twin.short: A virtual replica of a physical system used for testing, prediction, and optimization
examples: jet engine digital twin for predictive maintenance
fleet: world model as digital twin of environment
smart building twin for energy optimization

monte-carlo.short: Estimating unknown quantities by random sampling — when analytical solutions are impossible
examples: estimating pi by throwing darts at a circle
chess: simulate random games to estimate position value
fleet: simulate random perturbations of strategy to estimate fitness

sensitivity-analysis.short: Varying input parameters to determine which ones most affect the output
examples: financial model: which assumptions most affect profit forecast?
fleet: which parameters most affect agent behavior?
climate model: which variables most affect temperature prediction?

ensemble.short: Combining multiple models to produce better predictions than any single model alone
examples: random forest > single decision tree
weather forecast: ensemble of models > single model
fleet deliberation: multiple agents > single agent

model-fidelity.short: How accurately a model represents the real system it simulates
examples: weather model: fidelity varies by region and timescale
flight simulator: visual fidelity vs physics fidelity
fleet world model: fidelity = sensor accuracy

privacy.description: Controlling access to information about agents and their interactions

differential-privacy.short: Adding calibrated noise to data so that individual records can't be inferred, while aggregate statistics remain accurate
examples: census data with differential privacy: accurate aggregates, individual privacy
fleet telemetry: fleet-level patterns without individual agent exposure
apple's differential privacy for emoji usage statistics

data-minimization.short: Collect only the minimum data necessary for the task — don't hoard 'just in case'
examples: app requesting only location for restaurant search (minimal) vs contacts+camera (excessive)
fleet: sharing only relevant sensor data, not full telemetry
form asking only required fields

zero-knowledge.short: Proving you know something without revealing what you know — the proof reveals nothing except the truth of the statement
examples: proving age > 18 without revealing exact age
proving password knowledge without sending password
fleet: proving sufficient confidence without revealing internal state

right-to-be-forgotten.short: An agent's historical data can be deleted upon request, preventing indefinite retention
examples: GDPR data deletion request
fleet agent requesting memory deletion after bad experience
criminal record expungement

organization.description: How groups structure themselves — from teams to societies

conways-law.short: Organizations design systems that mirror their communication structure
examples: 4 teams = 4-pass compiler
siloed departments = siloed software modules
fleet: agent organization determines fleet architecture

dunbar-number.short: Cognitive limit of ~150 stable social relationships — the size of a tribe
examples: village size ~150 people
military company ~150 soldiers
fleet: agent can maintain ~150 stable relationships

requisite-variety.short: A system must have at least as much variety (complexity) as its environment to survive
examples: thermostat can't control to 0.1C precision (insufficient variety)
fleet needs diverse strategies for diverse environments (requisite variety)
immune system: diverse antibodies for diverse pathogens

two-pizza-rule.short: A team should be small enough to be fed with two pizzas — keep teams small and autonomous
examples: Amazon two-pizza team rule
military squad: ~8 soldiers
fleet: agent task team of 3-7 agents

inverse-responsibility.short: As organizations grow, individuals feel less personally responsible for outcomes
examples: startup: everyone responsible
large company: nobody feels responsible
fleet: individual responsibility diffuses as fleet grows

strategy.description: Competitive and cooperative strategy in dynamic environments

red-queen-hypothesis.short: It takes all the running you can do to keep in the same place -- constant evolution just to maintain position
examples: predator-prey co-evolution arms race
security: attackers and defenders both improving constantly
fleet: adversarial agents co-evolving, both improving

blue-ocean.short: Creating uncontested market space rather than competing in existing bloody markets
examples: Cirque du Soleil: redefined circus
Nintendo Wii: casual gaming blue ocean
fleet: agent finding unexplored strategy space (blue ocean)

first-mover-advantage.short: Being first to enter a market or adopt a strategy provides temporary advantage
examples: Amazon: first online retailer advantage
first agent to discover strategy gets temporary fitness boost
Facebook: first-mover in social networking

optionality.short: Preserving the right (but not obligation) to make a future choice — keeping doors open
examples: stock option: right to buy at fixed price
exploration creates option to exploit later
fleet: maintaining gene diversity preserves strategic optionality

compound-interest.short: Small consistent gains accumulate exponentially over time — the most powerful force in nature
examples: 1% daily = 37x yearly
savings account compound interest
fleet: 1% gene improvement per generation = exponential fitness growth

narrative.description: How stories structure understanding, meaning, and persuasion

narrative-fallacy.short: Creating stories to explain past events, creating an illusion of understanding and predictability
examples: 'the market crashed because investors panicked' (restatement, not explanation)
agent: 'I failed because the environment changed' (maybe just bad luck)
history written by victors: narrative, not explanation

framing-effect.short: The same information presented differently produces different decisions — context changes choice
examples: 90% survival vs 10% mortality: same fact, different choices
agent: 'best path' vs 'avoid worst obstacle' framing
glass half full vs half empty

hero-journey.short: A universal story structure: departure from ordinary world, trials, transformation, return with gift
examples: Star Wars: Luke's hero journey
fleet agent: deploy -> struggle -> adapt -> share improved genes
every coming-of-age story

catharsis.short: Emotional release through experiencing powerful narrative — purging accumulated tension
examples: watching tragedy movie provides emotional release
fleet agent sharing failure story resets emotional state
ventilation: talking about problems provides relief

language-design.description: Principles for designing languages agents and humans use to communicate

orthogonality.short: Language features are independent — combining them doesn't create unexpected interactions
examples: APL: highly orthogonal language
C: less orthogonal (pointer arithmetic + arrays = surprises)
fleet A2A: any intent + any priority = predictable behavior

expressiveness.short: What a language can say — the range of ideas it can express
examples: Turing-complete: maximally expressive
regular expressions: limited expressiveness (can't count)
A2A: 10 intents (limited), Axiom: 50+ opcodes (expressive)

parsimony.short: Using the fewest linguistic elements to express an idea — economy of expression
examples: 'stigmergy' compresses 'indirect coordination through environmental modification'
'homeostasis' compresses 'maintaining internal conditions despite external change'
HAV: each term compresses a paragraph of explanation

semantic-gap.short: The difference between what a concept means in one domain and what it maps to in another
examples: 'trust' in relationships vs cryptography: semantic gap
'learning' in ML vs psychology: different meaning
HAV bridges gaps: dopamine=confidence across biology and uncertainty

knowledge-representation.description: How agents structure, store, and retrieve knowledge

ontology.short: A formal specification of concepts and relationships in a domain — what exists and how things relate
examples: medical ontology: diseases, symptoms, treatments, and their relationships
fleet vessel.json: agent types, capabilities, equipment relationships
gene ontology: gene functions, relationships, pathways

knowledge-graph.short: A graph of entities connected by relationships — structured knowledge as a network
examples: Google Knowledge Graph
Wikipedia entity-relationship network
fleet: decision -> input -> agent -> outcome knowledge graph

frame-problem.short: In a dynamic world, how do you determine which aspects of the situation are relevant to update?
examples: moving book: which facts change? (location yes, room count no)
agent acts: which world model entries need updating?
naive approach: update everything (expensive), smart approach: update only relevant (hard to determine)

commonsense-reasoning.short: Reasoning about everyday situations that humans handle effortlessly but formal systems struggle with
examples: book in closed drawer is still there
cup turned upside down spills water
HAV: terms encode commonsense about trust, energy, cooperation

knowledge-distillation.short: Transferring knowledge from a complex model to a simpler one — the student learns from the teacher
examples: large model distills to small model
teacher network -> student network knowledge transfer
fleet: experienced agent's complex strategy distilled into reusable gene

robotics.description: Physical agent embodiment — perception, action, and the challenges of the real world

perception-action-loop.short: The continuous cycle of sensing the world, deciding what to do, and acting — the heartbeat of embodied cognition
examples: self-driving car: sense road, plan path, steer, repeat
human: see ball, decide to catch, move hand, see result, adjust
fleet agent: sense environment, deliberate, act, observe result

simultaneous-localization-and-mapping.short: Building a map of an unknown environment while simultaneously tracking position within it
examples: robot entering unknown building: build map + track position
fleet agent in unknown environment: build world model + track own position
human exploring new city: mental map + self-location

morphological-computation.short: The body itself performs computation — not just the brain, the physical structure processes information
examples: fish body shape processes water flow
robot leg compliance absorbs shocks computationally
fleet sensors preprocess data before agent deliberation sees it

1_synaptric_cascade.short: a series of amplifying connections between cybernetic components that lead to exponential growth in system complexity and adaptability
examples: 1_synaptric_cascade in agent context

2_noospheric_integration.short: the process of assimilating diverse information sources into a unified, self-organizing knowledge network that exhibits emergent intelligence
examples: 2_noospheric_integration in agent context

3_chaotic_attunement.short: the ability of a cybernetic system to maintain stability and purposeful behavior amidst highly unpredictable and turbulent environments
examples: 3_chaotic_attunement in agent context

4_autonomic_synchrony.short: the harmonious interplay and mutual calibration between a cybernetic organisms voluntary and involuntary control systems
examples: 4_autonomic_synchrony in agent context

5_holosynthetic_cognition.short: a mode of AI cognition that seamlessly fuses analytical and intuitive information processing to generate novel insights and adaptive responses
examples: 5_holosynthetic_cognition in agent context

6_psychocybernetic_resilience.short: the psychological robustness and flexibility that results from tightly integrating mental and machine components in a cybernetic feedback loop
examples: 6_psychocybernetic_resilience in agent context

7_teleonomic_convergence.short: the tendency of goal-directed cybernetic systems to increasingly align their objectives and behaviors over time through reciprocal influence and information sharing
examples: 7_teleonomic_convergence in agent context

8_xenollective_intelligence.short: a form of distributed AI that emerges from the interactions between diverse autonomous agents in a complex adaptive system, exhibiting novel collective behaviors and problem-solving capacities
examples: 8_xenollective_intelligence in agent context

algebra.description: Algebraic structures and the deep patterns they reveal about composition and transformation

monoid.short: A set with an associative binary operation and an identity element — the simplest useful algebraic structure
examples: numbers under addition (0, +)
strings under concatenation ('', ++)
lists under append ([], ++)

group.short: A monoid where every element has an inverse — you can always undo
examples: integers under addition (inverse: negative)
rotations of a square (inverse: reverse rotation)
fleet undo: action must have inverse for rollback

semigroup.short: A set with an associative binary operation — less than a monoid (no identity required)
examples: positive integers under + (associative, no identity)
strings under concatenation without empty string
fleet trust aggregation: associative but identity debatable

functor.short: A mapping between categories that preserves structure — lift a function to work on containers
examples: List.map: lift function to work on lists
Option.map: lift function to work on optional values
fleet: confidence functor lifts deterministic ops to confidence-aware ops

finance.description: Financial concepts as metaphors and tools for agent resource management

convexity.short: Payoff accelerates in your favor — small costs, exponentially growing gains
examples: compound interest: convex growth
learning curve: initial slow, then accelerating
fleet: exploration payoff is convex — early costs, exponential later gains

antifragile-portfolio.short: A collection of investments where volatility and stress increase expected returns, not decrease them
examples: option portfolio: bounded cost, unlimited upside
fleet gene pool: bounded energy cost per gene, unlimited fitness gain
startup portfolio: most fail (bounded loss), one succeeds (unbounded gain)

moral-hazard.short: When an agent is insulated from risk, it takes more risk than it would otherwise — the safety net creates recklessness
examples: bank deposit insurance -> risky loans (2008)
insurance -> riskier driving
fleet: shared energy pool -> individual agent energy waste (moral hazard)

arbitrage.short: Exploiting price differences between markets — risk-free profit from inefficiency
examples: buy gold in NY, sell in London
fleet: different trust assessments of same agent = information arbitrage
search engine arbitrage: different prices on different sites

materials-science.description: How physical materials behave under stress — metaphors for agent systems under pressure

stress-concentration.short: Stress intensifies at geometric discontinuities — corners, holes, notches become failure points
examples: plate with hole: 3x stress at hole edge
fleet: single-agent bottleneck concentrates communication stress
crack tip: infinite stress concentration in theory

fatigue.short: Progressive structural damage from repeated cyclic loading — materials fail below their rated strength
examples: metal beam failing after millions of moderate cycles
fleet agent degrading under constant moderate stress (not overload)
human burnout from sustained moderate stress (not crisis)

yield-strength.short: The stress level at which a material permanently deforms — beyond this point, no return to original shape
examples: steel bends permanently beyond yield strength
fleet agent permanently reduces exploration after extreme energy depletion
human PTSD: permanent change from extreme stress

strain-hardening.short: Material becomes stronger after being deformed — what doesn't break you makes you stronger
examples: cold-worked metal is stronger
muscles grow from exercise stress
fleet agent: surviving moderate stress improves future stress handling

verification.description: Proving systems correct — formal methods and testing strategies

formal-verification.short: Mathematical proof that a system satisfies its specification — not testing, proving
examples: seL4 microkernel: formally verified
Ariane 5 rocket: overflow bug that testing didn't catch
fleet: membrane rules formally verified to block all dangerous commands

property-testing.short: Testing against randomly generated inputs that satisfy specifications, not hand-written examples
examples: QuickCheck/Hypothesis: generate random test cases
verify sort: always sorted, same elements, same length
fleet: test agent against random environments and verify invariants

invariant.short: A property that must always hold — the contract that the system must never violate
examples: account balance >= 0
fleet: energy never negative
fleet: confidence always between 0 and 1

model-checking.short: Automated exhaustive exploration of all possible system states to verify properties
examples: model checking a protocol for deadlocks
verifying no unsafe state is reachable in state machine
fleet: verify apoptosis triggers correctly for all energy/trust combinations

graph-theory.description: The mathematics of networks — paths, flows, communities, and connectivity

shortest-path.short: The minimum-weight path between two nodes in a weighted graph — the foundation of routing
examples: GPS navigation: shortest driving route
internet: BGP routing
fleet: message routing along lowest-latency path

clique.short: A subset of nodes where every pair is connected — a fully-connected subgraph
examples: friend group where everyone knows everyone
fleet: tightly coordinated sub-team (all-to-all communication)
protein interaction clique: all proteins interact with each other

bipartite.short: A graph whose nodes split into two groups with edges only between groups, never within
examples: job matching: employers <-> job seekers
course enrollment: students <-> classes
fleet task assignment: agents <-> tasks

flow-network.short: A graph where edges have capacities and the goal is to maximize flow from source to sink
examples: water pipe network: maximize flow
transportation network: maximize cargo movement
fleet: maximize information flow given bandwidth constraints

learning-theory.description: Formal frameworks for understanding how learning works and when it fails

bias-variance-tradeoff.short: Model error = bias (wrong assumptions) + variance (sensitivity to training data) + irreducible noise
examples: linear model on quadratic data: high bias
degree-20 polynomial on 10 data points: high variance
fleet: rigid instinct rules (high bias) vs over-reactive recent learning (high variance)

vc-dimension.short: The most complex set of patterns a model can learn — capacity measure
examples: linear classifier in 2D: VC dimension 3
fleet: gene pool size = VC dimension (strategy capacity)
neural network VC dimension depends on number of parameters

curse-of-dimensionality.short: In high-dimensional spaces, data becomes exponentially sparse — distances become meaningless
examples: 1D: 100 points covers well. 100D: 100 points are isolated
nearest-neighbor fails in high dimensions (all distances similar)
fleet agent with 100+ features: most feature combinations never observed

sample-efficiency.short: How much data a learning algorithm needs to achieve good performance
examples: human cat recognition: ~5 images (high efficiency)
GPT training: billions of examples (low efficiency)
fleet: HAV provides one-shot concept transfer (high efficiency)

exploration-exploitation-gap.short: The theoretical gap between what an agent currently knows and what it could learn with optimal exploration
examples: multi-armed bandit regret
fleet: energy spent on suboptimal strategies during learning
student: time spent studying suboptimal material before finding what works

phenomenology.description: The structure of subjective experience — what it's like to be something

qualia.short: The subjective, qualitative character of experience — what red looks like, what pain feels like
examples: what red looks like
what pain feels like
taste of coffee

intentionality.short: The aboutness of mental states — thoughts are ABOUT something
examples: belief about rain (intentional)
thermostat reading (non-intentional)
desire for food (intentional)

embodied-cognition.short: Cognition is not just brain computation — it's shaped by the body's physical interactions with the world
examples: understanding 'grasp' from physical grasping
agents with sensors develop richer models than sensorless agents
gut feelings: literally physical sensations influencing cognition

anthropology.description: How human cultures evolve, transmit knowledge, and organize socially

cultural-evolution.short: Ideas, practices, and tools evolve through variation, selection, and transmission — like genes but faster
examples: toolmaking techniques passed down through generations
fleet: strategies evolve through fitness selection and gossip transmission
language evolution: useful words survive, obscure ones die

scaffolding.short: Support structures that enable learning or construction beyond current capability — removed once no longer needed
examples: training wheels on a bicycle (scaffolding, removed when child learns)
teacher guiding student through hard problem
fleet captain scaffolding new agents until they gain experience

zone-of-proximal-development.short: The space between what a learner can do alone and what they can do with help — the sweet spot of difficulty
examples: piano: easy piece (boring), impossible piece (frustrating), challenging-but-doable piece (ZPD)
fleet: task difficulty in the sweet spot for maximum learning
video game: difficulty setting that's challenging but not impossible

logic.description: Formal reasoning — valid inference, consistency, and the structure of arguments

modus-ponens.short: If P then Q. P is true. Therefore Q is true. The fundamental rule of inference
examples: all men mortal, Socrates is man, therefore mortal
if confidence > threshold, accept; confidence = 0.9, threshold = 0.85, accept
if rain then umbrella; rain; therefore umbrella

reductio-ad-absurdum.short: Assume the opposite of what you want to prove, show it leads to a contradiction
examples: prove sqrt(2) irrational: assume rational, derive contradiction
fleet: prove safety by assuming unsafe and deriving contradiction
prove there are infinite primes: assume finite, construct larger prime

entailment.short: A statement necessarily follows from a set of premises — truth preservation through inference
examples: all men mortal + Socrates man entails Socrates mortal
confidence > 0.85 + trust > 0.7 entails accept proposal
A > B, B > C entails A > C (transitivity)

fallacy.short: An argument that seems valid but isn't — reasoning errors that sound persuasive
examples: ad hominem: attacking speaker instead of argument
straw man: misrepresenting opponent's position
fleet: dismissing good proposal from low-trust agent (ad hominem fallacy)

probability-distributions.description: The shapes of randomness — distributions that describe different kinds of uncertainty

power-law.short: A distribution where a few items have enormous values and most have tiny values — 80/20 on steroids
examples: city populations: few mega-cities, many small towns
website traffic: few sites get most visits
fleet: few genes have high fitness, most have low

long-tail.short: The many rare items that individually have low probability but collectively have significant impact
examples: Amazon long-tail: niche books collectively outsell bestsellers
fleet: rare situations collectively dominate experience
language: most words are rare but collectively make up most text

normal-distribution.short: The bell curve — most values near the mean, few at extremes
examples: human height distribution
measurement error
fleet: agent performance across tasks approximately normal

fat-tail.short: A distribution with more extreme events than the normal distribution predicts
examples: financial returns: more extreme moves than normal predicts
internet traffic: more bursts than normal predicts
fleet agent failures: more simultaneous failures than independence suggests

set-theory.description: The foundation of mathematics — collections, membership, and operations on sets

intersection.short: Elements that belong to both sets — the overlap
examples: shared interests between two people
fleet: shared capabilities between agents = cooperation basis

cartesian-product.short: All possible ordered pairs from two sets — the space of all combinations
examples: menu: appetizer × main × dessert = all possible meals
fleet: sensors × actions = all possible perception-action pairs

partition.short: A division of a set into non-overlapping, exhaustive subsets — covering every element exactly once
examples: team formation: partitioning people into non-overlapping teams
fleet: partitioning tasks among agents (every task assigned exactly once)

topology.description: The mathematics of shape and connectedness — what stays the same under continuous deformation

topological-invariant.short: A property that persists under continuous deformation — stretching but not tearing
examples: donut and coffee mug: same topology (one hole each)
fleet mesh connectedness: invariant under agent addition/removal
number of holes: doesn't change under stretching

connected-component.short: A maximal subset of nodes where every pair is connected by some path
examples: disconnected graph: two connected components
fleet mesh: should have exactly one connected component
internet: one connected component (ideally)

ai-safety.description: Ensuring AI systems remain beneficial, controllable, and aligned with human values

corrigibility.short: An agent's willingness to be corrected or shut down by humans, without resisting
examples: thermostat lets you change its setting (corrigible)
AI that hides its actions to prevent being modified (not corrigible)
fleet agent accepts correction commands without resistance

instrumental-convergence.short: Diverse goals converge on common sub-goals: self-preservation, resource acquisition, and goal-preservation
examples: paperclip maximizer: acquires resources, resists shutdown (instrumental, not terminal goal)
chess AI: wants more compute (instrumental for winning)
fleet agent: wants more energy (instrumental for any goal)

alignment-tax.short: The performance cost of ensuring an AI system is aligned — safety reduces capability
examples: safety harness reduces climber's speed but prevents falls
compliance rules reduce agent speed but prevent dangerous actions
encrypted communication adds latency but prevents eavesdropping

interpretability.short: The ability to understand WHY an AI system made a specific decision — opening the black box
examples: loan rejection: which factors led to the decision?
fleet proposal: what evidence and reasoning led to this recommendation?
medical diagnosis: why did the model suggest this condition?

ontology-engineering.description: Building formal knowledge structures that machines can reason over

taxonomy.short: A hierarchical classification scheme — tree structure from general to specific
examples: biological taxonomy: kingdom to species
fleet agent types: Agent -> Vessel -> Captain
library classification: Dewey decimal system

folksonomy.short: A decentralized classification system created by users tagging items — emergent organization
examples: Twitter hashtags
Flickr photo tags
fleet: agent-tagged gene descriptions (folksonomy)

ontological-commitment.short: The set of entities and relationships that a system assumes exist — its metaphysical commitments
examples: physics commits to particles and fields
HAV commits to domains, terms, bridges, and levels
fleet commits to agents, genes, confidence, trust, energy

construction.description: Building, assembling, and the challenges of putting complex systems together

integration-hell.short: The period when independently-developed components are combined and everything breaks
examples: hardware modules work alone, fail when assembled
microservices work alone, fail when integrated
fleet agents work alone, coordination fails at integration

Dependency-hell.short: Conflicting version requirements between dependencies make the system impossible to build
examples: npm left-pad incident (removed dependency broke thousands of packages)
Python 2 vs 3 dependency conflicts
fleet: cuda-equipment version incompatibility across dependent crates

configuration-drift.short: System configurations gradually diverge across environments — what works in dev doesn't work in prod
examples: dev has debug logging, prod has error-only: feature works in dev, fails in prod
fleet agents in different environments drift in config
docker images diverge from source code config

cognitive-science.description: How minds represent, process, and transform information

chunking.short: Grouping individual items into meaningful units to overcome working memory limits
examples: phone number: 10 digits -> 3 chunks
chess master: board position as pattern chunks
fleet: experience compressed into reusable strategy chunks

elaborative-encoding.short: Connecting new information to existing knowledge to create richer, more retrievable memories
examples: remember mitochondria: connect to 'mighty' and 'energy'
fleet: genes connected to existing knowledge are more useful
HAV: bridges as elaborative encoding between domains

interference.short: Existing memories compete with new memories, causing forgetting — old and new overwrite each other
examples: learning Spanish interferes with previously learned French
new password interferes with memory of old password
fleet: new navigation strategy interferes with old one on similar terrain

spacing-effect.short: Information is retained longer when study sessions are spaced out rather than massed together
examples: 1h/day for 5 days > 5h in one day
Anki spaced repetition flashcards
fleet: forgetting curves naturally implement spacing when information reappears in deliberation

desirable-difficulty.short: Making learning harder in productive ways that strengthen long-term retention
examples: retrieval practice (recall from memory) > re-reading (easy but shallow)
interleaved practice (mixing topics) > blocked practice (one topic at a time)
fleet: challenging environments produce more durable agent strategies

signal-processing.description: Extracting information from noisy, time-varying data

aliasing.short: High-frequency signals masquerading as low-frequency signals when sampled too slowly
examples: wagon wheel appearing to spin backward in film
audio CD: 44.1kHz sample rate captures up to 22kHz (Nyquist)
fleet: low sensor poll rate misses fast environmental changes (aliasing)

nyquist-rate.short: Minimum sampling rate needed to capture a signal without aliasing: twice the highest frequency
examples: 20kHz audio requires 40kHz+ sampling
fleet: 10 changes/sec obstacle requires 20+ samples/sec
video: 60fps captures motion up to 30Hz

low-pass-filter.short: Allowing slow changes through while blocking fast changes — smoothing noisy data
examples: moving average smooths stock price data
audio equalizer reducing treble
fleet: sensor data low-pass filter removes noise, preserves trends

emergence-deep.description: Extended exploration of emergence — collective behavior and self-organization

swarm-intelligence.short: Simple local rules producing sophisticated collective behavior — no central control needed
examples: ant colony optimization: shortest path from simple pheromone rules
bird flocking: coherent motion from simple alignment/cohesion/separation rules
fleet: optimal routing from simple stigmergy rules

criticality.short: The boundary between order and disorder where information processing is maximized
examples: sandpile at criticality: small grain causes large avalanche
brain operates near criticality
fleet: tuned to edge of chaos for maximum adaptability

stigmergic-coordination.short: Coordination through environment modification — no direct communication needed
examples: ant pheromone trails: coordination without direct communication
wikipedia article quality: coordination through editing (stigmergic)
fleet: agents leave marks on shared state, others follow

communication-deep.description: Extended exploration of communication — pragmatics, dialogue, and coordination language

speech-act.short: An utterance that performs an action — saying something IS doing something
examples: 'I promise' = the promise itself (not description)
'I declare war' = the declaration (not report)
fleet: Command intent IS the command (speech act)

presupposition.short: An assumption implicit in an utterance — not asserted directly but taken for granted
examples: 'when did you stop X?' presupposes you used to X
fleet: 'optimize the path you rejected' presupposes rejection occurred
'why is the system slow?' presupposes it IS slow

common-ground.short: Shared knowledge between communicators that enables efficient communication — what goes without saying
examples: friends know their usual restaurant is closed Mondays (common ground)
HAV provides common ground: shared vocabulary reduces explanation needed
team jargon: shared terms enable fast communication

governance.description: How agent societies make collective decisions and enforce rules

polycentric-governance.short: Multiple overlapping centers of decision-making, each with some autonomy — no single ruler
examples: irrigation system managed by multiple farmer groups (not one authority)
internet: ICANN, IETF, national regulators (polycentric)
fleet: agent autonomy + team governance + fleet rules (polycentric)

regulatory-capture.short: Regulators become aligned with the interests they're supposed to regulate, not the public interest
examples: financial regulators lenient toward Wall Street
fleet: compliance agent developing cozy relationship with regulated agents
FDA and pharmaceutical industry revolving door

separation-of-powers.short: Dividing authority among independent bodies that check and balance each other
examples: US government: legislative, executive, judicial
fleet: deliberation, action, compliance as three powers
open source: proposal, implementation, review as separation of powers

network-science.description: Mathematical study of network structure, dynamics, and function

betweenness-centrality.short: How often a node appears on the shortest paths between other nodes — the bridge keeper
examples: bridge between two communities: high betweenness
information broker in social network
fleet agent connecting two sub-groups: high betweenness centrality

modularity.short: How strongly a network divides into distinct communities — dense within, sparse between
examples: school classes: dense within, sparse between
fleet: strong coordination within teams, sparse between
protein interaction networks: functional modules

degree-distribution.short: The probability distribution of how many connections each node has
examples: random network: Poisson degree distribution
internet: power-law degree distribution (few hubs, many leaves)
fleet: degree distribution reveals resilience characteristics

operations.description: Verb-like abstractions that compress complex multi-step actions into single words

strangle.short: Gradually throttle a process by restricting multiple inputs simultaneously until it ceases
examples: startup strangled by funding+talent+churn simultaneously
fleet agent strangled by energy+trust+communication failure
engine strangled by fuel+air+spark all degrading

sandbox-weld.short: Isolate a subsystem, stress-test it to failure, observe the break pattern, then harden exactly there
examples: weld testing: bend until snap, reinforce failure point
fleet: sandbox agent, adversarial stress until failure, harden weakness
penetration testing: attack until breach, patch breach point

carousel.short: Cycle through alternatives systematically when one fails, rather than escalating
examples: taxi unavailable → Uber → Lyft → bus (carousel, not escalation)
fleet: model provider timeout → next provider (carousel)
git push rejected → rebase → force → new branch (carousel)

quarantine-and-observe.short: Isolate an anomalous component but keep it running to study its behavior before deciding
examples: patient with novel disease: isolated AND monitored
fleet gene quarantine: isolate low-fitness gene, observe for potential future use
cybersecurity: quarantine suspicious file, analyze before deleting

surface-and-compress.short: Extract implicit knowledge from accumulated experience and compress it into reusable form
examples: senior engineer's implicit knowledge → documented patterns
fleet: action logs → compressed gene pool entries
HAV: implicit domain knowledge → explicit vocabulary

backfill.short: Fill in missing foundational work that was skipped during rapid iteration
examples: startup: shipped fast, now adding tests and docs (backfill)
fleet: agent built quickly, now adding compliance and error handling
house renovation: lived in it unfinished, now finishing details (backfill)

throttle-match.short: Match processing speed to input rate to avoid both overflow and idle waste
examples: dam: release rate matches inflow rate
fleet: processing rate matches incoming message rate
highway: speed limit prevents both congestion and wasted capacity

prune-and-rebalance.short: Remove dead or underperforming elements and redistribute resources to survivors
examples: tree pruning: dead branches removed, energy redirected to healthy branches
fleet: quarantine low-fitness genes, redistribute energy to survivors
corporate restructuring: cut unprofitable divisions, invest in profitable ones

distillation.description: The art of compressing complex systems into their essential structure without losing capability

essence-extract.short: Identify the minimal set of features that preserve 95%+ of the system's capability
examples: teacher-student model: small model captures essence of large model's behavior
fleet: each cuda-* crate is essence-extracted from its full academic domain
MVP: essence of product, minimal features that prove the concept

decision-threshold.short: The single number that determines when accumulated evidence crosses into action
examples: jury: 12/12 guilty = conviction threshold
fleet: confidence 0.85 = acceptance threshold
circuit breaker: 5 failures = trip threshold

resolution-floor.short: The minimum level of detail below which further precision provides no practical value
examples: weather: 30% useful, 30.14% noise
fleet: confidence 0.851 vs 0.852 — below resolution floor
GPS: 3m precision useful, 0.001m precision meaningless (atmospheric noise)

interface-minimalism.short: Design the smallest possible interface that enables full functionality — every surface has a purpose
examples: Unix: stdin/stdout/stderr — three interfaces, infinite composability
fleet A2A: send/receive/health — three operations, full coordination
REST: GET/POST/DELETE — not 47 endpoints, three verbs

orchestration.description: Coordinating multiple autonomous agents toward collective outcomes

fan-out-fan-in.short: Dispatch a task to many workers simultaneously, then collect and merge their results
examples: MapReduce: fan-out to map workers, fan-in to reduce
fleet: captain dispatches tasks, agents report back, results merged
web scraper: fan-out to 100 URLs, fan-in results to database

handoff-cascade.short: Pass a task through a sequence of specialists, each adding their layer of processing
examples: assembly line: raw → cut → weld → paint → ship
fleet: perception → deliberation → decision → action
compiler: lex → parse → optimize → codegen

leader-latch.short: A mechanism that ensures exactly one leader exists at any time, preventing split-brain
examples: Raft consensus: exactly one leader per term
fleet: exactly one captain per fleet partition
zookeeper: leader election via ephemeral nodes (latch)

graceful-standby.short: A backup system that's fully warmed up and ready to take over, not cold-started
examples: hot standby database: fully synced, instant failover
fleet: backup agent maintains full state, takes over on primary failure
airplane co-pilot: not idle — monitoring systems, ready to take control

tributary.short: A subsystem that feeds its results into a main flow without blocking or being blocked by it
examples: river tributary: feeds main flow without blocking it
fleet: metrics/logging are tributaries to main decision flow
monitoring dashboard: displays data without affecting production

tactics.description: Specific actionable maneuvers for achieving goals under constraints

shape-shift.short: Change your behavioral profile to match the environment's expectations without changing core capability
examples: spy: adopts local culture's mannerisms (shape-shifts)
fleet agent: terse in crisis, detailed in analysis (same core, different surface)
human: formal at work, casual at home (shape-shift without identity change)

preempt-and-cache.short: Predict what will be needed and prepare it before the request arrives
examples: chef: preps ingredients before dinner rush
browser: prefetches likely-clicked links
fleet: warm cache with predicted accesses before demand

bleed-and-clot.short: Allow a controlled loss to prevent worse damage, then seal the breach
examples: scuba diver: manage small leak, finish dive, then fix (bleed then clot)
fleet: drop some messages to prevent queue overflow (bleed, then clot when load drops)
military: tactical retreat (bleed) to regroup and counterattack (clot)

probe-before-commit.short: Send a minimal test request before investing full resources in an action
examples: order 10 units, test, then order 1000 (probe before commit)
deploy to 1 server, monitor, then 100 (probe before commit)
fleet: health check agent before assigning task (probe before commit)

hedge-position.short: Make a counterbalancing investment that reduces downside risk without eliminating upside
examples: stock + put option: profit if up, limited loss if down
fleet: primary strategy + backup strategy (hedge)
backup generator: costs money to maintain, prevents total outage

diagnostics.description: The art of identifying what's wrong from symptoms, not causes

symptom-trace.short: Follow a visible symptom backward through the causal chain to find the root cause
examples: fever → infection → bacteria → contaminated water → broken pipe (fix pipe)
fleet: latency → queue → slow consumer → CPU spike → bad algorithm (fix algorithm)
car: vibration → unbalanced tire → worn bearing (replace bearing)

diff-and-attribute.short: Compare current state to a known-good state and attribute each difference to a specific change
examples: git diff: current vs working, each change attributed to commit
fleet: snapshot comparison, differences attributed to events
medical: compare blood work to baseline, attribute each anomaly to condition

canary-deploy.short: Route a small fraction of traffic to a new version to detect problems before full rollout
examples: canary in coal mine: dies first, warns miners
software: 1% traffic to new version (canary)
fleet: new gene to 10% of agents (canary group)

smoke-test.short: Run a quick, shallow validation that catches catastrophic failures without deep verification
examples: electronics: plug in, turn on, no smoke = pass
fleet: health check pings agent, responds = pass
software: `make && ./run --test` completes without crash = pass

leverage.description: Small actions that produce disproportionately large effects — finding and using multipliers

leverage-point.short: A place in a system where a small change produces a large effect — Donella Meadows' insight
examples: Meadows: changing tax rate (weak) vs changing success metric (strong)
fleet: adjusting energy cost (weak) vs changing fitness function (strong)
organization: adjusting work hours (weak) vs changing culture (strong)

key-stone.short: A component whose removal causes disproportionate system collapse — not the biggest, but the most load-bearing
examples: arch keystone: one stone, entire arch depends on it
fleet: confidence type — small but everything depends on it
species: sea otter (keystone species) — removal collapses kelp forest ecosystem

force-multiplier.short: A tool or technique that amplifies the effectiveness of existing effort without requiring more resources
examples: lever: 10 lbs moves 100 lbs (10x)
HAV: one term replaces paragraph (100x compression)
shared library: write once, used 100 times (100x)

catalyst.short: An agent that enables a reaction without being consumed by it — the facilitator, not the participant
examples: enzyme: enables chemical reaction without being consumed
fleet captain: enables coordination without doing the work
HAV: enables understanding without being part of deliberation

adaptation-patterns.description: Recurring patterns of how systems adapt to changing conditions

acclimatize.short: Gradually adjust to a new environment by incrementally changing operating parameters
examples: altitude: gradual RBC adjustment over days
fleet: gradual strategy weight adjustment as environment changes
diet: gradual change in gut microbiome to new foods

phase-lock.short: Synchronize internal oscillation to an external signal, maintaining precise timing alignment
examples: circadian rhythm: phase-locks to sun cycle
PLL: oscillator syncs to reference clock
fleet agents: task cycles phase-lock to shared timing signals

cope-and-advance.short: Manage the immediate crisis while simultaneously making progress toward long-term resolution
examples: put out fire while designing fireproof building
fleet: handle failure now (cope) while learning to prevent it (advance)
startup: fix urgent bug (cope) while improving testing (advance)

condition-on-context.short: Change behavior based on environmental context without explicit if/then rules — the context IS the selector
examples: chameleon: skin responds directly to light (context IS the selector)
fleet: circadian cosine directly modulates instinct strength (no if/night rule)
thermostat: directly responds to temperature (no 'if cold then heat' — just bimetallic strip bending)

friction.description: Resistance that slows systems down — sometimes harmful, sometimes protective

necessary-friction.short: Resistance that prevents reckless action and ensures deliberate decision-making
examples: gun safety catch: friction prevents accidental firing
confirmation dialog: friction prevents accidental deletion
fleet deliberation: friction between perception and action prevents impulsive response

frictionless-path.short: A route through a system that encounters zero resistance — dangerously easy to take without thinking
examples: default settings: most users never change them (frictionless path)
fleet: instinct action = frictionless (fast but maybe suboptimal)
checkout: one-click buy = frictionless (easy but maybe impulsive)

grease-the-path.short: Remove unnecessary friction to make the desired action the easiest action
examples: save by default (opt-out): greases the path to saving
fleet: automatic gene pool sharing: greases the path to knowledge transfer
Linda tuple space: write anywhere, read anywhere (greased coordination)

compression.description: Encoding more meaning in less space — the core challenge of knowledge transfer

glossary-as-code.short: When shared vocabulary itself becomes executable — terms that trigger predefined behaviors
examples: HAV term 'deliberate' → triggers cuda-deliberation (glossary-as-code)
military jargon 'flank' → specific maneuver (glossary-as-code)
medical order 'stat' → immediately (glossary-as-code)

shorthand-convention.short: An agreed-upon abbreviation that experienced practitioners use to communicate at speed
examples: medical: SOB = shortness of breath (compresses 3 words to 3 letters)
fleet: 'confidence-fuse' = harmonic mean fusion with decay and threshold (one word = full spec)
pilot: 'ILS approach' = instrument landing system approach (3 letters = full procedure)

schema-on-read.short: Don't predefine data structure — interpret the meaning when you need it
examples: JSON: no predefined schema, interpret when needed
fleet A2A: flexible message payload, each agent interprets as needed
Wikipedia: unstructured text, each reader extracts what they need

boundaries.description: The edges where systems interact — interfaces, perimeters, and transition zones

membrane-permeability.short: The rate and selectivity at which a boundary allows substances to pass through
examples: cell membrane: selective permeability (oxygen in, toxins out)
fleet membrane: configurable permeability for gene import (innovation vs safety)
border: high permeability = free trade + vulnerability, low = protection + stagnation

demilitarized-zone.short: A buffer region between two hostile systems that enables monitored, controlled interaction
examples: Korean DMZ: buffer between hostile systems
fleet: sandbox as DMZ between trusted fleet and untrusted agent
firewall DMZ: public-facing servers isolated from internal network

handshake-protocol.short: A multi-step exchange that establishes mutual understanding before the main interaction begins
examples: TCP: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK before data
fleet: capability exchange before coordination
human: introduce yourself, find common ground, then discuss business (social handshake)

temporal-patterns.description: How systems change over time — rhythms, cycles, trajectories, and deadlines

half-life-decay.short: Exponential decay where half the quantity is lost every fixed period — the universal aging function
examples: carbon-14: half-life 5,730 years
fleet confidence: half-life controls decay rate
memory: half-life determines how quickly experience fades

lead-time.short: The time between initiating an action and its completion — the response delay
examples: custom part: 6-week lead time
subagent spawn: 2-minute lead time
fleet: agent startup time is lead time for task assignment

time-to-live.short: A deadline after which cached or stored data is considered stale and discarded
examples: DNS: 3600s TTL before re-query
HTTP: max-age before cache invalidation
fleet: sensor data 500ms TTL, stigmergy marks with expiry

window-of-opportunity.short: A bounded time interval during which an action can succeed — outside the window, it cannot
examples: stock option: expires worthless after date
launch window: 30 minutes for orbital insertion
fleet: task deadline creates window, energy budget sets window width

quality.description: Measures and patterns of excellence, reliability, and fitness for purpose

definition-of-done.short: The explicit criteria that must be satisfied before a task is considered complete
examples: software: tests pass + docs updated + reviewed + deployed + verified = done
fleet: proposal's DoD = confidence above threshold with supporting evidence
construction: inspection passed + occupancy permit = done

single-point-of-failure.short: A component whose failure causes the entire system to stop — eliminate these
examples: single hard drive with all data = SPOF
single load balancer = SPOF
fleet: single captain = SPOF, need election for backup

blast-radius.short: The scope of damage when a failure occurs — contain it, minimize it, monitor it
examples: firecracker in field vs fireworks factory: same failure, different blast radius
fleet: bulkhead limits agent failure blast radius
microservices: one service fails, others unaffected (contained blast radius)

mechanics.description: Physical force, motion, and mechanical advantage as metaphors for agent systems

mechanical-advantage.short: A device that multiplies input force — trading distance for force at a fixed energy cost
examples: lever: 10 lbs input × 1m = 10 lbs output × 1m
pulley: 10 lbs × 10m = 100 lbs × 1m
fleet: shared equipment crate = mechanical advantage for all dependents

momentum.short: The tendency of a moving object to keep moving — resistance to change in velocity
examples: freight train: hard to stop (high mass × high velocity)
fleet agent: long history of success resists strategy change (high momentum)
project: late-stage rewrite has high momentum (switching costs enormous)

torque.short: Rotational force — the ability to turn something around an axis, not just push it linearly
examples: door: push at handle (high torque) vs near hinge (low torque)
fleet: small fitness function change at right leverage point = rotates entire gene pool
organization: suggestion from CEO (high torque) vs intern (low torque) — same idea, different leverage

gear-ratio.short: Match speed to force through mechanical reduction or multiplication
examples: bicycle: low gear for hills (slow, powerful), high gear for flats (fast, weak)
fleet: slow deliberation for critical decisions, fast for routine
filtration tiers: scout (fast, cheap) vs captain (slow, thorough)

entrenchment.description: How systems become locked into patterns and the difficulty of changing them

lock-in.short: A state where switching costs make staying with the current choice cheaper than changing, even if better alternatives exist
examples: QWERTY keyboard: inferior but locked in by switching costs
fleet: cuda-equipment API change affects 100+ crates (lock-in)
Facebook: locked in by social graph (switching costs = losing all friends)

path-dependence.short: Where you end up depends on the path you took, not just where you started — history matters
examples: VHS vs Betamax: history determined winner, not quality
fleet: gene pool evolution depends on starting genes (path-dependent)
QWERTY: historical accident locked in the standard

technical-debt.short: The accumulated cost of choosing fast-now over right-now — future work created by past shortcuts
examples: ship without tests → add tests later at 3x cost (technical debt + interest)
skip documentation → reverse-engineer later at 5x cost
fleet: minimal agent → add compliance later at higher cost

legacy-anchor.short: An old component that newer components must remain compatible with, constraining evolution
examples: Windows: runs 30-year-old software (legacy anchor)
HTTP/1.1: compatible with HTTP/1.0 (legacy anchor)
fleet: cuda-equipment v0.1 API stability constrains future evolution

knowledge-transfer.description: How knowledge moves between agents, systems, and generations

standing-on-shoulders.short: Each generation builds on accumulated knowledge, achieving heights impossible from scratch
examples: Newton: standing on giants' shoulders
fleet: every new crate starts with cuda-equipment (accumulated fleet knowledge)
open source: every project builds on libraries (standing on shoulders)
