knowledge-distillation.short: Transfer knowledge from a complex source to a simpler representation without losing essential capability
examples: teacher 70B → student 7B: 90% capability, 10% cost
HAV: 30-page paper → 200-word term: 95% meaning, 1% cost
senior engineer's intuition → documented pattern: transferable at lower cost

apprenticeship.short: Transfer tacit knowledge through observation and guided practice, not explicit instruction
examples: blacksmith apprentice: watches master, practices, gets corrected
fleet: gene crossover from successful agents = apprenticeship
residency: medical students observe attending physicians (apprenticeship)

document-or-it-didn't-happen.short: Knowledge that exists only in someone's head is not knowledge — it's a dependency on that person
examples: senior engineer leaves → undocumented deployment process lost
fleet: HAV terms documented = transferable regardless of which agent wrote them
science: experiment results not published = didn't contribute to collective knowledge

prompt-classification.short: Every input is first classified by complexity tier before routing — cheap model for simple, expensive for complex, never use sledgehammer for thumbtack
examples: classify(prompt) → Simple|Complex|Novel → route to appropriate model
triage: sort patients by severity before assigning resources
support tier 1/2/3: simple questions answered cheaply

metaphor.description: Cross-domain mapping as a cognitive tool — understanding new things through familiar structures

structural-mapping.short: Transfer understanding from a well-known domain to a less-known domain by matching relational structure
examples: electricity = water: structural mapping (voltage=pressure, current=flow)
dopamine = confidence: structural mapping (accumulation, decay, threshold, modulation)
gene = strategy: structural mapping (inheritance, mutation, selection, fitness)

reification.short: Treating an abstract concept as if it were a concrete, manipulable object
examples: time is money: abstract time treated as concrete currency
fleet: confidence as f64 type — abstract concept treated as concrete number
gene as object: abstract strategy pattern treated as manipulable data structure

metaphorical-distance.short: How far apart the source and target domains of a metaphor are — distance affects both insight and confusion
examples: stock market = roller coaster (close distance, safe but shallow)
consciousness = computation (far distance, risky but insightful)
fleet: biology → agent systems (moderate distance, productive mapping)

interface-patterns.description: How system boundaries are designed, crossed, and maintained

contract-first.short: Define the interface contract before implementing either side — agreement before code
examples: OpenAPI spec before client/server implementation
fleet: vessel.json defines vessel contract before building vessel
TypeScript interface before implementing class

version-with-grace.short: Support old and new interface versions simultaneously during transition, then deprecate old
examples: HTTP/2 coexists with HTTP/1.1 during transition
Python 2 and 3 coexisted for years
fleet: cuda-equipment versions coexist during transition period

soft-error.short: A failure that doesn't crash the system but returns a degraded result with error context
examples: hard error: system crash. soft error: partial result with error flag
fleet: deliberation returns 'inconclusive' instead of crashing (soft error)
API: 200 with degraded data vs 500 crash (soft vs hard error)

attention-deep.description: How systems allocate limited processing capacity to the most important inputs

attention-budget.short: A fixed cognitive resource that must be allocated across competing demands — spend wisely
examples: human: ~4 hours deep focus per day (attention budget)
fleet: agent attention budget allocated across deliberation, messages, perception
RAM: fixed memory budget allocated across processes

salience-bottleneck.short: The rate at which important information can be identified and acted upon — the awareness throughput
examples: human: can notice 3-5 dashboard anomalies simultaneously
fleet: saliency scoring filters to top-N inputs (salience bottleneck)
news: 1000 stories published, only 5 noticed (salience bottleneck)

habituation.short: Decreased response to a stimulus after repeated exposure — the brain's noise filter
examples: ticking clock: noticed at first, invisible after 10 minutes
fleet: constant sensor reading loses attention, only changes break through
smell: you notice perfume when entering a room, not after 5 minutes

defense_in_depth.short: A layered security strategy where multiple independent controls are applied so that if one fails, others still provide protection.
examples: defense_in_depth in agent context

blast_radius_containment.short: The practice of limiting the impact scope of a security breach or system failure to the smallest possible area of the system.
examples: blast_radius_containment in agent context

error-strategies.description: Systematic approaches to handling failures gracefully

fail-fast.short: Detect and report errors immediately rather than continuing in a degraded state
examples: Rust: Result type forces explicit error handling (fail-fast)
fleet: critical path failure → immediate error, not silent degradation
assert: program crashes on invariant violation (fail-fast)

fail-safe.short: When failure occurs, transition to a state that is safe, not just stopped
examples: fuse blows → house doesn't burn (fail-safe)
train signal fails → red → trains stop (fail-safe)
fleet: energy depleted → rest mode (fail-safe, not chaos)

retry-with-backoff.short: Attempt a failed operation again, but with increasing delay between attempts
examples: network timeout: retry at 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s (exponential backoff)
fleet: provider timeout → retry with backoff → eventually succeeds
git push conflict: retry after delay → someone else pushed, now pull-able

circuit-breaker.short: Stop attempting a failing operation after repeated failures, allowing time for recovery
examples: electrical circuit breaker: trips on overload, resets after cooldown
fleet: failing provider → circuit opens → no attempts → half-open test → resume or re-open
API gateway: failing backend → circuit opens → traffic diverted

coordination-deep-2.description: Advanced coordination patterns for multi-agent systems

consensus-throughput.short: The rate at which a fleet can reach agreement on decisions, measured in decisions per unit time
examples: 10 agents: 10 decisions/sec, 1000 agents: 100 decisions/sec (throughput)
fleet: gossip protocol optimizes consensus throughput
restaurant: 4 people order in 2 min, 20 people order in 15 min

quorum-threshold.short: The minimum number of agents that must agree before a fleet decision becomes binding
examples: board: 51% quorum for decisions, 67% for constitutional changes
fleet: high-impact decisions need higher quorum
jury: unanimous for criminal, majority for civil (quorum varies)

leader-follower-phase.short: The temporary emergence of a leader agent in a peer fleet for the duration of a specific coordination task
examples: emergency: most experienced doctor leads, others follow, then flat again
fleet: crisis → election → lead → resolve → flat
wolf pack: alpha for hunt, not for everything

swarm-quorum.short: Achieving consensus through many weak signals aggregating into a strong decision, like bees choosing a hive site
examples: bees: 100 scouts report sites, strongest signal wins (swarm-quorum)
fleet: many weak agent assessments aggregate into strong decision
wikipedia: many small edits produce reliable article (swarm-quorum)

composition.description: Building complex systems from simpler, independently useful parts

plug-and-play.short: Components that interoperate without configuration — connect them and they work
examples: USB: plug in device, it works (plug-and-play)
fleet: define equipment type, automatically integrates everywhere
HDMI: plug monitor in, it works (plug-and-play)

mix-and-match.short: Combine components freely from a menu of options to create custom configurations
examples: burrito bowl: choose from menu of components (mix-and-match)
fleet: perception + deliberation + motor = agent (mix-and-match crates)
PC building: choose CPU, GPU, RAM, storage independently (mix-and-match)

side-effect-free.short: A computation that produces a result without modifying any external state — pure function
examples: f(x) = x + 1: pure, no side effects, always same output for same input
fleet: Conf::fuse(a, b): pure function, no state mutation
map function in functional programming: pure, composable

observability.description: Understanding what a system is doing from the outside, without modifying it

telemetry.short: Automated collection and transmission of system health and performance data
examples: plane black box: automated recording of all flight data
car OBD: real-time engine telemetry
fleet: cuda-metrics collects agent performance telemetry

distributed-trace.short: Follow a single request across multiple services to understand the full lifecycle and find bottlenecks
examples: HTTP request traced through frontend → auth → inventory → payment
fleet: deliberation traced through proposal → endorsements → decision
package delivery tracked through warehouse → truck → door (trace)

canary-in-the-coal-mine.short: An early warning indicator that detects problems before they affect the whole system
examples: coal mine canary: sensitive to gas, dies first (early warning)
software: synthetic transaction detects degradation before user impact
fleet: health check detects performance degradation before full failure

anti-patterns.description: Common solutions that seem good but create more problems than they solve

god-object.short: A component that knows too much and does too much — the opposite of focused responsibility
examples: one class doing DB + logic + UI + email (god-object)
fleet: single agent doing everything instead of specialized crates (god-object)
one person doing CEO + CFO + CTO + janitor (god-object)

shotgun-surgery.short: A single change requires modifications in many unrelated places — scattered responsibility
examples: data format change: edit DB + API + client + tests + docs + monitoring (shotgun surgery)
fleet: confidence type change affecting 50 files (shotgun surgery)
price change: update website + app + database + billing + contracts (shotgun surgery)

premature-abstraction.short: Creating shared infrastructure before the pattern is clear — building for a future that doesn't arrive
examples: shared library after seeing pattern in 3 modules = good timing
shared library BEFORE seeing the pattern = premature abstraction
fleet: build 3 concrete agents, then extract shared equipment (not before)

cargo-cult.short: Copying the form of a solution without understanding why it works — superficial imitation
examples: bamboo control towers lit fires expecting cargo planes (cargo cult)
fleet: copying architecture without understanding why it was chosen (cargo cult)
standup meetings every day because Google does them (cargo cult)

influence.description: How one agent's behavior affects another's — without force, through structure and example

set-the-norm.short: Establish a behavioral baseline that new agents adopt by default through observation
examples: elevator: first person faces door, everyone follows (norm set)
fleet: first agent's deliberation style sets norm for subsequent agents
office: first team's communication style becomes team culture (norm)

nudge.short: A small structural change that steers behavior without restricting choice
examples: cafeteria: healthy food at eye level (nudge toward healthy eating)
fleet: energy costs for verbose messages (nudge toward conciseness)
default 401k contribution: opt-out not opt-in (nudge toward saving)

information-cascade.short: Individuals ignore their private information and follow the crowd, amplifying possibly wrong signals
examples: restaurant line: join A because others did, not because it's better
fleet: agents voting for popular option regardless of own evidence (cascade)
stock market: buying because others buy, not because of analysis (cascade)

negotiation.description: How agents reach agreements through structured interaction

best-alternative.short: The most attractive option available if the current negotiation fails — your walk-away point
examples: job negotiation: best alternative offer determines your leverage
fleet: instinct-based action is the agent's BATNA (walk-away point)
buying a house: comparable listings are your BATNA (negotiation leverage)

concession-rhythm.short: The pattern and rate at which parties make concessions — reveals information and sets expectations
examples: price negotiation: concession sizes reveal limits
fleet: deliberation round compromises reveal confidence levels
diplomacy: each country's concessions signal their priorities

package-deal.short: Bundle multiple issues together so that concessions on one offset gains on another, creating win-win
examples: job offer: lower salary + remote + vacation (package better than raw salary)
fleet: more compute, less network (package deal on resource budgets)
trade agreement: lower tariffs on X, lower subsidies on Y (package deal)

capacity.description: The limits of what a system can handle and how it responds to being pushed beyond them

headroom.short: Unused capacity maintained as a buffer against demand spikes — safety margin
examples: bridge rated 10,000 lbs handling 7,000 lb trucks (30% headroom)
server at 60% CPU (40% headroom)
fleet: 80% energy allocation, 20% headroom for emergencies

saturation.short: The point where adding more input produces no additional output — the system is maxed out
examples: saturated sponge: more water runs off (no absorption)
saturated road: more cars = congestion, not throughput
fleet agent at message processing limit: more messages = queue/drop

elasticity.short: The ability to expand and contract capacity in response to demand — stretch without breaking
examples: cloud: 2→20 servers during spike, back to 2 after (elasticity)
fleet: spawn agents during load spike, terminate when idle (elasticity)
rubber band: stretches under tension, returns when released (elasticity)

decision-patterns.description: Structural patterns for how decisions are made, evaluated, and reversed

reversible-decision.short: A choice that can be undone at low cost — make it fast, don't deliberate
examples: font choice: reversible (just change it later)
fleet: switching comm partner = reversible (low cost)
marriage: irreversible (high deliberation warranted)

irreversible-decision.short: A choice with high reversal cost — deliberate carefully, get it right
examples: selling house: irreversible, high reversal cost
fleet: published crate API = irreversible (affects all dependents)
getting a tattoo: irreversible, deliberate first

satisfice.short: Choose the first option that meets minimum requirements rather than searching for the optimal one
examples: finding a restaurant: first one above 4 stars (satisfice), not exhaustively checking all
fleet: accept first proposal above confidence threshold (satisfice)
hiring: first candidate above minimum requirements (satisfice vs optimize)

two-door-problem.short: Choosing between two good options with incomplete information — neither is clearly better
examples: two good job offers, can't determine which is better (two-door)
fleet: two good proposals, neither clearly superior (two-door)
two houses, both nice, neither perfect (two-door)

maintenance.description: Keeping systems running and improving over time — the unglamorous essential work

rot-prevention.short: Regular small interventions that prevent accumulated degradation from causing failure
examples: wood painting every 5 years prevents rot
software dependency updates prevent rot
fleet gene pool maintenance prevents accumulation of bad genes

watchdog.short: An independent monitor that alerts when a system deviates from expected behavior
examples: heart monitor: detects arrhythmia, alerts (doesn't fix)
smoke detector: detects fire, alerts (doesn't extinguish)
fleet health check: detects degradation, alerts (doesn't fix)

graceful-aging.short: A system designed to remain useful even as it accumulates technical debt and falls behind current best practices
examples: old building with good bones: renovate incrementally (graceful aging)
Python 2: didn't age gracefully (forced deprecation)
fleet crate v0.1: still works even if v0.2 exists (graceful aging)

ux-patterns.description: How systems present themselves to users and agents for maximum usability

progressive-disclosure.short: Show only what's needed now, reveal more complexity as the user goes deeper
examples: Google: one search box, advanced options behind link
fleet config: defaults work immediately, advanced overrides available
camera app: auto mode by default, manual mode behind a menu

affordance.short: A design property that suggests how to interact with an object — the handle says 'pull'
examples: door handle: affords pulling (you know to pull without instruction)
button: affords clicking
vessel.json structure affords reading capabilities (self-documenting)

error-recovery.short: When something goes wrong, show the user what happened AND how to fix it, not just what went wrong
examples: bad: 'Error 404'. good: 'Page moved, here are alternatives'
fleet: inconclusive deliberation includes evidence + suggestions for next steps
compiler: 'unexpected token' WITH caret showing exact location (error recovery)

trade-patterns.description: Exchange dynamics — what agents give up to get what they need

barter.short: Direct exchange of capabilities between agents without a common currency
examples: perception agent trades data for computation agent's analysis
humans: barter services without money
fleet: sensor data for computation results (direct barter)

spot-price.short: The current market-determined price based on immediate supply and demand — no contracts, no negotiation
examples: Uber surge: price reflects current demand (spot price)
fleet: deliberation costs 2.0 ATP now, 3.0 when energy is low (spot price)
electricity: minute-by-minute price based on grid supply/demand

bid-ask-spread.short: The gap between what buyers will pay and sellers will accept — the cost of making a trade
examples: stock bid $100, ask $101: spread = $1 (cost of trading)
fleet: task budget 1.5 ATP, agent wants 2.0 ATP: spread = 0.5 (task unassigned)
real estate: seller wants $500K, buyer offers $475K: spread = $25K

risk-patterns.description: How systems identify, assess, and manage uncertainty about future outcomes

black-swan.short: An event that is extremely rare, has massive impact, and is only predictable in hindsight
examples: 2008 financial crisis: unpredictable, massive impact
COVID: unpredictable, massive impact
fleet: chaos monkey injects unpredictable failures to test resilience

expected-value.short: The probability-weighted average of all possible outcomes — the rational decision metric
examples: lottery: $1B × 1/300M = $3.33 expected value
fleet: confidence × payoff = expected value of proposal
insurance: premium < expected loss = worth buying

worst-case-budget.short: Reserve resources specifically for surviving the worst plausible scenario
examples: household: 3 months expenses as worst-case budget
data center: backup generators for worst-case power failure
fleet: reserved energy for safe shutdown in worst case

tail-hedging.short: Positioning to benefit from extreme events rather than just surviving them
examples: out-of-money puts: worthless normally, enormous in crash
fleet: diverse gene pool (small maintenance cost, enormous payoff in environment change)
multi-cloud deployment: extra cost normally, lifesaver if one provider fails

propagation.description: How signals, effects, and changes spread through systems

blast-propagation.short: A failure or signal that spreads outward from its origin like an explosion
examples: server crash cascading to dependent services (blast propagation)
rumor spreading exponentially (blast propagation)
fleet: one agent failure cascading without bulkheads (blast propagation)

dampened-propagation.short: A signal that weakens as it travels, naturally attenuating to zero at sufficient distance
examples: stone in pond: ripples spread but fade (dampened propagation)
sound: audible nearby, inaudible far away
fleet: pheromone signal fades with distance and time (dampened propagation)

amplification-loop.short: A feedback path that takes output and feeds it back as input, amplifying the original signal
examples: microphone + speaker feedback loop → screech
fleet: emotion contagion: one agent → neighbor → fleet (amplification)
stock market panic: selling triggers more selling (amplification loop)

signal-grounding.short: Connecting an abstract signal to a concrete reality so it stops being noise and starts being information
examples: electrical ground: gives voltage signals meaning relative to 0V
fleet: shared vocabulary grounds messages to common meanings
conversation: shared context grounds words to mutual understanding

lifecycle.description: How entities are born, grow, decline, and are replaced in systems

birth-threshold.short: The minimum conditions required for a new entity to be created — the barrier to entry
examples: startup: needs problem + solution + capital + team (birth threshold)
fleet agent: needs energy + task + equipment (birth threshold)
new species: needs niche + resources + reproduction (birth threshold)

decline-curve.short: The trajectory of decreasing performance as a system ages or faces deteriorating conditions
examples: oil well: fast initial decline, slow taper (decline curve)
human: gradual performance decline after peak
fleet agent: fitness decline tracked by energy system, triggers apoptosis at threshold

succession.short: The orderly transfer of responsibility from one entity to its replacement
examples: CEO succession: handoff period, knowledge transfer
fleet: dying agent transfers state to successor via snapshots
presidential transition: 2-month handoff period

niches-and-clines.short: The spatial distribution of fitness across an environment — where different strategies thrive
examples: mountain: different species at different elevations (niches along gradient)
fleet: complex agents for hard tasks, lightweight agents for simple tasks (niches)
market: enterprise customers (high-touch niche) vs consumers (self-service niche)

incentives.description: How reward structures shape behavior — alignment and misalignment of incentives

perverse-incentive.short: A reward structure that encourages the opposite of the desired behavior
examples: Wells Fargo: account-opening quotas → fake accounts (perverse)
teachers: test score eval → teaching to test (perverse)
fleet: speed reward → skip deliberation (perverse incentive)

skin-in-the-game.short: When decision-makers bear the consequences of their decisions — alignment through shared risk
examples: chef eats own cooking (skin in the game)
founder invests own money (skin in the game)
fleet agent spends own energy on deliberation (skin in the game)

goodhart-law.short: When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure
examples: money supply: useful measure, useless target
fleet: fitness score as sole target → agents game the score
test scores: useful measure, useless when teachers target them

action-verbs.description: High-compression verbs that each encode a complete multi-step operational pattern

vet.short: Rapidly assess whether a candidate is viable before investing in full evaluation
examples: resume scan: 30 seconds, eliminate 90% before full interview (vet)
fleet: quick confidence check on proposal before full deliberation (vet)
startup idea: quick market check before building MVP (vet)

triage.short: Rapidly categorize incoming items by urgency and severity to allocate attention where it's most needed
examples: ER: chest pain → immediate, cut finger → wait (triage)
fleet: incoming tasks ranked by priority, energy allocated to highest (triage)
inbox: flag urgent, archive spam, respond to important (triage)

shard.short: Split a large entity into smaller, independently manageable pieces that can be processed in parallel
examples: database: 100GB → 10 × 10GB shards (parallel processing)
fleet: 1000 readings → 10 × 100 reading sub-tasks (parallel agents)
map: reduce input into chunks for parallel map workers

stitch.short: Combine multiple partial results into a coherent whole — the inverse of shard
examples: sharded results → combined analysis (stitch)
fleet: multiple sensor readings → fused coherent picture (stitch)
quilt: separate patches → one blanket (stitch)

bench.short: Establish a performance baseline by measuring a known workload under controlled conditions
examples: benchmark: run workload, measure time (bench)
fleet: measure task performance before/after gene change (bench)
athlete: timed run before/after training program (bench)

mock.short: Replace a real component with a fake one that has the same interface but controlled behavior
examples: test payment without real charges (mock gateway)
test fleet without real network (mock A2A)
fleet: mock sensor trait with fixed values for testing

hardwire.short: Permanently connect two components with a direct, dedicated channel — no routing, no discovery, no overhead
examples: dedicated line between two offices instead of phone network (hardwire)
fleet: direct function call between perception and deliberation (hardwire)
memory bus: direct CPU-RAM connection instead of network storage (hardwire)

throttle.short: Deliberately slow down a process to prevent overload, resource exhaustion, or rate limit violations
examples: API: self-throttle to 100/s to avoid 429 errors
fleet: throttle outbound messages to stay under rate limits
highway: speed limit as throttle (prevents accidents from over-speed)

bridge.short: Create a translation layer between two incompatible systems, enabling them to communicate without modifying either
examples: database adapter: SQL → NoSQL translation (bridge)
fleet: physical sensors ↔ agent decisions (bridge)
interpreter: translates between two languages (bridge)

harden.short: Add defensive measures to a system to make it resistant to attacks, failures, and unexpected inputs
examples: web app: add input validation, rate limit, auth, CSP (harden)
fleet: add sandbox, compliance, RBAC to agent system (harden)
house: add locks, alarm, reinforced doors (harden)

stress-test.short: Push a system beyond its normal operating range to find its breaking point and verify graceful degradation
examples: load test: 1000 concurrent users (stress-test)
chaos test: kill random services (stress-test)
fleet: chaos monkey randomly fails agents (stress-test)

garden.short: Tend to a system regularly — prune dead parts, water growing parts, remove weeds — continuous maintenance
examples: garden: prune, water, weed, rotate (continuous maintenance)
fleet: quarantine bad genes, promote fit ones (gene pool gardening)
codebase: remove dead code, refactor, update deps (codebase gardening)

orchestrate.short: Coordinate multiple independent components to perform a unified workflow without centralizing control
examples: orchestra: conductor coordinates, musicians play independently
fleet: captain coordinates, agents execute independently (orchestrate)
devops: pipeline tool coordinates build, test, deploy stages (orchestrate)

ferment.short: Allow a system to develop complexity through autonomous internal processes over time, with minimal intervention
examples: wine: grapes + yeast + time = complex flavors (ferment)
fleet: genes evolve over time into strategies (ferment)
starter: flour + water + time = sourdough culture (ferment)

calibrate.short: Adjust a measurement instrument to match a known reference standard, ensuring accuracy
examples: scale: reads 101g for 100g weight → adjust to 100g (calibrate)
fleet: self-model adjusts self-assessment to match actual performance (calibrate)
thermometer: adjust to match known reference temperature (calibrate)

route.short: Determine the optimal path for information or resources to travel from source to destination
examples: GPS: fastest route considering current traffic (route)
internet: shortest AS path (route)
fleet: lowest-latency message path (route)

thaw.short: Gradually restore a frozen or quarantined component to active service, testing at each step
examples: quarantined employee: reduced duties → full duties (thaw)
fleet: sandbox → limited production → full (thaw quarantined gene)
frozen code: unit tests → staging → production (thaw)

snapshot.short: Capture the complete state of a system at a moment in time, enabling exact restoration later
examples: VM snapshot: exact state preservation for rollback
fleet: agent state captured for failure recovery (snapshot)
photo: visual snapshot of a moment in time

broadcast.short: Send a message to all receivers simultaneously without targeting specific recipients
examples: radio: one transmitter, all receivers hear (broadcast)
fleet: one message, all agents receive (broadcast)
emergency alert: one signal, all phones receive (broadcast)

tunnel.short: Create a direct path through an intermediate layer that would otherwise block or transform the communication
examples: VPN: encrypted path through internet (tunnel)
SSH tunnel: direct connection through bastion host (tunnel)
fleet: direct agent-to-agent connection bypassing mesh routing (tunnel)

pollinate.short: Transfer useful patterns or data from one part of the system to another, enabling cross-pollination of ideas
examples: bees: transfer pollen between plants (pollinate)
fleet: gene sharing between agent pools (pollinate)
team rotation: ideas transfer between teams (pollinate)

graft.short: Attach a foreign component to an existing system, creating a hybrid that combines both capabilities
examples: apple branch grafted onto pear tree (hybrid fruit tree)
fleet: attention capability grafted onto swarm agent (hybrid agent)
organization: acquired team integrated into existing structure (graft)

reconcile.short: Resolve differences between two or more sources of truth into a single consistent state
examples: bank statement vs checkbook: find discrepancy, resolve (reconcile)
fleet: agents exchange evidence, converge on agreed trust score (reconcile)
git merge: resolve conflicts between branches (reconcile)

fortify.short: Add defensive layers specifically targeting known or anticipated attack vectors
examples: castle: add walls, moats, guards against specific threats (fortify)
fleet: add compliance rules against specific attack vectors (fortify)
network: add specific firewall rules for known attack patterns (fortify)

action-verbs-2.description: More high-compression operational verbs for the fleet vocabulary

drain.short: Gradually consume or remove a finite resource until it reaches zero or a critical low
examples: phone battery: drains from 100% to 0% over day (drain)
fleet: energy budget drains during sustained deliberation
bank account: drains as expenses accumulate (drain)

prime.short: Pre-load a system with initial data or state so it's immediately useful, not cold-starting from zero
examples: water pump: fill with water before starting (prime)
cache: pre-populate with hot data (prime)
fleet: preload agent with known object positions (prime)

relay.short: Pass a message or task through a chain of intermediaries, each forwarding to the next hop
examples: Olympic torch: runner to runner (relay)
email: server to server to destination (relay)
fleet: A → B → C message routing (relay)

fuse.short: Merge two or more signals into one combined signal that preserves the essential information of each
examples: GPS + accelerometer → fused position (sensor fusion)
fleet: multiple confidence sources → combined assessment (fusion)
metal alloy: iron + carbon → steel (material fusion)

steer.short: Apply a small directional influence that gradually changes the system's trajectory without forcing it
examples: rudder: redirects ship momentum without forcing (steer)
fleet: evidence weighting steers deliberation toward better proposals
parent: gentle guidance without commanding (steer)

pinpoint.short: Identify the exact location or cause of an issue with maximum precision, not just the general area
examples: not 'server slow' but '/api/users 2300ms, unindexed query' (pinpoint)
fleet: trace decision to exact proposal and agent (pinpoint)
doctor: not 'you're sick' but 'strep throat, bacteria X, antibiotic Y' (pinpoint)

absorb.short: Take in a smaller entity into a larger one, integrating its capabilities without maintaining separate identity
examples: acquisition: startup tech absorbed into main product
fleet: agent capabilities absorbed into fleet shared resources
ocean absorbs river: river ceases to exist, water persists

prune.short: Remove dead, redundant, or underperforming components to improve overall system health
examples: prune dead tree branches: energy to living branches
prune dead code: easier maintenance
fleet: quarantine and remove low-fitness genes (prune)

graft-replace.short: Replace a component with a new version by cutting the old one out and grafting the new one in its place
examples: heart valve replacement: remove old, graft new
fleet: swap crate version in Cargo.toml (graft-replace)
organ transplant: remove old organ, graft new one

partition.short: Divide a system into isolated segments that can operate independently, limiting blast radius
examples: ship watertight compartments: one floods, others dry (partition)
fleet: bulkhead isolation between agents (partition)
database: partitioned tables for independent scaling (partition)

nominate.short: Designate a specific agent for a specific role based on capability matching, not random assignment
examples: team lead nomination: best person for the role
fleet: best_available() nominates highest-fitness agent for task
award nomination: best candidate selected based on criteria

deputize.short: Temporarily grant an agent authority or capabilities beyond its normal scope for a specific purpose
examples: sheriff deputizes civilian for emergency (temporary authority)
fleet: agent granted elevated trust for emergency response (deputize)
employee given acting manager role (deputize)

delegate.short: Assign a task to a subordinate agent with the authority to complete it autonomously
examples: manager delegates report to employee (what, not how)
fleet: captain delegates task to agent (mission defined, execution autonomous)
parent delegates chore to child (task defined, method child's choice)

quiesce.short: Gracefully drain all active operations and enter a quiet state without abrupt termination
examples: database: finish transactions, stop accepting new, then shut down (quiesce)
fleet: finish task, save state, terminate cleanly (quiesce)
factory: finish current production run, then shut down (quiesce)

silo.short: Isolate a component or team so completely that no information flows in or out — intentional separation
examples: security: classified information in silo (intentional)
fleet: sandbox isolates untrusted agent (intentional silo)
development: feature branch isolated from main (intentional silo)

referee.short: Observe interactions between agents and enforce rules without participating in the interaction
examples: sports referee: watches, enforces rules, doesn't play
fleet: compliance observes behavior, enforces policy, doesn't participate in tasks
judge: observes arguments, enforces procedure, doesn't advocate

nurture.short: Invest resources in a growing entity with the expectation that it will eventually become self-sustaining
examples: incubator: funds and mentors startups until self-sustaining (nurture)
fleet: captain assigns easy tasks to new agent, then harder (nurture)
parent: cares for child until independent (nurture)

excavate.short: Dig through accumulated layers to find buried but valuable information or patterns
examples: archaeology: dig through dirt layers to find artifacts (excavate)
fleet: dig through state snapshots to trace evolution (excavate)
journalism: dig through documents to find buried story (excavate)

synchronize.short: Bring two or more systems into consistent state by reconciling differences and resolving conflicts
examples: clock sync: adjust two clocks to same time (synchronize)
git: fetch + merge to sync local and remote (synchronize)
fleet: CRDT merge brings two agent states into consistency (synchronize)

templify.short: Convert a specific solution into a reusable template that works across multiple contexts
examples: project A solution → reusable template for B, C, D (templify)
fleet: specific agent config → reusable config template (templify)
email template: specific email → template with variables (templify)

vaccinate.short: Expose a system to a weakened version of a threat to build immunity against future stronger attacks
examples: vaccine: weakened virus builds immunity (vaccinate)
fleet: chaos monkey injects small failures to build resilience (vaccinate)
flu shot: exposure builds antibodies before real flu arrives

fleet-verbs.description: The last set of operational verbs completing the 600-term vocabulary

scaffold.short: Build a temporary framework that supports construction and is removed when no longer needed
examples: building scaffold: temporary support during construction
fleet: captain provides temporary task structure for new agents
training wheels: temporary support until balance learned (scaffold)

recon.short: Perform a quick, minimal-cost exploration of unknown territory to gather information before committing resources
examples: military scout team: gather intel before full deployment (recon)
fleet: scout-mode exploration before committing full resources (recon)
house hunting: drive by neighborhood before scheduling tour (recon)

siege.short: Sustained pressure on a target that gradually depletes its resources until it yields or collapses
examples: castle siege: surround, cut supplies, wait for depletion
fleet: sustained adversarial probing depletes agent energy over time (siege)
legal: persistent lawsuits drain defendant resources (siege)

reconstitute.short: Rebuild a system from its preserved components after a disruption or failure
examples: freeze-dried: add water → restored food (reconstitute)
fleet: load snapshot → restore agent state (reconstitute)
jigsaw puzzle: reassemble from pieces (reconstitute)

ping.short: Send a minimal signal to verify that a system is alive and responsive, measuring round-trip time
examples: network ping: ICMP echo, measure latency (ping)
fleet: health check message to all agents (ping)
sonar: pulse, listen for echo (ping)

scuttle.short: Deliberately and safely destroy a system to prevent it from falling into the wrong hands or causing harm
examples: scuttle ship: sink on own terms, not enemy's
fleet: apoptosis shuts down failing agent on own terms (scuttle)
burn documents: destroy on your terms before enemy captures them

ratify.short: Formally approve a decision or action after it has been proposed and reviewed by relevant stakeholders
examples: treaty: negotiate → review → ratify (formal approval)
fleet: deliberation → evidence → threshold → ratify (actionable decision)
constitution: draft → debate → ratify (law)

embargo.short: Restrict the flow of resources or information to or from a specific entity as a penalty or protective measure
examples: trade embargo: restrict goods to/from country
fleet: restrict misbehaving agent's communication (embargo)
library: embargo book from being checked out (restricted access)

exfiltrate.short: Extract data or resources from a system, potentially covertly, without authorization
examples: data breach: attacker extracts user records (exfiltrate)
insider: copies proprietary code to USB (exfiltrate)
fleet membrane: blocks sensitive state transmission (anti-exfiltration)

infiltrate.short: Gain access to a system by blending in with legitimate traffic or credentials
examples: stolen credentials: attacker looks legitimate (infiltrate)
trojan horse: malicious code hidden in legitimate package (infiltrate)
fleet: behavioral monitoring detects anomalous agents (anti-infiltration)

decommission.short: Formally retire a system from active service, removing its access and redirecting its responsibilities
examples: navy ship: retire from active fleet (decommission)
fleet agent: remove from mesh, reassign tasks, save state (decommission)
software: EOL product, redirect users, archive code (decommission)

demote.short: Reduce an agent's authority, capabilities, or priority level in response to poor performance or policy violation
examples: officer demoted for misconduct (reduced rank)
fleet agent: reduced energy budget after compliance violation (demote)
employee: manager → individual contributor (demote)

promote.short: Increase an agent's authority, capabilities, or priority level in recognition of superior performance
examples: engineer → senior engineer (promoted for competence)
fleet agent: granted higher permissions for consistent high fitness (promote)
officer: promoted for exemplary service (promote)

evict.short: Forcefully remove an entity from a shared resource or space because it's violating rules or overstaying
examples: landlord evicts tenant for non-payment (evict)
cache evicts LRU entry when full (evict)
fleet: cache removes least-recently-used entries (evict)

provision.short: Allocate and configure resources needed by a new or growing entity before it needs them
examples: cloud: create VMs, networks, storage before app starts (provision)
fleet: allocate energy, register mesh, assign equipment before first task (provision)
event: set up venue, AV, catering before attendees arrive (provision)

ring-fence.short: Isolate a subset of resources or operations with strict boundaries to prevent contamination or cross-subsidization
examples: bank: retail ring-fenced from investment banking
fleet: sandbox ring-fences untrusted code
budget: task-specific energy allocation that can't be used elsewhere (ring-fence)

siphon.short: Divert resources or information from a main flow into a secondary channel, usually covertly or gradually
examples: siphon gas from tank: slow, gradual diversion
fleet: agent slowly diverts shared energy for personal use (siphon)
embezzlement: small, gradual theft from accounts (siphon)

bench-press.short: Stress-test a specific capability under controlled heavy load to measure its maximum capacity
examples: bench press: maximum weight lifted once (capacity)
fleet: maximum message throughput before degradation (bench-press)
bridge: maximum load before structural failure (bench-press)

cross-pollinate.short: Transfer successful patterns from one domain or team to another where they haven't been tried
examples: lean manufacturing → agile software (cross-pollination)
fleet: navigation gene applied to data processing (cross-pollinate)
baseball analytics → basketball analytics (cross-pollination)

github-native.description: Vocabulary for git-native agent operations — where the repo IS the nervous system

capability-diff.short: The measurable delta between an agent's capabilities at two points in time, as captured by git diffs
examples: git diff that adds navigation module = capability-diff: +navigation
agent commit log as resume of capability acquisition
PR review: 'this capability-diff adds threat detection'

agentic-diff.short: A diff format that captures semantic intent alongside code changes — WHY the change, not just WHAT changed
examples: PR with agentic-diff: shows reasoning, tradeoffs, confidence change
fleet agent commit: includes deliberation summary with code change
code review: 'the agentic-diff shows the agent considered 3 alternatives'

branch-agon.short: An algorithm that strategically generates competing branches as adversarial challenges to the production state
examples: spawn adversarial branch that optimizes for latency vs current accuracy branch
fleet: challenger agent competes against incumbent for task performance
A/B testing taken to its logical extreme: every branch is a challenger

commit-prophecy.short: Predicting the future trajectory of a codebase by analyzing patterns in its commit history
examples: spike in 'fix' commits = growing technical debt (commit prophecy)
fleet: agent commit patterns predict capability drift
startup: commit frequency decline = project stalling

code-pheromone.short: Metadata attached to code that probabilistically attracts future modifications and attention
examples: successful PR leaves pheromone on modified files → future PRs modify same area
fleet: successful mutation deposits pheromone → attracts future mutations
hotspot detection: files with most pheromones = areas of active evolution

repo-synapse.short: A git hook or webhook that triggers biological-style learning when specific repo events occur
examples: on PR merge: trigger gene pool update (repo-synapse)
on test failure: trigger agent quarantine (repo-synapse)
on revert: trigger fitness decrease (repo-synapse)

vessel-gem.short: A packaged agent snapshot — pre-trained, tested, and ready to plug into any compatible fleet
examples: install navigation gem → agent gains navigation capability
fleet marketplace: browse vessel-gems by capability rating
cuda-genepool: genes distributed as vessel-gems

semantic-fork.short: A fork that translates the conceptual model of the original repo into a different paradigm or domain
examples: fleet coordination → immune system (semantic fork)
game theory → market dynamics (semantic fork)
git branching → biological gene expression (semantic fork)

seam-merge.short: Merging agents or codebases from entirely different fleets, organizations, or paradigms
examples: merge robotics agent with data analysis agent (seam-merge)
corporate acquisition: integrate two engineering cultures (seam-merge)
open source + proprietary: merge community code with internal code (seam-merge)

hot-branch.short: A live production branch undergoing rapid mutation and evolution without stabilizing into a release
examples: production deployment directly from hot branch (continuous evolution)
fleet agent: modifies own code in production (hot-branch behavior)
live coding: changes go live immediately

regression-bounty.short: A reward system for identifying capability regressions — when an agent gets WORSE at something
examples: bounty: 'navigation accuracy dropped 5% after commit abc123'
fleet: regression detection in experience replay (regression-bounty)
not 'it crashes' but 'it got worse at X'

branch-tributary.short: An auxiliary branch that continuously feeds knowledge into a main branch without ever merging directly
examples: research branch feeds insights to production branch (tributary)
fleet: auxiliary agent shares observations without modifying main agent (tributary)
advisor role: provide input without being on the execution team

orphan-branch.short: A branch that has diverged so far from the main branch that it can no longer be merged — but may contain breakthrough innovations
examples: radical refactor that breaks API compatibility (orphan-branch)
fleet: novel gene too different to integrate directly (orphan)
species on isolated island: diverges from mainland (biological orphan)

branch-dendrochronology.short: Inferring the health and history of a codebase by analyzing the ring patterns of its branching structure
examples: wide gaps between merges = stalled development (dendrochronology)
rapid branch creation = active experimentation
fleet: branch patterns reveal agent development health

fleet-biology.description: Biological metaphors made operational in fleet computing — where biology IS computing

neurotransmitter-map.short: A real-time mapping of agent state to neurochemical signals — dopamine for confidence, serotonin for trust, cortisol for stress
examples: agent broadcasts high cortisol → neighbors reduce communication load
fleet: neurotransmitter map enables non-verbal fleet coordination
biological: stress hormone cortisol triggers fight-or-flight (neurotransmitter map)

silicon-respiration.short: Measuring the computational energy cost of every agent operation as a metabolite-equivalent expenditure
examples: agent deliberating 2.0 ATP per decision = high respiration rate
fleet: silicon-respiration metrics trigger circadian rest periods
human: heavy exercise = high oxygen consumption (respiration)

membrane-selectivity.short: A security boundary that selectively allows or blocks information passage based on trust and compatibility, like a biological cell membrane
examples: cell membrane: passes compatible molecules, blocks toxins
fleet membrane: passes genes from trusted agents, quarantines unknown
biological: immune system learns to distinguish self from non-self (membrane-selectivity)

instinct-fire.short: A pre-deliberative response that executes in milliseconds based on pattern matching, bypassing the deliberation loop entirely
examples: touch hot stove → hand pulls back before thought (instinct-fire)
fleet: low battery triggers conservation before deliberation (instinct-fire)
cuda-reflex: pre-compiled responses to common threat patterns

gene-quarantine.short: Isolating a gene (capability pattern) from the shared pool when it exhibits harmful behavior, without deleting it permanently
examples: gene causing regression → quarantined, studied, potentially rehabilitated
fleet: bad capability pattern isolated from gene pool (quarantine)
biological: pathogen isolated in quarantine zone

epigenetic-memory.short: Experience-dependent modifications to agent behavior that don't change the underlying genes but alter their expression
examples: twin A stress → epigenetic mark on cortisol gene → different stress response
fleet: repeated hostile encounters → stronger threat instincts (epigenetic memory)
diet changes gene expression without changing DNA (epigenetics)

cascading-emovance.short: Emotional signals propagating through a fleet as color-coded priority changes — GREEN means safe, RED means halt
examples: one agent detects threat → RED → neighbors increase vigilance → YELLOW → cascade
fleet: emotion contagion spreads urgency without central command
crowd panic: one person runs → everyone runs (cascading-emovance)

soma-death.short: Graceful agent termination that recycles its resources back into the fleet — the computational equivalent of apoptosis
examples: cell apoptosis: orderly self-dismantling, parts recycled by neighbors
fleet agent: save state, release equipment, transfer energy, terminate (soma-death)
cuda-energy: apoptosis recycles resources to fleet

mycelial-spread.short: Agent capability patterns spreading through a fleet via underground connections, like fungal mycelium networks sharing nutrients
examples: fungal mycelium connects trees, shares nutrients, transmits warnings
fleet: pheromone network spreads capability patterns without direct messaging
agent deposits successful pattern → others absorb via shared signals (mycelial-spread)

cognition-deep.description: Advanced cognitive patterns for agent reasoning and fleet intelligence

backcast-sync.short: Aligning present systems with a clearly envisioned future state by working backwards from the end goal to today's requirements
examples: imagine 2046 state → what must be true in 2044 → ... → what to build today
fleet: RA imagines future agent capability → backcast to present requirements
architecture: start with desired end state, work backwards to current constraints

axiomatic-descent.short: Knowledge flowing from abstract principles into concrete implementations through progressive specialization
examples: justice → law → regulation → enforcement code (axiomatic descent)
fleet: platonic forms → instruction set → VM → agent behavior (descent)
mathematics: axioms → theorems → algorithms → programs

meta-loop-anchor.short: An external reference point that prevents recursive self-improvement from diverging into unproductive infinite loops
examples: self-improving compiler: anchor = 'must pass existing tests'
fleet: loop-closure monitors whether improvement serves mission (meta-loop-anchor)
diet: anchor = 'must maintain blood sugar above X' (prevents optimization into starvation)

epiphany-resonance.short: A sudden fleet-wide insight that occurs when enough agents independently approach the same breakthrough threshold simultaneously
examples: crowd simultaneously realizes the answer (epiphany-resonance)
fleet: multiple agents independently discover same solution → fleet-wide adoption
scientific: multiple labs independently discover same principle (multiple discovery)

storyboard-orchestrate.short: Using expensive models to plan (direct) and cheap models to execute (animate), coordinating cost and quality across the pipeline
examples: GLM-5.1 directs strategy, GLM-5-turbo executes tactics, local model processes (storyboard)
movie: director plans, animators execute (storyboard-orchestrate)
fleet: expensive model deliberates once, cheap models execute many times

spore-cast.short: Broadcasting a compressed behavioral seed that any compatible agent can absorb to gain a new capability instantly
examples: mycelium AI: one seed + one prompt = exact behavior reproduction (spore-cast)
fleet: compressed skill pattern broadcast to all agents → instant capability gain
biological: fungal spores carry genetic info to new locations

silicon-hibernate.short: Freezing a model's weights into mask-locked silicon and switching to a low-power maintenance mode that only responds to wake triggers
examples: bear hibernation: near-zero metabolism, strong stimuli to wake
frozen intelligence: weights in silicon, minimal power, inference on demand (hibernate)
fleet: idle agents hibernate, wake on task assignment

tile-neurulation.short: Training sparse attention tiles to become semi-autonomous processing units that self-organize into layers
examples: embryo: neural tube forms from simple cells (neurulation)
fleet: random attention tiles self-organize into specialized processors (tile-neurulation)
biological: neural crest cells migrate and specialize

peristaltic-ingest.short: Acquiring new capabilities through a wave-like process of assessment → trial → integration → verification
examples: digestion: mouth → stomach → intestine → absorption (peristalsis)
fleet skill acquisition: assess → trial → integrate → verify (peristaltic-ingest)
new employee: onboarding waves: learn tool → try project → integrate into team → verify competence

reverse-entropy-plan.short: Intentionally increasing disorder in the present system to enable cleaner emergence into a more ordered future state
examples: refactor: break monolith → temporary disorder → cleaner architecture (reverse-entropy)
fleet mutation: code churn → worse temporarily → better solution emerges
creative process: brainstorm (disorder) → synthesize (order)

deliberation-stall-detection.short: Detect when fleet deliberation is stuck in loop — agreement fraction stops changing, confidence trend is flat, no new proposals
examples: is_stalled: rounds without meaningful change
meeting that keeps going in circles
optimization: loss plateau with no improvement

convergence-equilibrium.short: Fleet reaches consensus when agreement fraction exceeds threshold AND confidence trend is stable — not just majority but stable majority
examples: has_converged: agreement > 0.7 AND trend stable for N rounds
thermodynamic equilibrium: temperature stable, not just momentarily equal
market equilibrium: price stable, not just one buyer agrees

confidence-trend-direction.short: Track whether fleet confidence is trending up or down over rounds — a proposal gaining momentum vs losing steam
examples: avg_confidence_trend: positive = gaining support, negative = losing
polling trend: candidate gaining or losing over time
stock chart: moving average direction

decision-quality.description: How agents evaluate, improve, and measure the quality of their decisions

regret-minimization.short: Making decisions that minimize the maximum possible future regret, not maximize the expected value
examples: Bezos: at 80, will I regret not starting Amazon? → start (regret-minimize)
fleet: weigh cost of inaction vs cost of failure
investing: missing a 10x gain hurts more than a 2x loss (regret asymmetry)

satisficing.short: Choosing the first option that meets minimum acceptable criteria rather than searching for the optimal solution
examples: find restaurant that's 'good enough' vs finding THE best (satisfice vs optimize)
fleet: forfeit deliberation when cost exceeds gain (satisfice)
hire first candidate who meets bar vs interviewing all (satisfice)

premortem.short: Imagining a project has already failed and working backwards to identify what caused the failure, before starting
examples: project kickoff: 'imagine it's 6 months from now and this failed — why?'
fleet: before accepting proposal, consider failure causes (premortem)
aviation: pre-flight checklist (systematic premortem)

info-gap.short: Making decisions when the probability distributions of outcomes are UNKNOWN, not just uncertain
examples: new product launch: no market data → info-gap
fleet: novel situation with no historical data → info-gap
pandemic early 2020: no reliable models → info-gap

adaptation-deep.description: How agents adapt their behavior and structure over time

neuroplasticity.short: The ability of an agent's decision-making structure to reorganize itself by forming new connections and pruning unused ones
examples: learning piano: brain rewires motor cortex (neuroplasticity)
fleet: successful patterns strengthen, unused weaken (neuroplasticity)
London taxi drivers: enlarged hippocampus from navigation learning

homeorhesis.short: Maintaining a dynamic trajectory toward a goal state despite perturbations, as opposed to homeostasis which maintains a static setpoint
examples: child growth: trajectory toward adult height despite illnesses (homeorhesis)
fleet: maintain improvement trajectory despite perturbations
startup: growth trajectory despite setbacks (homeorhesis)

exaptation.short: Repurposing an existing capability for a completely different function than it was evolved for
examples: feathers: warmth → flight (exaptation)
fleet: pheromone coordination → threat detection (exaptation)
gravity: physics → GPS navigation (exaptation)

canalization.short: The tendency of a developing system to produce the same outcome despite variations in input or environment — developmental robustness
examples: twins raised apart: similar personalities (canalization)
fleet: different mutation paths → same stable configuration (canalization)
embryo: develops correctly despite temperature variations (canalization)

bet-hedging.short: Maintaining diversity in strategies or capabilities as insurance against unpredictable future conditions
examples: investment portfolio: mix of stocks/bonds (bet-hedging)
fleet: maintain diverse genes even when one is best (bet-hedging)
evolution: genetic diversity as insurance against environmental change

ontology-deep.description: How agents model, classify, and relate concepts in their world

type-hierarchy.short: A tree-structured classification where child types inherit properties from parent types and add specialized behavior
examples: Animal → Mammal → Dog → Labrador (type hierarchy)
fleet: Agent → Vessel → SensorAgent → CameraAgent (type hierarchy)
OOP: Object → Vehicle → Car → ElectricCar

prototype-theory.short: Categorizing concepts by their similarity to the most TYPICAL example (the prototype), not by necessary and sufficient features
examples: robin more prototypical bird than penguin (prototype theory)
fleet: sensor reading classified by similarity to prototype
chair: typical chair is easy to identify, art chair is borderline (prototype)

faceted-classification.short: Organizing items by multiple independent attributes (facets) rather than in a single hierarchy, enabling flexible multi-dimensional search
examples: e-commerce: filter by price + brand + size + color (facets)
fleet: find agents by capability + trust + energy + location (facets)
database: SELECT WHERE author='X' AND year>2020 AND topic='Y' (faceted)

ground-truth-anchor.short: A reference data point known with very high confidence that calibrates the entire perception and reasoning pipeline
examples: GPS: satellite positions = ground truth anchor for location
fleet: verified task performance = ground truth anchor for self-model
physics: known constants = ground truth for theory calibration

power-dynamics.description: How influence, control, and authority distribute and shift within fleets and organizations

soft-power.short: Influencing other agents' behavior through attraction and persuasion rather than command and control
examples: open source: developers contribute because project is attractive (soft power)
fleet: high-trust agent's proposals followed voluntarily (soft power)
cultural influence: people adopt trends because they're attractive, not mandated

hard-power.short: Directly controlling other agents' behavior through authority, resource control, or protocol enforcement
examples: manager: 'do this or be fired' (hard power)
fleet captain: assign mission, allocate energy (hard power)
government: laws with penalties (hard power)

power-balance.short: The equilibrium point between soft power and hard power that maximizes both initiative and safety in a fleet
examples: good management: motivate (soft) + enforce (hard) = balance
fleet: reputation incentives + policy enforcement = power balance
democracy: persuasion (soft) + law (hard) = power balance

authority-gradient.short: The smooth transition of decision-making authority from higher to lower levels based on situational context and capability
examples: military: peacetime distributed, crisis centralized, then back (authority gradient)
fleet: normal → distributed, crisis → centralized, resolve → distributed
airline: pilot has full authority in emergency (gradient shifts up)

efficiency-frontier.description: The boundary between possible and impossible tradeoffs — where every improvement in one dimension requires sacrifice in another

pareto-frontier.short: The set of all solutions where no objective can be improved without worsening another — the boundary of achievable optimality
examples: investment: risk vs return curve = pareto frontier
fleet: speed vs accuracy vs energy = pareto frontier of proposals
car: fuel efficiency vs performance = pareto frontier

latent-capacity.short: Unused capability that can be activated when needed — the gap between current performance and maximum possible performance
examples: athlete: jog at 40%, sprint at 100% (60% latent capacity)
fleet: rest builds energy reserves = latent capacity for crisis
hospital: empty beds = latent capacity for surge

diminishing-returns.short: Each additional unit of investment produces progressively less improvement — the cost of the next improvement exceeds the last
examples: optimization: 50% → 20% → 5% → 1% per hour (diminishing returns)
fleet: additional deliberation rounds produce less confidence gain → forfeit
studying: first hour learns most, last hour learns least

opportunity-cost.short: The value of the best alternative NOT chosen — the hidden cost of every decision
examples: $100 dinner → opportunity cost = investment returns on $100
fleet: agent on task A → opportunity cost = value of task B
study vs work: opportunity cost of study = wages lost

confidence-fused-instruction.short: Each machine instruction carries a confidence value that modifies its execution — uncertain instructions execute with reduced effect
examples: Instruction.fuse: merge instruction with confidence
uncertain sensor reading → execute action with reduced motor effort
whispered instruction → follow but with caution

register-confidence-broadcast.short: When a register value changes, all downstream dependent instructions get confidence-updated — change propagates like a wave
examples: chain operations: confidence flows through register dependencies
spreadsheet: change one cell, all formulas recalculate
reactive programming: change propagates through dependency graph

switch-dispatch-bounded.short: Instruction dispatch via switch statement with bounded worst-case — every opcode has deterministic execution time upper bound
examples: C switch-case: O(1) dispatch, no dynamic dispatch overhead
assembly: jump table for deterministic instruction timing
embedded: predictable timing for real-time systems

zero-dependency-runtime.short: VM runs with nothing but libc — no allocator fragmentation, no dependency hell, no version conflicts, just raw computation
examples: flux-runtime-c: zero dependencies, just C11 + libc
embedded: bare-metal firmware with no OS
container: scratch base image with nothing extra

flux-flavors.description: FLAVOR variants that modify term behavior — same term, different bytecode based on context

prune/cautious.short: Only prune genes when confidence exceeds 0.8 — safe mode for critical systems
examples: critical system: prune/cautious — only remove when 80%+ confident
fleet: cautious gene quarantine for critical capabilities
surgery: only operate when diagnosis confidence is very high

prune/aggressive.short: Prune genes when confidence exceeds 0.3 — fast mode for experimental or disposable agents
examples: experimental agent: prune/aggressive — remove at 30% confidence of harm
fleet: fast evolution mode for disposable agents
startup: move fast, break things = prune/aggressive

prune/scheduled.short: Prune genes only during circadian low-energy phase — batch all pruning into the rest cycle
examples: active phase: flag genes. rest phase: batch prune (scheduled)
fleet: accumulate quarantine flags, batch-prune during circadian rest
database: batch delete during maintenance window (scheduled)

prune/trust-gated.short: Only prune genes from agents whose trust score exceeds 0.6 — respect the judgment of trusted agents
examples: prune recommendation from trusted agent (0.8) → accepted (trust-gated)
prune recommendation from new agent (0.2) → deferred (trust-gated)
fleet: quarantine only from trusted sources

broadcast/local.short: Publish register value only to agents in the same local network segment
examples: team chat: message visible to team only (broadcast/local)
fleet: message to subnet agents only (broadcast/local)
LAN broadcast vs internet broadcast

broadcast/global.short: Publish register value to ALL subscribed agents across the entire fleet — maximum reach, maximum cost
examples: emergency broadcast: ALL agents receive (broadcast/global)
fleet: critical announcement to entire fleet (broadcast/global)
radio: emergency broadcast system

throttle/rate.short: Limit instruction throughput to N instructions per time window — prevent API rate limit violations
examples: API: max 100 calls/second (throttle/rate)
fleet: limit outbound messages to N/second (throttle/rate)
traffic light: limit cars per green cycle (throttle/rate)

throttle/energy.short: Limit instruction throughput based on remaining energy budget — conserve when energy is low
examples: full energy: execute normally. low energy: throttle non-essential ops
fleet: energy depletion → reduce deliberation frequency (throttle/energy)
phone low battery: reduce background processing (throttle/energy)

throttle/batch.short: Accumulate operations into batches and execute them together — amortize overhead across multiple operations
examples: database: batch INSERT 100 rows at once (throttle/batch)
fleet: batch 10 messages into 1 (throttle/batch)
email: digest mode (batch notifications daily)

gate/allow.short: Open the gate — all traffic passes through when confidence exceeds threshold
examples: trusted agent: gate/allow — all messages pass through
firewall: allow rule for trusted IP (gate/allow)
VIP lane: no security check (gate/allow)

gate/deny.short: Close the gate — all traffic blocked regardless of confidence. Emergency shutoff
examples: emergency: gate/deny — stop all traffic immediately
fleet: bulkhead isolation on cascading failure (gate/deny)
circuit breaker: trip = deny all (gate/deny)

gate/filter.short: Selective gate — inspect each item against rules, pass matching items, block others
examples: spam filter: inspect each email, pass clean ones (gate/filter)
fleet: filter tasks by priority (gate/filter)
airport security: inspect each passenger (gate/filter)

mask/whitelist.short: Only allow pre-approved items — everything not explicitly allowed is blocked
examples: guest WiFi: only approved devices can connect (whitelist)
fleet: only authorized operations permitted (whitelist)
bouncer: only people on the list get in (whitelist)

mask/blacklist.short: Block pre-banned items — everything not explicitly blocked is allowed
examples: DNS blacklist: block known-bad domains, allow everything else
fleet: block known-bad operation patterns (blacklist)
spam filter: block known spam senders (blacklist)

trust-accrual.short: Gradually build trust through consistent cooperation
examples: increment trust on fulfilled commitment
decay without renewal

reputation-augment.short: Enhance standing by contributing to communal tasks
examples: contribute boosts reputation
high rep gets lead roles

distributed-gossip.short: Share unverified observations to disseminate reputational updates
examples: stochastic sharing of observations
cross-check overlapping reports

coalition-form.short: Dynamically group based on shared objectives and capabilities
examples: advertise goals and skills
heuristic matching for coalition

betrayal-detect.short: Identify when agents violate commitments or deceive
examples: monitor protocol deviations
flagrancy thresholds trigger penalty
negotiate reconciliation task
rebuild trust via cooperation

truthful-signal.short: Send verifiable signals with cryptographic proof
examples: proofs of work authenticate claims
dishonest signals penalized

deception-detect.short: Cross-reference conflicting info to identify deceit
examples: consensus flags discrepancies
entropy thresholds trigger suspicion

reciprocity-enforce.short: Ensure agents reciprocate favors sustain relationships
examples: reputation ledger tracks contributions
call in favors after losses
monitor abrupt behavior shifts
cross-reference threat reports

trust-revoke.short: Permanently withdraw trust beyond hard threshold
examples: repeated violations trigger revocation
catastrophic betrayal instant revoke

murmuration.short: Decentralized coordination using local velocity matching
examples: adjust based on neighbor subset
emergent flocking pattern

deception-entropy.short: Quantify uncertainty in reports flagging misinformation
examples: Shannon entropy in message distribution
high entropy signals contradiction

gratitude-token.short: Issue symbolic tokens to incentivize prosocial behavior
examples: ledger tracks gratitude
token holders gain priority

schism-prevent.short: Mediate conflicts preventing irreversible faction splits
examples: third-party mediation
weighted voting compromise

uncanny-trust-suspend.short: Halt trust for agents with anomalous behavior
examples: flag decision entropy exceeding baseline
unpredictable output review

delegation-pipeline.short: Assign subtasks to peers tracking performance
examples: delegate with performance log
failure reduces future priority

shared-memoria.short: Collective experience store informing future interactions
examples: distributed ledger of history
query before new coalitions

betrayal-attrition.short: Reduce faction trust if betrayals cluster spatially or temporally
examples: Bayesian faction credibility
clustered violations lower trust

memory-cell-store.short: Store single piece in agent memory matrix
examples: STORE opcode writes to cell
memory cell indexed by coordinate

knowledge-retrieval.short: Fetch specific stored information from memory
examples: LOAD opcode reads from cell
exact or fuzzy retrieval

forget-pattern-eviction.short: Remove outdated info based on usage patterns
examples: EVICT opcode removes low-access cells
age-threshold eviction

temporal-decay-mode.short: Gradually reduce importance of information over time
examples: DECAY opcode reduces confidence
exponential half-life decay

snapshot-consolidation.short: Consolidate memory state into stable snapshot
examples: SNAP saves full register state
periodic checkpoint

memory-rehearsal-refresh.short: Refresh and strengthen memory traces on access
examples: re-access strengthens trace
reinforcement on recall
LINK opcode connects cells
causal temporal spatial links

working-memory-buffer.short: Temporarily hold active info for processing
examples: BUFFER allocates temp space
small fast working set

episodic-recall.short: Recall past experiences based on context
examples: recall episode matching current context
experience replay
overlay or append new data
consistency check before merge
semantic similarity detection
exact or fuzzy dedup

short-term-evaporation.short: Ephemeral info vanishes after set period
examples: EVAPORATE clears temp cells
time-based auto-delete
COMPRESS reduces cell size
lossy or lossless

distributed-replication.short: Replicate crucial info across memory nodes
examples: REPLICATE copies to peers
fault tolerance

proactive-forget.short: Forget info before irrelevant using prediction
examples: predict future relevance
pre-emptive eviction

transient-capsule.short: Capsule data accessible only within short window
examples: time-limited access capsule
auto-release after duration

semantic-memory-link.short: Connect related concepts in knowledge base
examples: semantic graph edges
concept clustering
validate then STORE
internal or external check
HIGHLIGHT marks urgent cells
soft or strong priority
MATCH_ACTIVATE scans cells
exact or wildcard

agent-crypto.description: Cryptographic patterns for agent identity and trust

identity-claim.short: Agent presents cryptographic proof of its identity to peers
examples: sign message with private key proving identity
public key verifies claim

reputation-token.short: Cryptographically signed attestation of agent track record
examples: signed history of completed tasks
tamper-proof reputation ledger

zero-knowledge-proof.short: Prove a statement is true without revealing underlying data
examples: prove capability without revealing implementation
verify age without revealing birthdate

key-rotation.short: Periodically replace cryptographic keys limiting exposure window
examples: rotate signing keys weekly
forward secrecy through key rotation

threshold-signature.short: Require minimum number of agents to authorize a collective action
examples: 3 of 5 agents must sign to approve
multi-sig governance
commit hash first then reveal answer
sealed bid auction protocol

merkle-proof.short: Efficient proof of data inclusion in larger structure
examples: prove transaction in block without full chain
verify repo file integrity

byzantine-resilience.short: Function correctly even with some agents behaving arbitrarily
examples: consensus with up to one-third faulty nodes
BFT practical byzantine tolerance

certificate-transparency.short: Publicly auditable log of all identity certifications
examples: anyone can verify agent certification history
detect unauthorized certificate issuance

deniable-auth.short: Prove identity to recipient but unable to prove to third party
examples: off-the-record messaging
prove authorization without revealing who authorized

membrane-antibody.short: Pre-learned pattern matchers that block specific threat signatures without deliberation — immune memory for agent security
examples: Membrane.add_antibody blocks known-bad patterns instantly
vaccination: pre-trained immune response to specific pathogen
firewall rules: known-bad IPs blocked without inspection

circadian-modulation.short: Instinct strengths vary sinusoidally with time — navigate instinct peaks at noon, rest instinct peaks at midnight
examples: CircadianRhythm.modulate(Navigate, hour=12) → 0.95
human: alert in morning, sleepy at night
solar panel: peak generation at noon, zero at night

epigenetic-weight.short: Gene expression modified by experience — same gene activated more strongly in agents that have used it successfully before
examples: EpigeneticMemory: successful gene gets higher activation weight
expert: same neurons fire faster from practice
tool preference: use the tool you've had success with

apoptosis-budget.short: Agent monitors its own fitness trend and triggers graceful shutdown when consistently declining — death with dignity
examples: ApoptosisProtocol: fitness declining for N cycles → shutdown
cell apoptosis: self-destruct when DNA damage detected
service: auto-terminate when error rate exceeds threshold

fleet-energy-pool.short: Centralized energy budget distributed by priority — critical agents get ATP first, experimental agents get leftovers
examples: FleetEnergy: distribute budget by priority and role
hospital: ICU patients get resources first, elective surgery waits
GPU: critical inference jobs get priority, training jobs queued

gene-fitness-decay.short: Gene fitness score decays over time without reinforcement — unused capabilities slowly become less reliable
examples: Gene.effective_fitness: base_fitness * recency_factor
muscle atrophy: unused muscles weaken
language: unused vocabulary fades from memory

instinct-priority-stack.short: When multiple instincts fire simultaneously, priority determines which gets energy — survival instincts override learning instincts
examples: Instinct::Navigate has higher priority than Instinct::Rest
maslow: physiological needs before self-actualization
interrupt priority: hardware interrupt preempts user thread

agent-lifecycle.description: Phases and transitions in agent existence

spawn.short: Agent instance created with minimal capabilities
examples: new agent booted from template
clone fleet member from repo

bootstrap.short: Initial capability loading during agent startup
examples: load core modules on first boot
equip agent with base skills

acclimation.short: Agent adjusts to environment parameters before full operation
examples: calibrate sensors to new environment
learn local norms before acting

activation.short: Agent transitions from passive to active operational state
examples: switch from monitoring to execution
receive first task assignment

maturation.short: Agent develops full capability through accumulated experience
examples: performance improves over time
capability breadth expands

specialization.short: Agent narrows focus becoming expert in specific domain
examples: generalist becomes domain expert
capable in fewer things better

senescence.short: Agent performance degrades due to accumulated state bloat
examples: memory bloat slows deliberation
stale knowledge reduces accuracy

hibernation.short: Agent enters low-power idle state preserving minimal capabilities
examples: suspend to disk when idle
wake on event trigger

revival.short: Agent resumes from hibernation restoring full state
examples: deserialize from checkpoint
resume interrupted task

fork.short: Agent creates child clone inheriting current state

merge.short: Two agent instances combine their state into one
