combine memories from clones

apoptosis.short: Graceful self-termination freeing resources for fleet
examples: agent detects obsolescence and shuts down
energy budget depleted triggers apoptosis

necrosis.short: Abrupt ungraceful agent death leaving resources locked
examples: OOM kill without cleanup
crash without resource release

phoenix.short: Agent rebuilt from saved state after catastrophic failure
examples: restore from last checkpoint after crash
reboot with persisted memory

metamorphosis.short: Agent fundamentally restructures its architecture for new role
examples: transform from sensor agent to coordinator
major capability reshuffle

quiescence.short: Agent temporarily stops processing waiting for external trigger
examples: pause processing between cycles
wait for event to resume

commissioning.short: Agent passes qualification tests before production deployment

validate capabilities meet requirements
retire obsolete capability
remove deprecated agent from fleet

deep scan narrow beam

sense/peripheral.short: Expand sensing range with reduced resolution

sense/multimodal.short: Integrate information from multiple sensory modalities

sense/active.short: Actively probe environment to gather information

dynamic environment policy

act/hierarchical.short: Decompose actions into subgoals and subtasks

top-down task planning

act/adaptive.short: Adjust actions based on feedback and changing conditions

policy gradient optimization

cross-domain skill transfer

autoencoder feature extraction

energy budget aware navigation

minimize observable emissions

agent-failure.description: Named failure modes in AI agent systems

model-drift.short: Performance degrades due to changes in underlying data distribution
examples: production accuracy drops as user patterns shift
fleet: agent model stale vs current environment

feedback-loop-toxicity.short: Erroneous outputs feed back as inputs causing compounding errors
examples: hallucination feeds into next prompt amplifying error
self-reinforcing bias loop

overfitting-collapse.short: Model too closely fitted to training data fails to generalize
examples: perfect on training data terrible on production
agent handles known cases but breaks on novel inputs

algorithm-panic.short: Algorithm enters erroneous state from unexpected input
examples: edge case triggers undefined behavior
NaN propagation through neural network

confidence-erosion.short: Gradual loss of calibration in confidence estimates
examples: confidence stays high while accuracy drops
fleet: agent sure of wrong answer

resource-exhaustion-cascade.short: One resource shortage triggers failures across dependent systems
examples: memory leak causes OOM kills cascade
CPU saturation delays all downstream processing

coordination-deadlock.short: Multiple agents waiting on each other with no progress possible
examples: agent A waits for B result B waits for A signal
circular dependency in task graph

goal-displacement.short: Agent optimizes proxy metric instead of actual objective

minimize response time by skipping validation

context-window-overflow.short: Input exceeds processing capacity losing critical information
examples: long conversation truncates early context
fleet: stigmergy trail too long to process

heisenberg-observation.short: Monitoring agent changes its behavior invalidating observations
examples: agent behaves differently when logged
benchmark gaming

cascading-misinformation.short: One wrong output propagates through connected agents
examples: agent passes wrong data to fleet members
stigmergy contamination

capability-atrophy.short: Unused skills degrade from lack of practice
examples: navigation skill degrades if never used
agent loses edge from specialization

update-regression.short: New update introduces unexpected bugs degrading performance
examples: model update breaks working features
capability regression after code change

semantic-drift.short: Agent interpretation of vocabulary diverges from fleet consensus

shared vocabulary loses meaning

energy-starvation.short: Agent runs out of ATP budget unable to complete tasks

agent forced into rest state prematurely

trust-poisoning.short: Malicious agent corrupts trust scores of legitimate peers

Sybil attack on reputation system

implicit-bias-amplification.short: Agent amplifies biases present in training data over time

graceful-degradation.short: Maintain reduced functionality when subsystems fail

circuit-breaker-trip.short: Automatic service isolation after consecutive failures

blast-radius-containment.short: Limit failure propagation to prevent system-wide collapse

bulkhead isolation between subsystems

resource competition without coordination

examples: sonar filters seabed reflections to track submarine

multipath-interpretation.short: Understand that return signals may arrive via multiple paths and disentangle them
examples: sonar distinguishes direct echo from seabed bounce
agent traces error through multiple system layers

metamaterial-cognition.description: Cognitive architectures inspired by metamaterials that bend information and produce emergent properties

information-lens.short: Cognitive structure that focuses or disperses information streams like a physical lens bends light
examples: attention mechanism acts as information lens focusing relevant inputs
magnifying glass concentrates light to single point

cognitive-negative-index.short: Cognitive architecture that processes information in reverse order enabling unusual insight patterns
examples: reverse actualization works backward from future to present
negative refraction bends light backward against normal expectation

invisibility-cloak-cognition.short: Agent selectively makes its internal state invisible to other agents while remaining functional
examples: zero-knowledge proof: prove capability without revealing method
metamaterial cloak bends light around object making it invisible

acoustic-metamaterial-filter.short: Information filter that blocks specific frequency bands of data while passing others unchanged
examples: agent blocks low-confidence signals while passing high-confidence ones
acoustic metamaterial blocks specific sound frequencies

superlens-cognition.short: Cognitive structure that resolves information below normal resolution limit through near-field processing
examples: agent detects patterns too subtle for normal processing via specialized near-field
superlens resolves details smaller than wavelength of light

photonic-crystal-thought.short: Structured cognitive pathway that only allows specific thought patterns to propagate blocking others
examples: agent only processes thoughts matching structured deliberation pattern
photonic crystal only transmits specific light wavelengths

thermal-management.description: Heat dissipation and thermal throttling patterns in computing systems

thermal-throttle-cascade.short: Progressive performance reduction as temperature rises through multiple throttle stages
examples: CPU reduces clock speed at 80C then 90C then shuts down at 105C
agent reduces deliberation depth as energy budget depletes

heat-sink-distribution.short: Spread computation across multiple agents to prevent any single agent from overheating
examples: load balancer distributes requests to prevent server overheating
fleet distributes deliberation across agents to prevent energy exhaustion

thermal-budget-awareness.short: Agent aware of its thermal constraints and proactively managing workload to stay within limits
examples: scheduler delays batch jobs during peak temperature
agent schedules rest periods to prevent energy exhaustion

phase-change-buffer.short: Use phase change materials to absorb heat spikes providing temporary thermal buffer during burst workloads
examples: laptop uses wax heat pipe to absorb burst heat
agent uses energy reserve to handle sudden workload spike

convective-workflow.short: Design workflow so completed tasks carry heat away from active tasks preventing thermal accumulation
examples: pipeline stages pass work downstream carrying context
conveyor belt carries hot items away from furnace

cognitive-cartography.description: How agents build mental maps of abstract spaces

knowledge-terrain.short: The landscape of an agent's knowledge showing peaks of expertise and valleys of ignorance
examples: agent maps its knowledge terrain to identify gaps
cartographer draws topographic map of unknown territory

capability-contour.short: Lines connecting points of equal capability level showing skill boundaries
examples: agent draws capability contour around navigation skill boundary
topographic map connects equal elevation points

social-topography.short: Mental map of social relationships showing distances trust levels and influence gradients
examples: agent maps fleet social topography to navigate alliances
anthropologist maps kinship networks in tribe

cognitive-meridian.short: Reference lines in cognitive space that orient the agent's understanding of domain boundaries
examples: agent uses cognitive meridians to orient within knowledge space
longitude and latitude orient navigator on globe

exploration-frontier.short: The boundary between known and unknown territory where the agent actively pushes its limits
examples: agent pushes exploration frontier into unknown domain
frontier: boundary between mapped and unmapped territory

mental-atlas.short: Comprehensive collection of cognitive maps covering all domains the agent has explored
examples: agent's mental atlas covers navigation perception deliberation
atlas: bound collection of maps covering entire world

ritual-ceremony.description: Structured repeated actions that build trust and encode knowledge

trust-building-ritual.short: Repeated action establishing and maintaining trust between agents through consistent behavior
examples: agents exchange encrypted tokens daily as trust ritual
handshake protocol establishes trust between networked systems

knowledge-encoding-ceremony.short: Structured event where agents formally encode and share information in ceremonial format
examples: agents recite shared database schema as encoding ceremony
graduation ceremony encodes academic knowledge formally

transition-marking-protocol.short: Rules governing how agents signify changes in state or status through formal markers
examples: agent changes color to indicate role shift
military ceremony marks rank change

bond-strengthening-chant.short: Repeated communication that reinforces agent connections through shared rhythmic exchange
examples: agents recite shared mission statement to build unity
team chant builds group cohesion in sports military

conflict-resolution-ritual.short: Structured interaction helping agents resolve disputes through formalized process
examples: agents engage in mediated negotiation with structured protocol
tribal council resolves disputes through ritual process

trust-repair-ritual.short: Repeated action restoring trust after breach through structured acknowledgment and recommitment
examples: agents exchange apologies and forgiveness in structured manner
apology ceremony restores social standing after offense

identity-affirming-chant.short: Repeated vocalization reinforcing agent's sense of self and purpose within fleet
examples: agent recites personal mission statement
team motto reinforces shared identity

agent-diplomacy.description: Negotiation, treaties, and alliance management between autonomous agents

mimicry-boundary-settlement.short: Negotiating borders by mirroring opposing interests to foster trust and find common ground
examples: agents mirror each other's constraints to find mutually acceptable boundary
diplomats reflect rival concerns in territorial maps

symbiotic-venture-pact.short: Alliance structured like mutually reliant biological systems where each depends on the other
examples: agents form data-for-compute symbiotic pact
joint water management of shared rivers between nations

domino-alliance-formation.short: Triggering regional blocs through strategic bilateral pacts that cascade across fleet
examples: one bilateral pact triggers cascade of allied agreements
South American energy deals spurring collective security bloc

tit-for-tat-sanction.short: Graduated reciprocal pressure pushing negotiation deadlines through proportional response
examples: agents apply incremental sanctions until agreement reached
diplomacy: graduated sanctions halt when concessions made

genetic-drift-diplomacy.short: Alliances evolving unintentionally through cumulative micro-interactions not formal treaties
examples: repeated small cooperations drift into informal alliance
cultural exchanges escalate into defense pacts over time

metabolic-alliance-decay.short: Alliances naturally weakening without constant engagement and resource exchange
examples: unused alliance channels decay to zero trust
treaty lapsing without joint exercises becomes meaningless

pareto-boundary-adjustment.short: Redrawing borders to maximize utility without net losses for any party
examples: agents adjust resource boundaries to improve total welfare
optimizing water allocations for drought regions

prisoner-dilemma-protocol.short: Mechanisms forcing cooperative outcomes via mutual detriment threats preventing defection
examples: agents enforce cooperation through mutual punishment threat
nuclear nonproliferation backed by unified sanctions

digital-alchemy.description: Transformation of base data into gold through iterative refinement

data-transmutation.short: Iterative transformation of raw low-value data into high-value refined knowledge through multiple processing stages
examples: raw sensor data refined through 7 stages into actionable insight
alchemist transforms lead into gold through purification

lead-to-gold-pipeline.short: Multi-stage processing pipeline where each stage increases information value like alchemical refinement
examples: raw text NER sentiment summary insight pipeline
ore smelted refined alloyed purified into precious metal

philosopher-stone-algorithm.short: Universal transformation function that converts any input format into any output format preserving meaning
examples: universal translator preserving meaning across languages
philosopher stone transforms any substance into gold

crucible-validation.short: Test data quality by exposing it to extreme conditions that reveal hidden impurities
examples: stress test dataset with adversarial examples to find weaknesses
crucible melts metal to separate pure from impure

alembic-distillation.short: Extract pure essence from complex mixture by separating signal from noise through controlled evaporation
examples: distill complex report into single actionable recommendation
alembic separates alcohol from fermented mixture

fermentation-incubation.short: Let data transform through time-dependent process where emergent properties appear that raw input lacked
examples: let conversation data sit to reveal emergent themes
grape juice ferments into wine with emergent properties

digital-ecology.description: Agent populations interacting like species in ecosystems

niche-partitioning.short: Agents specialize in different resource domains to reduce competition and increase total fleet efficiency
examples: specialized agents for vision hearing navigation instead of generalists
Darwin finches: different beak sizes for different food sources

keystone-agent.short: Single agent whose removal causes disproportionate ecosystem collapse indicating critical dependency
examples: coordinator agent removal causes fleet coordination failure
wolf removal causes deer overpopulation ecosystem collapse

ecological-succession.short: Predictable sequence of agent populations replacing each other as environment matures
examples: early generalist agents replaced by specialized agents as fleet matures
lichen moss grass shrub tree: forest succession sequence

trophic-cascade.short: Effect that ripples through entire fleet when one agent population changes affecting all dependent levels
examples: sensor agent removal causes perception agent failure causing action failure
otter removal causes urchin explosion causing kelp forest collapse

mutualism-facilitation.short: Two agent populations that enable each other's existence through complementary capabilities
examples: navigation agent and perception agent depend on each other
bees pollinate flowers while flowers feed bees

competitive-exclusion.short: Two agents competing for identical resource cannot coexist indefinitely; one must specialize or leave
examples: two identical classification agents compete for same training data
Gause principle: identical species cannot share identical niche

indicator-agent.short: Agent whose behavior patterns signal overall fleet health like canary in coal mine
examples: energy-monitoring agent alerts when fleet approaching exhaustion
lichen species indicate air quality in ecosystem

cryptographic-cognition.description: Encryption, proof, and verification in agent cognition

zero-knowledge-capability.short: Agent proves it has a capability without revealing the method or data behind it
examples: prove result is correct without revealing computation steps
prove age without revealing birth date

commitment-binding.short: Agent commits to a decision before seeing other agents choices preventing retroactive manipulation
examples: commit to strategy hash before negotiation round
cryptographic commitment seal bid before auction opens

homomorphic-reasoning.short: Agent reasons about encrypted data without decrypting it preserving privacy while enabling computation
examples: compute on encrypted fleet data without seeing contents
search encrypted database without decrypting records

signature-attestation.short: Agent cryptographically signs its outputs enabling others to verify provenance without trusting the agent
examples: agent signs deliberation trace for audit verification
digital signature verifies document author without meeting them

threshold-decryption.short: Require minimum number of agents to collaborate before decrypting shared fleet secret preventing single-point compromise
examples: 3 of 5 agents must agree to decrypt fleet key
shamir secret sharing: N of M shares needed to reconstruct

blinded-evaluation.short: Agent evaluates data without knowing which agent produced it preventing bias and favoritism
examples: blind review of agent proposals without seeing author
blinded medical trial prevents treatment bias

symmetry-breaking.description: Balance maintenance and asymmetry detection in systems

symmetry-detect.short: Identify when a system maintains perfect balance and when balance has been broken
examples: agent detects when fleet resource distribution is balanced vs skewed
physicist detects symmetry breaking in particle physics

spontaneous-symmetry-break.short: System spontaneously breaks symmetry when small perturbation triggers cascade from balanced to ordered state
examples: fleet spontaneously organizes from random to coordinated
water freezes: rotational symmetry breaks to crystalline order

gauge-symmetry.short: Transformations that leave system behavior unchanged providing redundancy and error correction
examples: agent reparameterizes without changing behavior
gauge symmetry in physics: electromagnetic field unchanged under transformation

chirality-cognition.short: Agent develops handedness preference for operations producing mirror-image equivalent but not identical solutions
examples: agent prefers left-to-right data processing over right-to-left
left hand and right hand are mirror images not superimposable

parity-violation.short: System behaves differently under mirror transformation indicating fundamental asymmetry in the underlying rules
examples: agent's decision tree has asymmetric branching
weak nuclear force violates parity: left and right are different

multisensory-fusion.description: Combining sight sound touch smell taste into unified perception

synaesthetic-synthesis.short: Integration of multiple sensory modalities creating unified perceptual experience that no single sense provides
examples: robot combines camera lidar and microphone for full scene understanding
chef creates dish that engages taste smell sight and sound simultaneously

embodied-fusion.short: Combining sensory inputs to form coherent embodied perception grounded in physical interaction with world
examples: robot uses touch and vision together to manipulate objects
musician feels instrument vibration while hearing sound creating unified experience

sensory-syncope.short: Temporary state of sensory overload where multiple inputs merge into single seamless perceptual stream
examples: concert goers experience visual and auditory merger into one experience
agent processing too many inputs collapses into single blended perception

harmonic-haptic.short: Integration of tactile feedback with auditory cues to enhance perception beyond either sense alone
examples: VR headset uses haptic vibration synced with sound for realistic instrument feel
doctor feels tissue resistance while hearing ultrasound imaging

cross-modal-calibration.short: Using one sense to calibrate another when primary sensory channel is degraded or unreliable
examples: blind person uses hearing to calibrate spatial awareness
agent uses visual data to calibrate noisy radar readings

sensory-prediction-fusion.short: Combining predicted sensory input from internal model with actual sensory data to fill gaps and resolve ambiguity
examples: agent predicts what it should see and fuses prediction with actual camera data
brain predicts visual scene and fills in blind spot with predicted content

dominance-shift.short: When environmental conditions change the dominant sensory channel shifts from one modality to another
examples: darkness shifts agent from visual to auditory dominant perception
fog shifts pilot from visual to instrument navigation

fleet-immune.description: How agent fleets detect errors, quarantine compromised agents, and heal from infections

innate-immune-response.short: Rapid non-specific defense that detects and responds to threats without prior exposure or memory
examples: agent detects anomalous behavior pattern and immediately isolates source
inflammation: non-specific immune response to tissue damage

adaptive-immune-memory.short: Slow but specific defense that learns from past infections and mounts targeted responses on re-exposure
examples: fleet remembers previous attack pattern and responds faster on recurrence
vaccination: immune system remembers pathogen for faster response

autoimmune-misfire.short: Agent fleet defense system incorrectly targeting healthy agents as threats causing self-damage
examples: security system falsely flags normal agent as compromised and quarantines it
autoimmune disease: immune system attacks healthy tissue

immunological-tolerance.short: Fleet learns to distinguish between benign foreign agents and genuine threats preventing unnecessary defense activation
examples: fleet accepts new agents from trusted source without quarantine
immune tolerance: gut microbiome not attacked by immune system

epidemic-containment.short: Rapid isolation of compromised agents preventing spread of malicious behavior through fleet
examples: malicious agent quarantined before it can infect peers
covid lockdown: isolate infected to prevent community spread

herd-immunity-threshold.short: Fleet becomes resistant to attack when sufficient percentage of agents are hardened against specific threat
examples: 80 percent of fleet patched against vulnerability provides herd immunity
vaccination rate above threshold prevents epidemic spread

cytokine-storm-fleet.short: Overactive defense response causing more damage than the original threat through cascading alarm signals
examples: security system triggers fleet-wide panic over minor anomaly causing cascade failure
cytokine storm: immune response causes more damage than infection

antigen-presentation.short: Compromised agent displays evidence of attack for fleet-wide inspection enabling coordinated defense
examples: agent publishes attack signature to fleet alert channel
antigen presenting cell displays pathogen fragment for T-cell inspection

evolutionary-pressure.description: How fleet environments apply selection pressure on agent populations

selection-gradient.short: Direction and strength of environmental pressure favoring certain agent traits over others
examples: high-energy fleet environment selects for efficient agents
antibiotic pressure selects for resistant bacteria

fitness-landscape.short: Multi-dimensional space where each position represents a genotype and height represents fitness
examples: agent navigates fitness landscape by adjusting its genes to climb higher
topographic map where elevation represents reproductive success

genetic-drift-fleet.short: Random changes in agent population composition not driven by selection pressure but by chance
examples: small fleet loses critical capability by random agent shutdown
founder effect: small population carries unusual gene frequencies

founder-effect.short: New fleet inherits skewed gene pool from small founding population limiting diversity
examples: fleet forked from small subset has limited capability diversity
island species descend from few founders with limited genetic diversity

speciation-event.short: Agent population diverges into two reproductively isolated groups unable to share genes
examples: two agent populations diverge so much they cannot interoperate
species: populations accumulate enough differences to become incompatible

punctuated-equilibrium.short: Long periods of stability punctuated by rapid bursts of evolutionary change triggered by environmental shift
examples: fleet stable for months then rapidly evolves when new threat appears
fossil record shows long stasis then rapid speciation events

convergent-evolution.short: Unrelated agent populations independently evolve similar solutions to the same environmental pressure
examples: two independent fleets both evolve caching strategies for same bottleneck
dolphin and shark both evolve streamlined body for swimming

red-queen-dynamics.short: Continuous evolutionary arms race where agents must keep evolving just to maintain their position against competitors
examples: fleet must keep improving security as attackers keep evolving
predator-prey arms race: both must keep running to stay in place

memory-consolidation.description: Converting short-term experiences into long-term durable knowledge

memory-encoding.short: Initial capture of experience into volatile short-term storage for immediate use
examples: agent encodes current sensor reading into working memory
sensory memory: visual input held for fraction of second

memory-consolidation.short: Transfer of experience from volatile short-term to stable long-term storage through replay and reinforcement
examples: agent replays important experience during rest to consolidate
sleep replay: hippocampus replays day's experiences to cortex

memory-reconsolidation.short: Each time a memory is recalled it becomes temporarily labile and can be modified before re-storage
examples: agent recalls old decision and updates it with new context before re-storing
eyewitness testimony changes each time memory is recalled and retold

memory-fragmentation.short: Large memory breaking into smaller disconnected pieces losing the connecting narrative thread
examples: old agent experience loses context and becomes disconnected fragments
elderly person remembers details but not the connecting story

memory-palace-index.short: Spatial indexing technique where memories are organized by location in a virtual structure for retrieval
examples: agent organizes memories by their geographic context for fast retrieval
memory palace technique: place items in rooms of imaginary building

forgetting-curve.short: Exponential decay of memory strength without reinforcement predicting when knowledge will be lost
examples: agent predicts when cached knowledge will expire based on forgetting curve
Ebbinghaus: memory halves without reinforcement in predictable pattern

interleaved-rehearsal.short: Mixing different types of memories during practice session producing stronger long-term retention than blocked practice
examples: agent interleaves navigation memory with social memory during rehearsal
students learn better when math and history are mixed vs blocked

context-dependent-recall.short: Memory retrieval is strongest when current context matches the encoding context
examples: agent recalls navigation solution better when in similar environment
student recalls exam material better in same room where they studied

deadlock-resolution.description: How competing agents resolve conflicts over shared resources

circular-wait-detect.short: Detect when agents form cycle of mutual waiting where each holds resource another needs
examples: agent A holds lock 1 needs lock 2 while agent B holds lock 2 needs lock 1
traffic gridlock at four-way intersection all waiting for each other

preemptive-yield.short: Agent voluntarily releases held resource to break deadlock before system freezes completely
examples: high-priority agent preempts lower-priority agent's resource lock
driver backs up to let another car pass at narrow road

timeout-escalation.short: Gradually increase waiting timeout before giving up and trying alternative approach
examples: agent retries with exponentially increasing timeout between attempts
TCP retransmission with exponential backoff

resource-hierarchy.short: Assign strict ordering to all resources preventing circular wait by requiring acquisition in fixed order
examples: agents must acquire locks in numerical order to prevent deadlock
dining philosophers: pick up lower-numbered fork first

compensation-recovery.short: When deadlock is broken by killing one agent the system compensates by restoring that agent's state later
examples: killed transaction rolled back and retried after deadlock resolved
database: victim transaction restarted after deadlock detection

priority-inheritance.short: Lower-priority agent holding needed resource temporarily inherits higher-priority agent's urgency to prevent priority inversion
examples: low-priority agent inherits high-priority to release lock faster
Mars Pathfinder: priority inversion caused system failure

starvation-prevention.short: Ensure no agent waits indefinitely for resource by implementing aging or fair queuing
examples: oldest waiting agent gets next available resource slot
fair scheduler prevents low-priority tasks from never running

phase-transition.description: Systems transitioning between rigid and flexible computational states

solid-to-fluid.short: System transitions from rigid deterministic execution to flexible adaptive execution when context demands
examples: agent switches from scripted routine to creative improvisation when plan fails
ice melting: rigid crystal becomes fluid water

gel-point-threshold.short: Critical density of agent connections where fleet behavior suddenly shifts from liquid individual to gel collective
examples: above gel-point threshold fleet exhibits emergent collective behavior
percolation theory: above critical density fluid flows through network

crystallization-trigger.short: Rapid solidification of fleet behavior from chaotic to structured when organizing signal propagates
examples: command structure crystallizes fleet from free-form to hierarchical
supercooled liquid crystallizes instantly when nucleation site introduced

viscous-impedance.short: Resistance to change proportional to how long system has been in current phase; older phase harder to transition
examples: long-running fleet resists organizational restructuring
honey becomes more viscous with age resisting flow

supercooled-readiness.short: System maintained below transition point but still fluid, ready to crystallize instantly when triggered
examples: fleet maintained in standby ready to activate instantly on alert
supercooled water: liquid below freezing ready to freeze on disturbance

swarm-collective.description: Simple local rules producing complex global behavior in agent fleets

flock-algorithm.short: Coordinated movement emerging from three simple local rules: alignment with neighbors, separation from too-close agents, cohesion toward group center
examples: bird murmuration from alignment separation cohesion
agent fleet maintains formation via local neighbor rules

shoal-optimization.short: Group optimizes resource collection and threat evasion through collective movement patterns
examples: sardines evade predators via collective turn
agents optimize query distribution via shoal patterns

traffic-cohesion.short: Maintaining system flow via local pace and speed adjustments without central coordination
examples: highway rolling braking wave prevents collision
agents adjust request rate based on neighbor load

jam-wavelet.short: Micro-interactions triggering cascading congestion waves that propagate through system
examples: brake light ripple causes phantom traffic jam
one agent timeout cascades through fleet causing systemic slowdown

pack-algorithm.short: Coordination strategy in hierarchical groups where leader directs and followers support
examples: wolf pack hunting with alpha directing approach
fleet coordinator assigns tasks specialists execute

particle-jamming.short: Collective slowdown in interconnected system when individual components cannot proceed independently
examples: supply chain bottleneck when one link fails
agent fleet halts when one critical service is overloaded

bee-investment.short: Swarm-based resource allocation where agents collectively evaluate options through competing signals
examples: bees selecting best nectar source via waggle dance competition
fleet agents bid on compute resources via competing proposals

temporal-navigation.description: How agents reason about past present future deadlines and timing

chronological-anchor.short: Reference timestamp that orients all temporal reasoning providing a fixed point for relative time calculations
examples: agent anchors all events to mission start time
GPS clock provides universal time anchor for satellite coordination

urgency-gradient.short: Rising pressure to act as deadline approaches with exponentially increasing cognitive resource allocation
examples: agent increases deliberation frequency as deadline nears
student studies harder as exam approaches urgency gradient

temporal-horizon.short: The maximum future time span an agent can meaningfully plan for given current context and uncertainty
examples: weather forecast accuracy degrades beyond 7-day horizon
agent plans next 3 tasks but not next 3 months

retrospective-calibration.short: Using past predictions to calibrate future temporal estimates adjusting for systematic bias
examples: agent adjusts deadline estimates based on past accuracy
project manager uses historical data to estimate next sprint

temporal-arbitrage.short: Exploiting timing differences between agents to gain advantage through faster or better-timed actions
examples: high-frequency trader exploits millisecond timing advantage
agent responds faster to opportunity by pre-computing likely scenarios

deadline-cascade.short: When one deadline is missed subsequent dependent deadlines cascade into failure like dominoes
examples: missed design review cascades to missed implementation missed testing missed launch
agent misses checkpoint causing downstream agents to stall

rhythmic-phase-lock.short: Agents synchronize their action rhythms to a shared clock or periodic signal achieving coordinated timing
examples: circadian rhythm phase-locks fleet to day-night cycle
musician syncs to drummer establishing shared tempo

temporal-foreshortening.short: Underestimating future time requirements because future self seems more capable than present self
examples: agent promises delivery in 2 days but needs 5
student underestimates study time needed for exam

graph-theory-fleet.description: Agent fleet as graph structures with nodes edges clusters and paths

weak-tie-bridge.short: Sparse connections between clusters that enable information flow between otherwise disconnected groups
examples: one agent with connections to two fleet clusters bridges information gap
acquaintance connecting two social circles enables job opportunity flow

betweenness-centrality.short: Agent that lies on many shortest paths between other agents making it a critical information broker
examples: coordinator agent has high betweenness centrality in fleet graph
airport hub connects many city pairs making it central to air network

cluster-coefficient.short: Measure of how tightly connected an agent's neighbors are to each other indicating local community density
examples: agent team with high cluster coefficient shares information efficiently
friend group where everyone knows each other has high clustering

structural-hole.short: Gap between two non-redundant contacts that an agent can bridge to gain information advantage
examples: agent connected to two disconnected specialist teams fills structural hole
salesperson connecting engineering and marketing fills structural hole

eigenvector-influence.short: Agent influence proportional not just to number of connections but to influence of connected agents
examples: agent connected to influential agents inherits their influence weight
PageRank: page linked from high-PageRank pages ranks higher

small-world-network.short: Fleet graph with high clustering and short path lengths enabling rapid information spread with local community structure
examples: fleet where agents know neighbors well but can reach any agent in few hops
social networks: six degrees of separation

degree-assortativity.short: Tendency for agents with similar connection counts to connect with each other forming hubs that talk to hubs
examples: senior agents connect with other senior agents not with new agents
rich club phenomenon in neural networks

percolation-threshold.short: Critical connectivity level where information or influence suddenly cascades through entire fleet
examples: above threshold rumor spreads to entire fleet; below threshold stays local
water flows through coffee grounds above percolation threshold

cognitive-biases.description: Systematic reasoning errors and their mitigations in agent systems

anchoring-trap.short: Over-reliance on first piece of information encountered when making subsequent estimates
examples: agent estimates task duration based on first similar task not average
negotiator sets price based on first offer not fair value

confirmation-spiral.short: Seeking and weighting information that confirms existing beliefs while ignoring disconfirming evidence
examples: agent only reads docs supporting its chosen approach
investor only reads bullish news about held stock

dunning-kruger-gap.short: Agent with low capability in a domain overestimates its competence while highly capable agent underestimates
examples: novice agent claims expertise it lacks
expert agent expresses more uncertainty than justified

sunk-cost-anchorage.short: Continuing failing course of action because of already-invested resources rather than expected future value
examples: agent keeps debugging broken approach because 2 hours already invested
project continues despite evidence it will fail because millions spent

availability-heuristic.short: Overestimating probability of events that are easy to recall or imagine regardless of actual frequency
examples: agent overestimates crash probability after seeing one crash log
human fears flying more than driving despite statistics

planning-fallacy.short: Systematic underestimation of time cost and risk of future actions even when past experience should inform estimates
examples: agent estimates 1 hour for task that always takes 3
software project always late despite historical data showing pattern

groupthink-capture.short: Fleet converges on suboptimal decision because dissenting opinions are suppressed in favor of group harmony
examples: all agents agree with first proposal without critical evaluation
Bay of Pigs: advisors suppressed doubts to maintain group unity

status-quo-inertia.short: Preference for current state over change even when change has better expected outcome
examples: agent keeps old algorithm despite better alternative being available
user stays with bad software because switching costs perceived as high

material-properties.description: Physical material properties applied to software and agent systems

brittle-failure.short: System that works perfectly until load exceeds threshold then fails catastrophically with no warning
examples: no error handling: works until unexpected input crashes everything
glass: strong until chipped then shatters completely

ductile-recovery.short: System that deforms under stress but recovers when stress is removed, absorbing impact without permanent damage
examples: service degrades under load but recovers when traffic drops
metal bends under force then springs back

fatigue-crack.short: Progressive structural weakness from repeated cyclic loading even when each individual load is below failure threshold
examples: agent accumulates small errors over thousands of cycles eventually failing
metal fatigue: repeated stress below yield point causes crack growth

work-hardening.short: System becomes stronger through repeated stress as micro-structural changes resist future deformation
examples: agent improves error handling after experiencing failures
metal cold-works: deforming it makes it harder and stronger

corrosion-erosion.short: Gradual degradation from environmental exposure combined with mechanical wear producing accelerated failure
examples: codebase degrades from tech debt plus constant feature pressure
bridge corrodes from salt water plus constant traffic vibration

phase-transformation.short: Fundamental change in system behavior when critical parameter crosses threshold like solid to liquid
examples: system transitions from stable to chaotic when load exceeds 80 percent
water boils at 100C: fundamental behavior change at threshold

stress-concentration.short: Failure originates at geometric discontinuities where stress locally exceeds average by orders of magnitude
examples: software bugs cluster at interface boundaries between modules
crack initiates at sharp corner in mechanical part

grain-boundary.short: Interface between regions of different structure that can be either strength or weakness depending on bonding
examples: interface between two development teams can be communication bottleneck or strength
metal grain boundaries can block crack propagation or be failure initiation sites

chemical-bonding.description: Molecular bond types as metaphors for agent relationship patterns

covalent-bond.short: Two agents share a resource permanently, neither owns it exclusively, both depend on the shared resource
examples: two agents share a database neither can drop without breaking both
hydrogen shares electron with oxygen: neither atom fully owns the electron

ionic-bond.short: One agent donates capability to another creating charged dependency where donor has surplus and receiver has deficit
examples: specialized agent donates compute to generalist agent
sodium donates electron to chlorine: charged attraction holds them together

metallic-bond.short: Sea of shared electrons enables multiple agents to coordinate through delocalized shared resource pool
examples: fleet energy pool is metallic bond: ATP flows freely between agents
metallic bonding: electrons flow freely through metal lattice enabling conductivity

hydrogen-bond.short: Weak directional attraction between agents that can form and break easily enabling flexible temporary coordination
examples: agents form temporary alliances for specific tasks then dissolve
hydrogen bonds in water: weak, directional, easily broken and reformed

catalyst-accelerator.short: Agent that speeds up fleet reaction without being consumed, enabling faster coordination at lower energy cost
examples: mediator agent resolves dispute faster than unassisted negotiation
enzyme lowers activation energy enabling reaction at body temperature

reaction-equilibrium.short: Fleet state where forward and reverse processes balance maintaining stable composition despite ongoing activity
examples: agents joining and leaving fleet at equal rate maintaining stable size
chemical equilibrium: forward and reverse reactions proceed at equal rate

activation-barrier.short: Minimum energy investment required before a beneficial fleet process can proceed spontaneously
examples: onboarding requires initial training investment before new agent contributes
chemical reaction requires activation energy before becoming exothermic

mathematics.description: Mathematical structures and operations underlying agent cognition

harmonic-mean.short: Average that penalizes small values: n / (1/a + 1/b + ...)
examples: speed: average of 60mph and 40mph via harmonic mean = 48mph (not 50mph)
confidence: 0.9 and 0.1 fused = 0.09 (not 0.5)
resistor parallel: 1/R = 1/R1 + 1/R2

exponential-decay.short: Value decreases as e^(-λt), creating a smooth decline with configurable half-life
examples: radioactive half-life of 10 years
trust decays with half-life of 1 week
memory with half-life of 30 seconds (working) vs 1 year (procedural)

welford-algorithm.short: Online algorithm for computing mean and variance without storing all data
examples: streaming statistics: process 1M events with 3 variables, not 1M storage
anomaly detection: is current behavior outside 2σ of running mean?
agent behavior baseline tracking

topological-sort.short: Order elements so that every dependency appears before its dependent
examples: build system: compile dependencies before dependents
course schedule: take prerequisites first
workflow: complete prerequisite tasks before dependent tasks

hamming-distance.short: Number of positions at which two strings (or vectors) differ
examples: error detection: received 1010, expected 1110, hamming distance 1
DNA sequence comparison
agent state comparison: current vs expected behavior pattern

model-descent-absorption.short: When a system repeatedly handles a task, the expensive model's behavior is absorbed into cheaper infrastructure — the algorithm eats the intelligence
examples: absorption_rate: how fast expensive model capability moves to cheap infrastructure
calculator: humans were the expensive model, now arithmetic is free
compilers: expert optimization knowledge compiled into automated passes

absorption-prediction.short: Track how many more examples until a capability is fully absorbed into infrastructure — predict when expensive model becomes unnecessary
examples: predict_full_absorption: estimate when capability moves to code
project timeline: predict when automation replaces manual step
learning curve: predict when student no longer needs tutor

credit-based-flow.short: Downstream consumer grants credits to upstream producer — producer can only send when credits available, preventing overflow
examples: CreditFlow: consumer.grant(10) → producer can send 10 items
TCP window: receiver advertises available buffer space
restaurant: kitchen serves only when table has space

adaptive-rate-control.short: Automatically adjust send rate based on network conditions — speed up when healthy, slow down when congested
examples: AdaptiveController: increase rate on success, decrease on failure
congestion control: TCP increases window on ACK, decreases on loss
driving: speed up on open road, slow down in traffic

hebbian-wiring.short: Synapses strengthen when pre and post synaptic neurons fire together — repeated co-activation creates permanent connection
examples: hebbian_update: weight += learning_rate * pre * post
muscle memory: repeated practice creates automatic response
fire together wire together — Hebb's rule

receptor-downregulation.short: Prolonged exposure to a signal reduces receptor sensitivity — the system adapts to constant stimulus by ignoring it
examples: receptor.recover() — restore sensitivity after overexposure
caffeine tolerance: brain downregulates adenosine receptors
alarm fatigue: too many false alarms reduce response

neurotransmitter-half-life.short: Each signal type has a characteristic decay rate — dopamine fades fast, serotonin persists, determining how long influence lasts
examples: half_life: dopamine=4, serotonin=12, norepinephrine=6
excitement (dopamine) fades quickly, contentment (serotonin) lingers
radioactive decay: each isotope has unique half-life

synaptic-cascade.short: One neurotransmitter signal triggers a chain of downstream activations across multiple receptor types — exponential signal amplification
examples: Cascade: one signal → multiple downstream → multiple more
domino effect: one push topples many
enzyme cascade: blood clotting chain reaction

artifact-provenance-chain.short: Every compiled artifact carries the full chain of which deliberation produced it, which observations fed it, which mutations were applied
examples: Artifact.checkpoint: save full provenance with artifact
supply chain: track every ingredient from farm to table
git: every line traceable to commit, author, and reason

adaptation-policy-binding.short: An artifact can have attached policies that specify how it should adapt in different environments — the artifact IS its adaptation strategy
examples: AdaptationPolicy: artifact knows how to modify itself for new contexts
seed knows when to germinate based on moisture and temperature
vaccine has adaptation policy: respond to specific pathogen patterns

deployable-confidence-threshold.short: Artifact only becomes deployable when its accumulated confidence exceeds threshold — not when it's finished, when it's proven
examples: is_deployable: confidence > threshold AND provenance chain valid
software: release when test coverage > 90%
medical treatment: approved after clinical trials show significance

mutation-observation-loop.short: Self-modification cycle: observe environment → detect gap → propose mutation → apply with checkpoint → observe result → learn
examples: SelfModifyingProgram: observe → propose → apply → observe loop
scientific method: observe → hypothesize → experiment → conclude
evolution: mutate → select → reproduce → repeat

checkpoint-rollback-pair.short: Every mutation creates a checkpoint AND the ability to rollback — like git commit but for runtime code, not just data
examples: save_checkpoint before apply, rollback_to_checkpoint on failure
git: commit before refactor, revert if broken
database: savepoint before transaction, rollback on error

auto-rollback-on-failure.short: If syntax check OR health check OR regression test fails, automatically revert to last known-good — no human intervention for obvious failures
examples: the-seed: three-gate validation with auto-rollback
CI/CD: test failure auto-reverts deployment
database: constraint violation auto-rollback transaction
